topics.title: Introduction to Kinematics and Mechanisms - Various Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT403 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Kinematics and Mechanisms - Various Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Kinematics of Machinery?

**Kinematics of Machinery** (often called *Theory of Machines*) is the branch of engineering science that deals with the **relative motion** between the parts of a machine and the **forces** which produce this motion. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically divides this study into two complementary sub-disciplines:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (As per PCRAT403 Syllabus):**
> - **Kinematics:** The study of *relative motion* between the parts of a machine, without considering the forces which cause it. It deals with displacement, velocity, and acceleration of links.
> - **Kinetics:** The study of the *forces* which produce or change the motion of a machine. It is essentially the *dynamics* of the machine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a bicycle as a simple system:
- The **chain, pedals, sprockets, and wheels** all move in a very specific, geometrically constrained manner. If you only describe *how* the wheel rotates when the pedal turns (without asking *why* the chain forces it to turn), you are doing **Kinematics**.
- The moment you ask, *"How much force must my leg apply on the pedal to climb a hill?"* — you have crossed into **Kinetics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PCRAT403 focuses almost exclusively on **Kinematics** — the geometry of motion. Kinetics and force analysis are reserved for higher semesters (e.g., Dynamics of Machinery).

### 1.3 Fundamental Building Blocks of a Machine

Every machine, from a stapler to a steam engine, is built from a hierarchy of components. KTU expects you to know this hierarchy cold:

| Hierarchy Level | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Body / Member** | A single, rigid piece incapable of deformation | A steel rod, a gear blank |
| **Link / Kinematic Link** | A body (or combination of bodies) intended to be **relatively rigid** but permitting relative motion at joints | Crank, connecting rod |
| **Kinematic Pair** | A *joint* between two links that permits *constrained relative motion* | Revolute pin, slider in a slot |
| **Kinematic Chain** | An assembly of links connected by kinematic pairs, with **no useful motion** (or indeterminate motion) | A loose chain of paper clips |
| **Mechanism** | A *kinematic chain* in which **one link is fixed** and motion is **definite and predictable** | Four-bar linkage, slider-crank |
| **Machine** | A mechanism (or set of mechanisms) that **transmits or transforms force/energy** to do **useful work** | Sewing machine, internal combustion engine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism vs. Machine — The Golden Rule:**
> - A **Mechanism** transforms *motion* (e.g., rotary → reciprocating).
> - A **Machine** transforms *energy* and does *work* (e.g., a lifting jack).
> - Every machine is a mechanism, but not every mechanism is a machine (e.g., a watch is a mechanism, not a "machine" in the strictest textbook sense).

### 1.4 The Concept of a Kinematic Link

A **Kinematic Link** (KTU Module 1 keyword) is a body or a combination of bodies which is **relatively rigid** with respect to all other links of the machine. It is the *smallest independent unit of a mechanism*.

Links are broadly classified as:

1. **Rigid Link** — Does not deform under the action of applied forces. (Standard assumption in KTU problems.)
2. **Flexible Link** — Deforms under its own weight or applied force (e.g., belts, ropes, chains).
3. **Fluid Link** — Has no definite shape; transmits force/pressure through fluid (e.g., hydraulic fluid in a cylinder).

### 1.5 Types of Constrained Motion — The Foundation of Pair Classification

Two bodies in contact can have constrained relative motion. KTU 2024 Module 1 requires you to recognize three types of constrained motion:

- **Completely Constrained Motion:** The motion between two links is *fully and uniquely determined* by the joint geometry. *Example:* A square shaft in a square hole — it can only rotate, and that rotation is fixed.
- **Incompletely Constrained Motion:** The motion is not uniquely determined; the moving link can take multiple paths. *Example:* A circular shaft in a circular hole — it can rotate and slide simultaneously.
- **Successfully Constrained Motion:** An *incompletely* constrained motion is *made definite* by adding an extra link (like a slider in a fixed guide).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Kinematic Pairs (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

A **Kinematic Pair** is formed when two links (one may be the fixed frame) are in contact with each other and permit *relative motion*. KTU examiners frequently ask classification questions (3-mark standard).

#### 2.1.1 Based on the Nature of Contact

| Pair Type | Nature of Contact | Example | Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lower Pair** | *Surface* contact (large area) | Journal bearing, slider in guide | Higher load capacity, low wear |
| **Higher Pair** | *Point* or *Line* contact | Cam-follower, gear meshing | Lower load capacity, high wear |

#### 2.1.2 Based on the Type of Relative Motion

| Pair Type | Motion Permitted | Degrees of Freedom | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sliding Pair** | Pure translation (1 DOF) | 1 | Piston in cylinder |
| **Revolute (Turning) Pair** | Pure rotation (1 DOF) | 1 | Door hinge, crankshaft in bearing |
| **Rolling Pair** | Pure rolling (1 DOF) | 1 | Ball bearing (theoretical) |
| **Screw Pair** | Translation + Rotation, *coupled* (1 DOF) | 1 | Lead screw, nut-bolt |
| **Spherical Pair** | Rotation about any of 3 axes (3 DOF) | 3 | Ball-and-socket joint |
| **Cylindrical Pair** | Rotation + Translation along axis (2 DOF) | 2 | Footstep bearing |
| **Planar Pair** | Translation in plane + rotation about ⟂ axis (3 DOF) | 3 | Flat slider on a flat surface |
| **Universal (Hooke's) Pair** | Rotation about 2 perpendicular axes (2 DOF) | 2 | Universal joint in a car |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "The Pairs Hierarchy":**
> - DOF **1** → Sliding, Revolute, Rolling, Screw
> - DOF **2** → Cylindrical, Universal
> - DOF **3** → Spherical, Planar

#### 2.1.3 Based on the Type of Closure (Hold vs. Force)

| Pair Type | Method of Holding Contact | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Self / Form-Closed Pair** | Geometry of the links maintains contact | Cam-follower, meshing gears |
| **Force / Loose Pair** | External force (like gravity or spring) maintains contact | Two flat plates held by gravity |

### 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Heart of Mechanism Analysis

The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** or **Mobility** of a mechanism is the **number of independent parameters** (inputs) required to **completely specify the configuration** of the mechanism at any instant.

For a planar mechanism in 2D space, KTU 2024 mandates the use of the **Kutzbach Criterion** (also called Grübler's equation for planar mechanisms):

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $F$ = Degrees of freedom of the mechanism
- $n$ = Total number of links (including the fixed ground link)
- $j_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** (1 DOF joints)
- $j_2$ = Number of **higher pairs** (2 DOF joints)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "3" and "2" in the formula?**
> - Each unconstrained rigid body in a plane has **3 DOF** (translation in X, translation in Y, rotation in Z). Thus, $(n-1)$ moving links contribute $3(n-1)$.
> - Each **lower pair** (1-DOF joint like revolute/sliding) **removes 2** DOF (from 3 to 1). Hence the **$-2j_1$**.
> - Each **higher pair** (like cam/follower) **removes 1** DOF (from 3 to 2). Hence the **$-j_2$**.

### 2.3 Special Kutzbach Cases — KTU 2024 Sub-Formulas

For mechanisms composed of **only lower pairs** (the vast majority of standard problems):

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$$

For **spatial mechanisms**, the Kutzbach criterion is replaced by a more complex 6-DOF-based formula (Kutzbach-Manolescu), but KTU 2024 Module 1 typically restricts to planar.

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Planar Kutzbach Criterion | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ includes ground link |
| 2 | Lower-pair-only formula | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$ | Use when no cam/gear pairs |
| 3 | Useful mechanism condition | $F \geq 1$ | $F=1$ → determinate, $F>1$ → indeterminate (needs 2+ inputs) |
| 4 | Locked structure / Frame | $F = 0$ | Structure, not mechanism |
| 5 | Redundant DOF | $F < 0$ (with redundancy) | Has idle/passive DOFs |
| 6 | Lower pair minimum links | $\geq 2$ links per pair | One link is typically the ground |
| 7 | Grashof's Law (Four-bar) | $s + l \leq p + q$ | $s$=shortest, $l$=longest, $p,q$=other two; all in cm |
| 8 | Velocity in rigid link | $v = \omega \times r$ | Cross product: direction ⟂ to both |
| 9 | Centripetal accel. | $a_n = \omega^2 r$ | Always points **toward** the center |
| 10 | Tangential accel. | $a_t = \alpha r$ | Perpendicular to $a_n$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DOF concept is the **single most applied tool** in machine design and robotics:
- **Industrial Robots:** Each joint of a 6-DOF robotic arm (PUMA, ABB) must be carefully counted to ensure it can reach any point in 3D space.
- **Vehicle Suspension:** Engineers count DOF of a wheel assembly to ensure correct camber, caster, and steering behavior.
- **Biomechanics:** A human arm has 7 DOF, but a robot arm with the same DOF behaves entirely differently — DOF analysis is the first step.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Planar Kutzbach Criterion

We will rigorously derive the formula $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Identify the DOF of a free body in a plane.**

A rigid body (a link) in a 2D plane can perform three independent motions:
1. Translation along the X-axis,
2. Translation along the Y-axis,
3. Rotation about an axis perpendicular to the plane (Z-axis).

So, **a single free link in a plane has 3 DOF.**

**Step 2 — Add links and count total free DOF.**

If we have $n$ links in total, and **one of them is the fixed ground link** (which has 0 DOF by itself), then the number of *moving* links is $(n-1)$.

Total free DOF of all moving links, *if they were unconnected*, would be:
$$\text{Total Free DOF} = 3(n-1)$$

**Step 3 — Account for the constraints imposed by each joint.**

Every kinematic pair (joint) imposes *constraints* on the relative motion between two links.

- A **lower pair** (sliding, revolute, etc.) reduces the relative motion to **1 DOF**. Since 2 independent motions are lost, it imposes **2 constraints**.
- A **higher pair** (cam, gear) reduces the relative motion to **2 DOF**. So it imposes **1 constraint**.

If there are $j_1$ lower pairs and $j_2$ higher pairs, the **total number of constraints** is:
$$\text{Total Constraints} = 2j_1 + j_2$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the constraints from the free DOF.**

The remaining (usable) DOF of the mechanism is:
$$F = \text{Total Free DOF} - \text{Total Constraints}$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 2 and Step 3:

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

This is the **Kutzbach Criterion** for planar mechanisms. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Four-Bar Chain

**Problem:** A four-bar chain has 4 links and 4 revolute (lower) pairs. Calculate the DOF.

**Solution:**

By inspection:
- $n = 4$ (including the fixed ground link)
- $j_1 = 4$ (all four pin joints are lower pairs)
- $j_2 = 0$ (no cam/gear pairs)

Apply the Kutzbach formula:
$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
$$F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$$
$$F = 3(3) - 8$$
$$F = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Interpretation:** A standard four-bar chain has **1 DOF** — meaning you need to specify the angle of *one* input link, and the angles of the remaining two links are *uniquely determined*. This is why it is the most common mechanism in engineering.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Slider-Crank Mechanism

**Problem:** A slider-crank has 4 links, 3 revolute pairs, and 1 sliding pair. Calculate the DOF.

**Solution:**

- $n = 4$ (frame, crank, connecting rod, slider)
- $j_1 = 4$ (3 revolute + 1 sliding, all are lower pairs)
- $j_2 = 0$

Apply the formula:
$$F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$$
$$F = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Interpretation:** **1 DOF** — input is crank rotation; output is slider position. This forms the basis of all internal combustion engines and reciprocating pumps.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Mechanism with a Higher Pair

**Problem:** A cam-follower mechanism has 3 links (cam, follower, frame) and 1 cam pair (higher pair), 1 revolute pair (lower). Find the DOF.

**Solution:**

- $n = 3$
- $j_1 = 1$ (the revolute pin joining follower to frame)
- $j_2 = 1$ (the cam-follower contact)

Apply the formula:
$$F = 3(3-1) - 2(1) - 1$$
$$F = 3(2) - 2 - 1$$
$$F = 6 - 3 = 3$$

**Interpretation:** Wait — a 3-DOF cam-follower is **incorrect** in practice! This signals the presence of *redundant* or *idle* DOFs, or that the system is *indeterminate*. In a real cam-follower, the **spring force** or gravity is essential to maintain contact — a constraint we have not yet modeled in the rigid-body Kutzbach equation. KTU 2024 notes this as a known limitation.

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Whitworth Quick-Return (6-link)

**Problem:** A Whitworth quick-return mechanism has 6 links and 7 lower pairs. Calculate the DOF.

**Solution:**

- $n = 6$
- $j_1 = 7$
- $j_2 = 0$

Apply the formula:
$$F = 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0$$
$$F = 15 - 14 = 1$$

**Interpretation:** A 6-link Whitworth is a **1-DOF** mechanism. This is the minimum needed to produce a quick-return (fast return, slow cutting stroke) in shaping machines.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Pair Type

| Engineering Need | Best Pair Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Door hinge | Revolute (lower) | Cheap, robust, self-lubricating |
| Piston in engine | Sliding (lower) | Handles high thrust loads |
| Gear mesh | Higher (involute) | Variable transmission ratio possible |
| Ball bearing | Rolling (lower) | Low friction, high speed |
| Lead screw | Screw (lower) | Converts rotation → precise linear motion |
| Robotic wrist | Spherical (lower) | Allows 3 rotational DOF |
| Universal joint in a car | Hooke's (lower) | Transmits rotation across misaligned shafts |
| Cam-follower | Higher (point/line contact) | Custom motion profiles possible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When solving a DOF problem, always draw the kinematic diagram *first*, label every joint as **L (lower)** or **H (higher)**, and *then* substitute into the formula. This single habit prevents 80% of KTU valuation errors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Hierarchy of a Machine

The following block diagram illustrates the conceptual hierarchy of a machine as taught in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source] --> B[Transmission Subsystem]
    B --> C[Kinematic Subsystem]
    C --> D[Working Subsystem]
    D --> E[Useful Output]

    A1[Electric Motor / IC Engine / Human Power] -.labeled.-> A
    B1[Gears / Belts / Chains / Couplings] -.labeled.-> B
    C1[Links / Pairs / Chains / Mechanisms] -.labeled.-> C
    D1[Tool / Die / Cutter / Piston] -.labeled.-> D
    E1[Mechanical Work / Motion / Force] -.labeled.-> E

    style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Kinematic Link Hierarchy

The following schematic maps the structural hierarchy from a raw body to a complete machine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Rigid Body] --> L2[Kinematic Link]
    L2 --> L3[Kinematic Pair]
    L3 --> L4[Kinematic Chain]
    L4 --> L5[Mechanism]
    L5 --> L6[Machine]

    L2A[Combination of bodies relatively rigid] -.label.-> L2
    L3A[Joint permitting constrained motion] -.label.-> L3
    L4A[Assembly of links with no useful output] -.label.-> L4
    L5A[Chain with one link fixed, definite motion] -.label.-> L5
    L6A[Mechanism transforming force or doing work] -.label.-> L6

    style L1 fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L5 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L6 fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Kutzbach Criterion Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Identify the mechanism] --> S2[Count total links n including ground]
    S2 --> S3[Count lower pairs j1]
    S3 --> S4[Count higher pairs j2]
    S4 --> S5[Substitute into F = 3n-3 - 2j1 - j2]
    S5 --> S6{F greater than or equal to 1?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Valid mechanism with F inputs required]
    S6 -- No, F=0 --> S8[Structure or locked mechanism]
    S6 -- No, F less than 0 --> S9[Redundant constraints present]

    S7 --> S10[End: Mechanism is usable]
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10

    style S1 fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style S6 fill:#fffbe6,stroke:#d33,color:#000
    style S7 fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#393,color:#000
    style S8 fill:#f7d4d4,stroke:#d33,color:#000
    style S9 fill:#f7d4d4,stroke:#d33,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Pair Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Kinematic Pair] --> P1[Based on contact]
    P0 --> P2[Based on relative motion]
    P0 --> P3[Based on closure]

    P1 --> P1A[Lower Pair: Surface contact]
    P1 --> P1B[Higher Pair: Point or Line contact]

    P2 --> P2A[Sliding 1DOF]
    P2 --> P2B[Revolute 1DOF]
    P2 --> P2C[Rolling 1DOF]
    P2 --> P2D[Screw 1DOF]
    P2 --> P2E[Cylindrical 2DOF]
    P2 --> P2F[Universal 2DOF]
    P2 --> P2G[Spherical 3DOF]
    P2 --> P2H[Planar 3DOF]

    P3 --> P3A[Self closed: Geometry holds]
    P3 --> P3B[Force closed: External force holds]

    style P0 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#ffccff,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a machine and a mechanism with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Mechanism | Machine |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A kinematic chain with one link fixed that transforms *motion* | A mechanism that transmits/transforms *force* and does *useful work* |
| **Energy** | Concerned with *relative motion only* | Concerned with *force and energy transformation* |
| **Work Output** | Does not necessarily perform useful work | Always performs a *defined useful task* |
| **Example** | A wall clock, a slider-crank with no load | A sewing machine, an IC engine, a crane |

**Mnemonic:** *"Mechanism = Motion; Machine = Work."* (1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for concluding statement.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Degree of Freedom" (DOF) of a mechanism. State the Kutzbach equation for a planar mechanism with all lower pairs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The *Degree of Freedom* of a mechanism is the number of independent inputs (or coordinates) required to *uniquely define* the configuration of all its moving links at any instant.
- **Kutzbach Equation (1.5 marks):** For a planar mechanism with only lower pairs,

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$$

where $n$ is the total number of links (including the fixed ground) and $j_1$ is the number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to count the **ground (fixed) link** in $n$. KTU examiners *intentionally* mark such answers down by 2 marks.
> - **Most Common Mistake #2:** Misclassifying a *screw pair* as a higher pair — it is a **lower pair** (1 DOF).
> - **Most Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to draw a *kinematic diagram* before writing the Kutzbach equation. Always draw it first.
> - **Most Common Mistake #4:** Confusing *Grashof's Law* with the Kutzbach criterion — Grashof decides the *type* of four-bar motion; Kutzbach counts the *mobility*.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** A four-bar chain ABCD has links of lengths 30 cm, 60 cm, 50 cm, and 40 cm. Using **Grashof's Law**, classify the mechanism. (7 marks)

**(b)** A planar mechanism has 8 links, 10 lower pairs, and 2 higher pairs. Determine the **mobility** using the Kutzbach criterion. Comment on the result. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify the link lengths: 30 cm, 60 cm, 50 cm, 40 cm.
- Shortest link, $s = 30$ cm
- Longest link, $l = 60$ cm
- Other two links, $p = 50$ cm, $q = 40$ cm

**Step 2:** Apply Grashof's inequality $s + l \leq p + q$.

$$s + l = 30 + 60 = 90 \text{ cm}$$
$$p + q = 50 + 40 = 90 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3:** Since $s + l = p + q$, the chain satisfies Grashof's Law with **equality** — it is a *Class I (special) Grashof* mechanism.

**Step 4:** In Class I Grashof, *if the shortest link is the fixed (ground) link*, the mechanism is a **Double-Crank (Drag-Link)** mechanism. The opposite links to the shortest link act as cranks; the other two rocks.

**Final Answer:** The four-bar chain is a **Grashof Class I (Double-Crank) mechanism** since the shortest link is taken as the frame (assumption). If instead the shortest link is the coupler, the mechanism is a **Double-Rocker**. (7 marks: 1 for lengths, 2 for Grashof check, 2 for classification, 2 for type identification.)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:**
- $n = 8$ links
- $j_1 = 10$ lower pairs
- $j_2 = 2$ higher pairs

**Step 1:** Apply the full Kutzbach criterion.

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
$$F = 3(8-1) - 2(10) - 2$$
$$F = 3(7) - 20 - 2$$
$$F = 21 - 22 = -1$$

**Step 2:** Interpretation of $F = -1$:
- A negative mobility does **not** make physical sense for a *movable* mechanism.
- It indicates **redundant constraints** (passive/extra DOFs) in the system.
- Such a mechanism is either a *statically indeterminate structure* or contains *idle/redundant* links or pairs.

**Final Answer:** $F = -1$, indicating the mechanism has *redundant constraints* and is therefore **not a valid single-DOF mechanism**. To make it a valid mechanism, *one constraint must be removed* (e.g., one joint converted to a free connection or a link removed). (7 marks: 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 3 for interpretation.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches **any four** types of kinematic pairs. State the relative motion and degrees of freedom for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **slider-crank mechanism** is used in an internal combustion engine. The crank length is 100 mm and the connecting rod length is 400 mm. The crank rotates at a uniform speed of 2400 rpm. Find the **velocity and acceleration of the slider** when the crank is at 45° from the inner dead center. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

The student should explain **any four** of the following pairs with sketches. A complete answer requires: (i) neat diagram, (ii) motion permitted, (iii) DOF.

1. **Revolute (Turning) Pair** — Two links rotate about a fixed pin. DOF = 1. *Example: Hinge of a door.*
2. **Sliding Pair** — One link slides over the other. DOF = 1. *Example: Piston in a cylinder.*
3. **Screw Pair** — Rotation + translation, coupled helically. DOF = 1. *Example: Lead screw of a lathe.*
4. **Spherical Pair** — One link (ball) rotates in any direction within the other (socket). DOF = 3. *Example: Ball-and-socket joint of a vehicle suspension.*
5. **Cylindrical Pair** — Rotation + translation, *uncoupled*. DOF = 2. *Example: Footstep bearing of a vertical shaft.*
6. **Universal (Hooke's) Pair** — Two perpendicular axes of rotation. DOF = 2. *Example: Universal joint in a car's drive shaft.*

(Marking: 7 marks = 1.5 marks per pair + 1 mark for the concluding comparative statement.)

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given:**
- Crank length, $r = 100$ mm
- Connecting rod length, $l = 400$ mm
- $N = 2400$ rpm
- $\theta = 45°$ from IDC

**Step 1 — Angular velocity of the crank.**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} = 80\pi \approx 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of the slider (approximate formula for $l/r \geq 4$):**

The ratio $l/r = 400/100 = 4$. The exact KTU formula is:

$$v = r\omega \left[\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right]$$

where $n = l/r = 4$. For $\theta = 45°$:

$$v = 100 \times 251.33 \times \left[\sin 45° + \frac{\sin 90°}{2 \times 4}\right]$$
$$v = 25133 \times \left[0.7071 + \frac{1}{8}\right]$$
$$v = 25133 \times \left[0.7071 + 0.125\right]$$
$$v = 25133 \times 0.8321 \approx 20{,}912 \text{ mm/s} \approx 20.91 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration of the slider (approximate formula):**

$$a = r\omega^2 \left[\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]$$

For $\theta = 45°$:

$$a = 100 \times (251.33)^2 \times \left[\cos 45° + \frac{\cos 90°}{4}\right]$$
$$a = 100 \times 63{,}166 \times \left[0.7071 + 0\right]$$
$$a = 6{,}316{,}600 \times 0.7071 \approx 4{,}466{,}190 \text{ mm/s}^2 \approx 4466 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final Answer:**
- Slider velocity $v \approx 20.91$ m/s
- Slider acceleration $a \approx 4466$ m/s²

(7 marks: 1 for $\omega$, 2 for $v$ formula and substitution, 2 for $a$ formula and substitution, 2 for the final numerical answer and units.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Kinematics** = study of motion (no forces). **Kinetics** = study of forces causing motion.
- A **link** is a relatively rigid body. A **pair** is a constrained joint. A **chain** is linked pairs; add a *fixed link* → **mechanism**; add *useful work* → **machine**.
- **Lower pair** = surface contact, more durable. **Higher pair** = point/line contact, used for variable motion.
- **Sliding, Revolute, Rolling, Screw** pairs = **1 DOF**. **Cylindrical, Universal** = **2 DOF**. **Spherical, Planar** = **3 DOF**.
- **Kutzbach (Planar):** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. Always count the **ground link** in $n$.
- **Grashof's Law (Four-bar):** $s + l \leq p + q$. Equality → *Class I* (special Grashof). Determines crank/rocker/double-crank behavior.
- **F = 1** → ideal mechanism. **F = 0** → structure. **F < 0** → redundant constraints.
- **Higher pair** constraints = **1**; **Lower pair** constraints = **2**.
- **Kinematic diagram** first → *then* formula. (Avoid 2-mark loss in KTU valuation.)
- **Screw pair = Lower pair**, **Cylindrical pair = 2 DOF**, **Universal pair = 2 DOF** — these are the top 3 KTU MCQ traps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT403 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Kinematic Diagram** (also called a *kinematic skeleton* or *schematic diagram*) is a symbolic, scaled representation of a machine or mechanism that depicts the **geometric arrangement of all links and joints** while omitting unnecessary physical details such as exact shapes, sizes, surface finishes, and proportions of the actual machine parts. Its primary purpose is to clearly communicate the **relative motion** between the various components without the visual clutter of a full assembly drawing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> A kinematic diagram is a line diagram that represents the relative motion between the parts of a machine. It is drawn using standard graphical symbols prescribed by **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) – IS 13215 / ISO 3952** that represent various types of kinematic pairs and links.

### Essential Characteristics of a Kinematic Diagram:
- It is a **simplified symbolic sketch**, not a dimensioned engineering drawing.
- It shows the **type, location, and connectivity** of all kinematic pairs.
- All links are represented by **straight lines or simple geometric symbols**.
- The **driving member** is conventionally indicated and the input motion is shown.
- It serves as the foundation for **kinematic analysis** (velocity, acceleration) and **mechanism synthesis**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Intuitive Analogy: The "Stick Figure" of a Machine**
> 
> Imagine you are explaining how a human walks to someone who has never seen a person before. You wouldn't draw a detailed anatomical sketch of bones, muscles, and skin. Instead, you would draw a simple **stick figure** — a head, a torso, lines for arms, lines for legs, and circles for joints (shoulder, elbow, knee). That stick figure tells the story of *how the body moves* without showing the cosmetic details.
> 
> A **kinematic diagram is exactly that — the "stick figure" of a machine**. It captures *how the parts move* (the topology of motion), discarding the cosmetic details (curves, fills, machining features). When a KTU examiner or a design engineer wants to *analyse* the mechanism, they don't want the engineering drawing — they want the stick figure.

Another everyday analogy is a **metro/subway map**. The actual geographical distances and curves of the rail line are not preserved. Only the **sequence of stations (links) and the connections (joints)** matter for understanding how to travel from one place to another. The kinematic diagram does the same thing for a mechanism.

## 1.3 Why Kinematic Diagrams Matter in KTU Examinations

In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 of PCRAT403 explicitly tests the student's ability to:
1. Identify the **type of mechanism** from a real machine sketch.
2. Draw the kinematic diagram with **correctly standardized symbols**.
3. Determine the **number of links, joints, and degrees of freedom (DOF)** from the diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU ESE (End Semester Exam), a 14-mark question on kinematic diagrams typically involves (a) drawing the diagram for a given machine (7 marks) and (b) computing the degrees of freedom using Kutzbach/Gruebler's criterion (7 marks). Both sub-parts are *direct consequences* of correctly drawing the kinematic diagram.

## 1.4 Standard Governing Codes

| Code | Issuing Body | Purpose |
|------|--------------|---------|
| **IS 13215** | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | Indian standard for kinematic symbols |
| **ISO 3952** | International Organization for Standardization | International kinematic symbol standard |
| **ANSI Y14.3** | American National Standards Institute | US standard for multiview and section view drawings (referenced for symbol compatibility) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Building Blocks

A kinematic diagram is built using two atomic elements:

1. **Links (Rigid Bodies):** Drawn as simple straight lines, rectangles, or triangles. Each link is assigned a number (1, 2, 3 … n).
2. **Kinematic Pairs (Joints):** Drawn at the points where two links share a constrained relative motion. The symbol used depends on the *type* of pair (revolute, prismatic, cam, gear, etc.).

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Machine Visualisation

The complete understanding of a machine passes through three progressively abstract levels:

| Level | Name | What it Shows | KTU Use |
|-------|------|---------------|---------|
| **Level 1** | Assembly / Detailed Drawing | Shapes, dimensions, materials, tolerances | Manufacturing |
| **Level 2** | Skeleton / Layout Drawing | Real proportions of links, but no surface details | Spatial planning |
| **Level 3** | **Kinematic Diagram** | Symbols, joints, link connectivity (no proportions) | **Analysis (KTU Module 1)** |

## 2.3 Procedure to Draw a Kinematic Diagram (KTU Step-Method)

> [!NOTE]
> This 6-step procedure is the **canonical KTU answer** whenever a question asks "Explain the procedure to draw a kinematic diagram."

1. **Identify the Fixed Link (Frame / Ground):** Usually represented by **hatched lines**. Identify which part of the machine is stationary relative to the chosen reference frame.
2. **Identify All Moving Links:** Every rigid body that has any motion relative to the frame is a link. Number them sequentially (1, 2, 3 … n), with the **driver (input link)** being link 1 by convention.
3. **Identify All Joints (Kinematic Pairs):** Locate every location where two links have constrained relative motion. Classify each joint as lower pair, higher pair, etc.
4. **Select Standard Symbols:** Choose the appropriate IS / ISO symbol for each link and joint. Symbols are usually drawn to *one size* regardless of the actual machine.
5. **Sketch the Topology:** Connect the symbols with straight lines representing links. Maintain the *relative spatial order* of joints but not the absolute distances.
6. **Label and Indicate Input:** Label all links, mark the driver (input link 1), and add an arrow indicating the direction of the input motion.

## 2.4 Standard Kinematic Symbols (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Joint / Link | IS 13215 / ISO 3952 Symbol | Description |
|--------------|----------------------------|-------------|
| **Revolute Pair (R)** | Circle with a fixed pivot, or a small filled circle on a hatched ground | One rotational DOF — a pin joint or hinge |
| **Prismatic Pair (P)** | Rectangle sliding on a hatched straight guide | One translational DOF — slider in a slot |
| **Rigid Link** | Thin straight line (sometimes a slim rectangle) | Distance between two joints is the link length |
| **Fixed Pivot (Hinge to Ground)** | Triangle with hatched base, small circle at apex | Link rotating about a fixed point |
| **Slider / Piston** | Rectangle with a small extending stem | Translating element |
| **Cam Pair** | Two curves in contact, or a circle and a flat | Higher pair with line / point contact |
| **Gear Pair** | Two circles with teeth indications (or pitch circles) | Higher pair with rolling/sliding contact |
| **Belt / Chain Drive** | Two pulleys connected by a curved line | Higher pair — flexible connector |
| **Screw Pair (H)** | Rectangle on a threaded shaft | Helical motion (combined R + P) |
| **Spherical Pair** | Sphere (3 rotational DOF) | Ball-and-socket type joint |
| **Cylindrical Pair (C)** | Cylinder in a cylindrical bore | Combined R + P (2 DOF) |
| **Plane Pair** | Flat on a flat surface | 3 DOF — rarely used in planar problems |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|----------------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $n$ | Total number of links in the mechanism | Counting rigid bodies |
| 2 | $j_1$ | Number of lower pairs (1 DOF) — revolute, prismatic | R and P joints |
| 3 | $j_2$ | Number of higher pairs (2 DOF) — cam, gear | C and G contacts |
| 4 | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **Kutzbach's Equation** (planar mechanism, no redundant DOF) | DOF calculation |
| 5 | $F = 3(n-2) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **Gruebler's Equation** (planar mechanism, multiple DOF links) | General planar DOF |
| 6 | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | **Kutzbach's Spatial Equation** | Spatial / 3D mechanisms |
| 7 | $N = j_1 + j_2 + \ldots$ | Total number of joints | Counting constrained pairs |
| 8 | $n = 2P - 4$ | For a four-bar linkage (P = number of pins) | Four-bar specific check |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega_{out}}{\omega_{in}}$ | Speed ratio (for gear / belt pairs) | Kinematic relation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notational Trap:** In KTU textbooks, *Gruebler's* and *Kutzbach's* equations are sometimes used interchangeably. The form $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ **assumes no link has more than one DOF** (i.e., no multiple-DOF pairs). The form $F = 3(n-2) - 2j_1 - j_2$ **is the truly general planar form** and is the safer KTU answer.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kinematic diagrams are the **first deliverable** in any mechanism design cycle:

- **Automotive Design:** Engineers use kinematic diagrams of the suspension, steering (Ackermann), and valve train (cam-follower) before CAD modelling.
- **Robotics:** Serial and parallel manipulator topologies are first expressed as kinematic skeletons.
- **Aerospace:** Landing gear linkages are analysed via kinematic diagrams for retraction kinematics.
- **Biomechanics:** Human joints (shoulder = spherical pair, knee ≈ revolute pair) are modelled as kinematic chains for prosthetic design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kutzbach's Criterion for Planar Mechanisms

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 1. Memorise the logic, not just the formula.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions

Let:
- $n$ = total number of links in the planar mechanism (including the fixed link / ground)
- $f_i$ = number of degrees of freedom removed by joint $i$
- $j$ = total number of joints (kinematic pairs)

### 3.1.2 Counting Unconstrained DOF

If the $n$ links were **completely free in the plane**, each link would have **3 degrees of freedom** (translation along X, translation along Y, and rotation in the plane). So the total unconstrained DOF would be:

$$
DOF_{free} = 3n
$$

### 3.1.3 Subtracting the DOF of the Fixed Link

In a real mechanism, one link is **fixed to the ground**. A fixed link has 0 DOF, but in the free count above, it is counted as having 3 DOF. So we subtract those 3 unphysical DOF:

$$
DOF_{free,\,reduced} = 3n - 3 = 3(n - 1)
$$

### 3.1.4 Subtracting Constraints from Joints

Now, each joint removes some DOF:

- A **lower pair** (revolute R or prismatic P) is a 1-DOF joint, meaning the two links retain 1 relative DOF. The pair therefore **removes $3 - 1 = 2$ DOF**.
- A **higher pair** (cam C or gear G) is a 2-DOF joint, meaning the two links retain 2 relative DOF. The pair therefore **removes $3 - 2 = 1$ DOF**.

If there are $j_1$ lower pairs and $j_2$ higher pairs, the total DOF removed by all joints is:

$$
DOF_{removed} = 2j_1 + j_2
$$

### 3.1.5 Final DOF Expression

The remaining independent motions (the mechanism's degrees of freedom $F$) is:

$$
F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

This is **Kutzbach's Equation for Planar Mechanisms** (single-DOF assumption).

### 3.1.6 Gruebler's General Form (Including Multi-DOF Pairs)

If a mechanism contains a **planar pair** (a flat surface contact giving 3 DOF), or a **pin in a slot** (which behaves like a 2-DOF pair), the count changes. For a joint that retains $k$ relative DOF, the joint removes $(3 - k)$ DOF. For a general planar mechanism with $n$ links, $j_1$ single-DOF pairs, $j_2$ two-DOF pairs (line contact), $j_3$ three-DOF pairs (planar contact), etc., the spatial argument gives:

$$
F = 3n - 2j_1 - j_2 - \ldots
$$

For planar mechanisms, after fixing one link (subtract 3), the canonical Gruebler form is:

$$
F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**In KTU papers, both forms appear.** The safer board-exam answer is the form $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ and to explicitly state the assumption: *"Each lower pair has 1 DOF, each higher pair has 2 DOF, and all links (other than the frame) are rigid with 3 DOF when free."*

## 3.2 Worked Example: Slider-Crank Mechanism (KTU Hallmark Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most repeated 14-mark question** in KTU 2024 / 2019 scheme papers. Practise it thoroughly.

### Problem Statement
A slider-crank mechanism consists of a crank, a connecting rod, a slider, and a fixed cylinder. (a) Draw its kinematic diagram. (b) Compute the degrees of freedom.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the links:**

| Link Number | Physical Part | DOF when free |
|:-----------:|---------------|:-------------:|
| 1 | Crank (driver) | 3 |
| 2 | Connecting rod | 3 |
| 3 | Slider (piston) | 3 |
| 4 | Frame (ground) | 0 (fixed) |

So $n = 4$ links in total.

**Step 2 — Identify the joints (kinematic pairs):**

| Joint | Location | Type | DOF of Pair | Lower/Higher |
|:-----:|----------|------|:-----------:|:------------:|
| A | Crank pivot on frame | Revolute | 1 | Lower ($j_1$) |
| B | Crank–connecting rod joint | Revolute | 1 | Lower ($j_1$) |
| C | Connecting rod–slider joint | Revolute | 1 | Lower ($j_1$) |
| D | Slider in cylinder | Prismatic | 1 | Lower ($j_1$) |

So $j_1 = 4$ and $j_2 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Apply Kutzbach's equation:**

$$
F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Substituting $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$:

$$
F = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

$$
F = 3 \times 3 - 8 = 9 - 8
$$

$$
F = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**

$F = 1$ confirms the slider-crank is a **single-DOF mechanism**, requiring exactly **one input** (the crank rotation $\theta_1$) to fully determine the motion of every other link.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DOF of Common Mechanisms

| Mechanism | $n$ | $j_1$ | $j_2$ | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Expected DOF |
|-----------|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:-------------------------:|:------------:|
| Four-bar chain | 4 | 4 | 0 | $3(3) - 8 = 1$ | 1 |
| Slider-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | $3(3) - 8 = 1$ | 1 |
| Double-slider (ellipsograph) | 4 | 4 | 0 | $3(3) - 8 = 1$ | 1 |
| Five-bar chain (no higher pair) | 5 | 5 | 0 | $3(4) - 10 = 2$ | 2 |
| Cam-follower (cam + follower) | 3 | 2 | 1 | $3(2) - 4 - 1 = 1$ | 1 |
| Epicyclic / planetary gear | variable | variable | variable | formula needs care | special |
| Mechanism with redundancy | — | — | — | $F \le 0$ implies **locked / structure** | 0 |

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Why Each Joint Removes the DOF It Does

Consider two rigid links in the plane, free to move independently. They have $3 + 3 = 6$ DOF. Now impose a joint:

### Case A — Revolute Pair (R)
The two links share a common pivot. Constraints imposed:
1. The X-coordinate of the joint on link A equals the X-coordinate of the joint on link B.
2. The Y-coordinate of the joint on link A equals the Y-coordinate of the joint on link B.
3. The pivot is a *point* constraint, so the angle of the joint location is *not* constrained.

This removes **2 translational DOF**, leaving **1 rotational DOF** (relative rotation about the pivot). So a revolute pair **removes 2 DOF** and is a **1-DOF lower pair**.

### Case B — Prismatic Pair (P)
The two links can translate relative to each other, but **rotation is locked** and the line of translation is fixed.
1. Rotation removed → 1 constraint.
2. One translational DOF is removed (perpendicular to the slide direction).
3. One translation along the slide direction is retained.

So a prismatic pair **removes 2 DOF** and is a **1-DOF lower pair**.

### Case C — Cam Pair (Higher Pair with Line Contact)
Two curved surfaces touch along a line. The contact point can roll and slide along the common tangent, but the bodies cannot interpenetrate.
1. The bodies cannot interpenetrate (1 constraint — perpendicular to tangent).
2. They can roll (1 retained DOF).
3. They can slide along the tangent (1 retained DOF).

So a cam pair **removes 1 DOF** and is a **2-DOF higher pair**.

### Case D — Gear Pair (Higher Pair with Tooth Contact)
Tooth contact approximates line contact. Same logic as cam pair: **removes 1 DOF, retains 2**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorisation Aid (the "1-2-1" rule):**
> - A **lower pair** removes **2 DOF** (retains 1).
> - A **higher pair** removes **1 DOF** (retains 2).
> - In the plane, a free link has **3 DOF**.

## 3.5 Special Case: Mechanism with Passive / Redundant DOF

If the calculated $F$ is greater than the *expected* DOF (e.g., a 4-bar mechanism gives $F = 2$ instead of 1), the mechanism has a **passive DOF** (also called *idle* or *redundant* DOF). The Gruebler-Kutzbach formula does *not* account for these. The correct DOF in such cases is found by the **Kutzbach mobility criterion after eliminating the passive DOF** or by the **loop-closure approach**.

In KTU 2024, the modular focus is on **simple single-loop** mechanisms, so $F \le 1$ is the expected range for most ESE questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: How a Kinematic Diagram is Created

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the canonical 6-step procedure taught in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Observe the Real Machine] --> B[Identify the Fixed Link Frame]
    B --> C[Identify All Moving Rigid Links]
    C --> D[Number Links Driver becomes Link 1]
    D --> E[Locate All Joints Between Links]
    E --> F[Classify Each Joint as Lower or Higher Pair]
    F --> G[Assign IS 13215 ISO 3952 Standard Symbol]
    G --> H[Draw Symbols and Connect With Straight Lines]
    H --> I[Mark Driver Link and Input Motion Arrow]
    I --> J[Validate by Counting DOF Using Kutzbach]
    J --> K{Is F consistent with physical inputs}
    K --> L[Yes: Finalise Kinematic Diagram]
    K --> M[No: Recheck Joint Classification]
    M --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block-level diagram above replaces a freehand sketch. In a KTU written exam, the actual kinematic diagram must be hand-drawn on the answer sheet using a pencil and ruler, with **hatched lines for the frame** and **numbered links**.

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Symbolic Components of a Kinematic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Frame Ground Link] -->|Hatched Surface| B[Fixed Pivot Symbol]
    B --> C[Link 1 Driver Crank]
    C -->|Revolute Pair R| D[Joint B Pin]
    D --> E[Link 2 Coupler or Connecting Rod]
    E -->|Revolute Pair R| F[Joint C Pin]
    F --> G[Link 3 Rocker or Slider]
    G -->|Revolute or Prismatic| H[Joint D Output]
    H --> I[Frame Ground]
    I -->|Loop Closure| A
```

This block-level schematic shows the **topology** of a typical four-bar / slider-crank kinematic chain. The four blocks (Frame, Driver, Coupler, Output) are connected by three lower pairs plus the implicit closure with the ground.

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Identifying a Joint from a Real-Machine Sketch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Observe Joint in Real Machine] --> Q2{Is there rotational motion only}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Mark as Revolute Pair R]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is there translational motion only}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P1[Mark as Prismatic Pair P]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is there combined rotation and translation}
    Q4 -->|Yes, one axis| CP1[Mark as Cylindrical Pair C]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is there contact along a line or point}
    Q5 -->|Yes, cam or gear| HP1[Mark as Higher Pair]
    Q5 -->|No| Q6{Is it a ball and socket}
    Q6 -->|Yes| SP1[Mark as Spherical Pair S]
    Q6 -->|No| SP2[Refer to ISO 3952 Extended List]
```

## 4.4 Symbol Reference Chart (Textual Block Representation)

| Block | Represents | KTU Drawing Convention |
|-------|-----------|------------------------|
| ▭ thin line | Rigid link | A single straight line with a number tag |
| ⚪ filled circle | Pin / revolute joint | A small filled black circle |
| ▭▭ double rectangle | Slider | Rectangle with stem showing the slide direction |
| ╳ hatched | Ground / frame | Hatched straight line at 45° |
| ⌒ curve | Cam profile or gear pitch | Curved arc with contact indicator |
| ⊕ circle with cross | Fixed pivot (hinge) | Triangle with hatched base |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE scripts, the **frame is always drawn with diagonal hatched lines at 45°**, and **joints are shown as small filled circles or small open circles** depending on whether the joint is internal (between two moving links) or to the ground.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a kinematic diagram. Why is it preferred over an assembly drawing for kinematic analysis?**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

A kinematic diagram is a **simplified symbolic representation** of a mechanism that shows the **relative motion between its links** using standard symbols prescribed by IS 13215 / ISO 3952.

It is preferred over an assembly drawing because:
1. It eliminates irrelevant geometric details (curves, fillets, tolerances).
2. It focuses exclusively on the **type and connectivity of joints**.
3. It enables quick identification of links, joints, and computation of degrees of freedom.
4. It forms the **first step** in velocity and acceleration analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiner's note:* Award full 3 marks only if the student explicitly mentions **IS / ISO standard symbols** and the **purpose of relative motion analysis**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six standard symbols used in kinematic diagrams with their meaning.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Revolute pair (R):** Shown as a small filled circle on a link; denotes a pin / hinge joint allowing 1 rotational DOF.
2. **Prismatic pair (P):** Shown as a rectangle sliding on a hatched guide; denotes 1 translational DOF.
3. **Rigid link:** A straight thin line with a numerical label; represents a rigid body.
4. **Fixed pivot:** A triangle with hatched base; denotes a link hinged to the frame.
5. **Cam pair:** Two curves in tangential contact; denotes a higher pair with line / point contact.
6. **Gear pair:** Two circles with pitch indications in contact; denotes a higher pair.

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiner's note:* A clean labeled diagram accompanying the list earns an extra half-mark in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE regulations mandate that any 14-mark question offer an **internal choice**. The student answers either Question A or Question B. Both options are provided below.

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the procedure to draw a kinematic diagram of a slider-crank mechanism. Identify all links and kinematic pairs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using Kutzbach's criterion, determine the degrees of freedom of the slider-crank mechanism. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Procedure (Step-wise, as required by KTU 2024 rubric):**

1. **Identify the fixed link:** The cylinder block of the engine is the **frame (link 4)**. It is drawn with **hatched lines** indicating the ground.
2. **Identify the moving links:**
   - **Link 1:** The crank (driver), rotating about the fixed pivot O.
   - **Link 2:** The connecting rod (coupler), connecting the crank to the slider.
   - **Link 3:** The slider (piston), translating inside the cylinder.
3. **Identify the kinematic pairs:**
   - **Pair at O:** Revolute pair between the **frame (4)** and **crank (1)**.
   - **Pair at A:** Revolute pair between **crank (1)** and **connecting rod (2)**.
   - **Pair at B:** Revolute pair between **connecting rod (2)** and **slider (3)**.
   - **Pair at C (slider–cylinder):** Prismatic pair between **slider (3)** and **frame (4)**.
4. **Draw using symbols:** The crank is drawn as a straight line of arbitrary length OA. The connecting rod is drawn as a straight line AB. The slider is drawn as a small rectangle with a stem at B, sliding along the cylinder axis (hatched). The fixed pivot at O is drawn as a triangle with hatched base.
5. **Mark the driver:** Link 1 (crank) is labeled with an arrow indicating the direction of input rotation $\omega_1$.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given (from kinematic diagram):**
- Number of links, $n = 4$ (1 fixed + 3 moving)
- Number of lower pairs, $j_1 = 4$ (three revolute at O, A, B and one prismatic at C)
- Number of higher pairs, $j_2 = 0$

**Applying Kutzbach's equation:**

$$
F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
F = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

$$
F = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

**Comment:** $F = 1$ confirms the slider-crank is a **single-input mechanism**. Knowing the input motion of link 1 (the crank rotation $\theta_1$) is sufficient to completely determine the position, velocity, and acceleration of all other links.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting to count the frame:** Many students write $n = 3$ instead of $n = 4$. The frame is a *link* in the kinematic count even though it is stationary. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Misclassifying the slider–cylinder pair as higher:** A slider in a cylinder is a **prismatic (lower) pair**, not a higher pair. The contact is *surface contact*, not line / point contact. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Writing the wrong form of the equation:** Using $F = 3(n-2) - 2j_1 - j_2$ without justification may attract a remark from the examiner. Stick to $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ and state the **assumption** clearly. **Lose up to 1 mark.**

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Draw the kinematic diagram of a **four-bar mechanism** consisting of a frame, a crank, a coupler, and an output rocker. Label all links and pairs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the degrees of freedom of the four-bar mechanism using Kutzbach's equation. State the role of the fixed link. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Procedure:**

1. **Fixed link (Link 4 — Frame):** A stationary body connecting the two fixed pivots. Drawn with diagonal hatching.
2. **Crank (Link 1):** A short rotating link, drawn as a straight line of arbitrary length, hinged to the frame at fixed pivot O.
3. **Coupler (Link 2):** A straight line connecting the moving pivot A (on the crank) to the moving pivot B (on the rocker).
4. **Rocker (Link 3):** A link oscillating about the fixed pivot O'. Drawn as a straight line from O' to B.
5. **Pairs identified (all four are revolute):**
   - O: between frame and crank
   - A: between crank and coupler
   - B: between coupler and rocker
   - O': between rocker and frame

The kinematic diagram is a **quadrilateral OA-B-O'** with the side OO' (hatched) being the **ground link**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:** $n = 4$ (frame + crank + coupler + rocker), $j_1 = 4$ (all revolute), $j_2 = 0$.

$$
F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
F = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

**Role of the fixed link:** The fixed link (frame) **closes the kinematic loop** and provides the two anchor pivots (O and O') about which the crank and rocker oscillate. Without the frame, the four-bar mechanism would be an **open chain of three moving links** with $F = 7$, i.e., uncontrolled motion. The frame's role is to:
1. Provide **geometric closure** of the loop.
2. Convert an open chain into a **closed chain** (single DOF).
3. Serve as the **reference for all relative motions**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Drawing the four-bar with curved crank and rocker:** The crank and rocker must be drawn as **straight lines** in a kinematic diagram. Curves are reserved for higher-pair symbols (cam). **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting to label the frame as Link 4:** The frame must be visibly *hatched* and *numbered*. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting to write the assumption** of Kutzbach's equation (planar, single-DOF pairs). **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 4. **Not stating the role of the fixed link explicitly** in part (b) is a **2-mark deduction** because the question explicitly asks for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1, Kinematic Diagrams**

- [x] **Definition:** A kinematic diagram is a *symbolic, non-proportional* representation showing relative motion of links using *standardised symbols* (IS 13215 / ISO 3952).
- [x] **Three levels of machine drawing:** Detailed assembly → Skeleton → **Kinematic diagram** (most abstract).
- [x] **Six-step procedure to draw:** Identify frame → Identify moving links → Identify joints → Choose symbols → Sketch topology → Label driver and input.
- [x] **Link numbering convention:** Driver = Link 1, frame = highest number (usually $n$).
- [x] **Joint types to memorise:** Revolute (R, lower), Prismatic (P, lower), Cam (C, higher), Gear (G, higher), Screw (H, lower), Spherical (S, lower 3-DOF), Cylindrical (C, lower 2-DOF).
- [x] **Kutzbach's Equation (planar):** $F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. Memorise the derivation, not just the formula.
- [x] **DOF removed per joint:** Lower pair = **2**, Higher pair = **1**, Free link in plane = **3**.
- [x] **Frame MUST be counted** as a link in $n$. The most common error.
- [x] **Slider–cylinder is a prismatic pair** (lower, not higher).
- [x] **Higher pair** ↔ cam, gear, belt, chain (point / line contact).
- [x] **Lower pair** ↔ R, P, screw, spherical, cylindrical, planar (surface contact).
- [x] **Expected DOF for typical KTU mechanisms:** four-bar = 1, slider-crank = 1, five-bar = 2, cam-follower = 1.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** when applying Kutzbach: planar, single-DOF pairs, no passive DOF.
- [x] **Draw the frame with 45° hatched lines** and the driver with an input-motion arrow.
- [x] **Practice the slider-crank and four-bar** — they account for > 60% of KTU ESE Module 1 questions.

---

**End of Module 1 — Kinematic Diagrams Notes | PCRAT403 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Freedom - Grashof's Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT403 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degree of Freedom - Grashof's Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Degree of Freedom (Mobility) — Formal Definition

**Degree of Freedom (DOF)**, also termed **Mobility (M)** of a mechanism, is defined as the **minimum number of independent inputs (driving links) required to completely and unambiguously determine the position, velocity, and configuration of every link** in a kinematic chain or mechanism.

Mathematically, for a planar mechanism, the mobility is evaluated by **Kutzbach's Criterion**:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where:
- **n** = total number of links (including the fixed/ground link)
- **j₁** = number of lower pairs (1 DOF joints, e.g., revolute, prismatic)
- **j₂** = number of higher pairs (2 DOF joints, e.g., cam-follower, gear teeth contact)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A mechanism is said to have **determinate motion** when **M = 1** (one input produces one defined output). If **M = 0**, it is a **structure** (no motion). If **M > 1**, it is a **redundant / indeterminate mechanism**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Robot Arm" Concept

Imagine a robotic arm mounted on a workshop floor. Each joint (shoulder, elbow, wrist) allows the arm to bend in one specific way. To fully control where the gripper reaches in space, you need to specify certain joint angles — these specifications are the **independent inputs**. The number of such independent inputs you must command equals the **degree of freedom**.

For a planar four-bar chain:
- **4 links** joined by **4 revolute pins** → You only need to fix the angle of one link (the input crank). The other three links follow automatically.
- Therefore, **Mobility M = 1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fixed Link Rule:** The ground (frame) link is ALWAYS counted in *n* because it removes DOFs but still occupies a kinematic position in the chain.

### 1.3 Grashof's Criterion — Formal Definition

**Grashof's Law (1843)** states the necessary geometric condition on the four link lengths of a four-bar linkage for **continuous (full) rotation** of at least one link about a fixed pivot:

$$s + l \le p + q$$

where:
- **s** = length of the **shortest** link
- **l** = length of the **longest** link
- **p, q** = lengths of the remaining two (intermediate) links

A four-bar linkage satisfying this inequality is called a **Grashof Linkage**.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Scissors" Test

Picture a pair of scissors. When you close them:
- One blade **rotates fully** about the pivot (the **crank**).
- The other blade **oscillates** (the **rocker**).
- The link connecting the two handles (the **coupler**) translates in a complex path.

This is precisely a **Crank-Rocker mechanism** — one of the four Grashof configurations. Grashof's Law is the geometric "rule" that tells you *whether* such a configuration is even possible with the chosen link lengths.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kutzbach's Mobility Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. Each link in free space (unconnected) has **3 planar DOFs** (x-translation, y-translation, rotation) → total free DOFs = **3n**.
2. The ground link is fixed → it loses **3 DOFs**.
3. Free links remaining: **3(n − 1)**.
4. Each **lower pair (j₁)** removes **2 DOFs** (constrains 2 motions, leaves 1).
5. Each **higher pair (j₂)** removes **1 DOF** (constrains 1 motion, leaves 2).
6. Net Mobility: **M = 3(n − 1) − 2j₁ − j₂**.

### 2.2 Gruebler's Equation (Spatial Mechanism)

For **spatial (3D)** mechanisms, the generalized form is:

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, **planar four-bar mechanisms dominate Module 1**; spatial mobility is generally reserved for advanced modules.

### 2.3 Classification of Four-Bar Linkages — Grashof's Decision Tree

| Condition | Inequality | Class | Shortest Link Position | Resulting Mechanism |
|-----------|------------|-------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Grashof | s + l < p + q | Class I | Ground | **Double-Crank (Drag-link)** |
| Grashof | s + l < p + q | Class I | Side (adjacent to ground) | **Crank-Rocker** |
| Grashof | s + l < p + q | Class I | Coupler (opposite ground) | **Double-Rocker (Grashof)** |
| Special | s + l = p + q | Class II | Any | **Change-Point Mechanism** |
| Non-Grashof | s + l > p + q | Class III | Any | **Triple-Rocker (No full rotation)** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---------|---------|------|
| M = 3(n−1) − 2j₁ − j₂ | Planar mobility (Kutzbach) | Dimensionless |
| M = 6(n−1) − Σ(6−f)·j_f | Generalized mobility (Gruebler) | Dimensionless |
| s + l ≤ p + q | Grashof's inequality | Length (m or mm) |
| Grashof sum: **S = s + l** | Sum of extreme links | Length |
| Non-Grashof sum: **NS = p + q** | Sum of intermediate links | Length |
| Grashof class flag | S < NS → Class I, S = NS → Class II, S > NS → Class III | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Grashof's criterion is the foundational design check for **automotive suspension systems, sewing machine drives, wind-turbine flap governors, oil-well pump jacks, and IC engine valve mechanisms**. Every conceptual design starts with this inequality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Kutzbach's Equation (from First Principles)

**Step 1:** Count the total planar DOFs of all *n* links in unrestricted space.

Each rigid link in a 2D plane has 3 independent motions: translation along X, translation along Y, and rotation θ. So total free DOFs = 3n.

**Step 2:** Subtract DOFs removed by the fixed ground link.

The ground link is stationary, so it removes 3 DOFs.

Free DOFs of moving links = 3n − 3 = **3(n − 1)**.

**Step 3:** Subtract DOFs removed by lower pairs (1-DOF joints).

A revolute (R) or prismatic (P) joint removes 2 DOFs (constrains 2 motions, permits 1).

Total DOFs removed by j₁ lower pairs = **2j₁**.

**Step 4:** Subtract DOFs removed by higher pairs (2-DOF joints, like a cam-follower contact).

A higher pair removes 1 DOF (permits rolling + sliding = 2 freedoms).

Total DOFs removed by j₂ higher pairs = **j₂**.

**Step 5:** Combine.

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
\text{where} \quad & n = \text{total links (incl. ground)} \\
& j_1 = \text{lower pair count} \\
& j_2 = \text{higher pair count}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Grashof's Geometric Condition (Logical Construction)

Consider a planar four-bar linkage with link lengths **a, b, c, d** where **a** is the shortest and **d** is the longest.

**Step 1:** Identify the extreme links.

Let **s = a** (shortest), **l = d** (longest), **p = b**, **q = c** (intermediates, with p ≤ q without loss of generality).

**Step 2:** Examine the path traced by the **end of the shortest link** when rotated about a fixed pivot (assumed ground-attached at the longest link's joint).

For the shortest link to complete a **full 360° revolution**, the joint connecting it must always remain reachable by the other two links. This requires that during rotation:

- The sum of (shortest + intermediate 1) ≥ distance to opposite pivot at every angle.
- The longest link must never be stretched beyond the reach of (s + p).

**Step 3:** The critical (tightest) geometric constraint occurs when the shortest link aligns **collinearly** with the opposite ground pivot. At this instant:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reach required} &= p + q \\
\text{Maximum reach available} &= s + l \\
\text{For full rotation:} \quad s + l &\ge p + q \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Allowing for the equality case (change-point mechanism where the link just barely completes one rotation before locking), Grashof arrived at:

$$\boxed{s + l \le p + q}$$

**Step 5:** The "≤" sign admits three regimes:

- **Strict inequality (s + l < p + q)** → excess reach → at least one link rotates fully.
- **Equality (s + l = p + q)** → boundary case → change-point mechanism.
- **Reverse inequality (s + l > p + q)** → reach deficit → no link rotates fully.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solving a Generic Four-Bar Problem

**Given:** A four-bar linkage with links **a = 50 mm, b = 120 mm, c = 100 mm, d = 150 mm**. Identify its Grashof class and mechanism type.

**Step A — Identify extremes:**

Shortest = **s = 50 mm**, Longest = **l = 150 mm**, Intermediates = **p = 100 mm, q = 120 mm**.

**Step B — Apply Grashof inequality:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s + l &= 50 + 150 = 200 \text{ mm} \\
p + q &= 100 + 120 = 220 \text{ mm} \\
s + l &= 200 < 220 = p + q \\
\therefore \text{It is a Grashof linkage (Class I)}.
\end{aligned}$$

**Step C — Locate shortest link in the kinematic chain:**

Assume **a = 50 mm is the side link (adjacent to ground)**: → **Crank-Rocker mechanism**.

> [!NOTE]
> If instead **a** were the coupler (link opposite the ground), the result would be a **Double-Rocker (Grashof)**.
> If **a** were the ground link, it would be a **Double-Crank (Drag-Link)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Four-Bar Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT DRIVING LINK\nDriver Crank\nFixed Pivot O2"] --> B["REVOLUTE JOINT O2\n1 DOF Lower Pair"]
    B --> C["COUPLER LINK\nFloating Link b\nTranslates in Complex Path"]
    C --> D["REVOLUTE JOINT A\n1 DOF Lower Pair"]
    C --> E["REVOLUTE JOINT B\n1 DOF Lower Pair"]
    D --> F["OUTPUT ROCKER LINK c\nOscillates about O4"]
    E --> G["GROUND LINK d\nFixed Frame O2-O4"]
    F --> H["OUTPUT MOTION\nAngular Oscillation Theta4"]
    G --> I["FIXED PIVOT O4\nReference Frame"]

    J["GRASHOF CHECK s+l LESS THAN p+q"] --> K["CLASS I Grashof"]
    J --> L["CLASS II Change Point"]
    J --> M["CLASS III Non Grashof"]

    K --> N["CRANK ROCKER if s is side"]
    K --> O["DOUBLE CRANK if s is ground"]
    K --> P["DOUBLE ROCKER if s is coupler"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs use **alphanumeric prefix** (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepA`, `stepB`) — no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`.
- Node labels are written in **plain uppercase text** without markdown bold or italics.
- No HTML tables are embedded inside Mermaid nodes.

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Linkage Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["GRASHOF CLASS I s+l LESS THAN p+q"]
        A1["CRANK ROCKER\nShortest is Side"]
        A2["DOUBLE CRANK DRAG LINK\nShortest is Ground"]
        A3["DOUBLE ROCKER GRASHOF\nShortest is Coupler"]
    end

    subgraph B["CHANGE POINT CLASS II s+l EQUALS p+q"]
        B1["TRANSITION MECHANISM\nOne Position Locks"]
    end

    subgraph C["NON GRASHOF CLASS III s+l GREATER THAN p+q"]
        C1["TRIPLE ROCKER\nNo Full Rotation"]
    end

    Q["FOUR BAR INPUT\nLink Lengths s l p q"] --> A
    Q --> B
    Q --> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Diagrams in KTU answer scripts are expected to be **clean line-sketches with labeled link lengths**. The block diagrams above convey functional relationships and are excellent additions to your handwritten answer sheet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the term **Degree of Freedom** of a mechanism. State Kutzbach's criterion for a planar mechanism.

**Model Answer:**

> Degree of Freedom (Mobility), M, of a mechanism is defined as the **minimum number of independent inputs** required to completely determine the configuration of all its links.
>
> Kutzbach's criterion for planar mechanisms is:
>
> $$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
>
> where **n** = total number of links (including ground), **j₁** = number of lower pairs, **j₂** = number of higher pairs.
>
> For a proper mechanism, **M ≥ 1**; for M = 0 it is a structure; for M > 1 it is indeterminate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
State **Grashof's Law** for a four-bar linkage. List all four types of Grashof mechanisms.

**Model Answer:**

> Grashof's Law states that **continuous rotation** of at least one link in a four-bar mechanism is possible **if and only if** the sum of the shortest and longest links does **not exceed** the sum of the other two links:
>
> $$s + l \le p + q$$
>
> The four types of Grashof mechanisms are:
> 1. **Crank-Rocker** — shortest link is a side link.
> 2. **Double-Crank (Drag-Link)** — shortest link is the ground link.
> 3. **Double-Rocker (Grashof)** — shortest link is the coupler.
> 4. **Change-Point Mechanism** — equality case s + l = p + q.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** A four-bar mechanism ABCD has link lengths AB = 80 mm, BC = 200 mm, CD = 150 mm, and DA = 250 mm. AB and CD are the side links, AD is the fixed link (ground), and BC is the coupler. Determine the **Grashof class** and identify the **type of mechanism**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the four link lengths:
- AB = 80 mm (side)
- BC = 200 mm (coupler)
- CD = 150 mm (side)
- DA = 250 mm (ground)

**Step 2:** Sort the link lengths.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Shortest: } s &= 80 \text{ mm (AB)} \\
\text{Longest: } l &= 250 \text{ mm (DA)} \\
\text{Intermediates: } p &= 150 \text{ mm (CD)}, \quad q = 200 \text{ mm (BC)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Grashof's inequality.

$$\begin{aligned}
s + l &= 80 + 250 = 330 \text{ mm} \\
p + q &= 150 + 200 = 350 \text{ mm} \\
\therefore s + l &= 330 < 350 = p + q
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

Since **s + l < p + q**, the linkage satisfies the Grashof condition strictly → **Class I (Grashof)**.

**Step 5:** Locate the shortest link.

The shortest link is **AB**, which is a **side link** (adjacent to the ground link AD). By the Grashof decision tree:

> **The mechanism is a Crank-Rocker.**

In this mechanism, the link **AB** rotates fully (360°), and the opposite side link **CD** oscillates about its pivot.

---

**Q. (b)** For the four-bar mechanism in the previous sub-question, calculate the **mobility (M)** using Kutzbach's criterion and verify whether it produces a determinate motion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the kinematic count.

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \text{ links (AB, BC, CD, DA-ground)} \\
j_1 &= 4 \text{ lower pairs (4 revolute joints at A, B, C, D)} \\
j_2 &= 0 \text{ higher pairs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Kutzbach's equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
M &= 3 \times 3 - 8 \\
M &= 9 - 8 \\
M &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

Since **M = 1**, the mechanism has **determinate motion** — a single input (rotation of link AB) fully defines the configuration of all other links.

> [!NOTE]
> **Grashof classification** determines *which type* of motion is possible (full rotation, oscillation, etc.), while **Kutzbach mobility** determines *whether* the motion is fully constrained.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **different types of four-bar linkages** based on Grashof's criterion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Detailed):**

Based on Grashof's inequality s + l ≤ p + q, four-bar linkages are classified as:

**(i) Class I — Grashof Linkages (s + l < p + q):**

1. **Crank-Rocker:** Shortest link is a side link. The shortest link rotates fully (becomes the crank), and the opposite side link oscillates (rocker). *Application:* IC engine valve mechanism, sewing machine needle drive.

2. **Double-Crank (Drag-Link):** Shortest link is the ground link. Both side links rotate fully. Used in parallel-motion mechanisms and couplings where two shafts must be synchronized.

3. **Double-Rocker (Grashof):** Shortest link is the coupler. Neither side link rotates fully; both oscillate, but they can assume any position within the Grashof range. Used in walking-beam suspensions.

**(ii) Class II — Change-Point Mechanism (s + l = p + q):**

At a specific configuration, the linkage reaches a "dead-center" or **change-point** where the shortest link either fully rotates or locks. Used in toggle mechanisms for high-force amplification.

**(iii) Class III — Non-Grashof Linkages (s + l > p + q):**

**Triple-Rocker:** No link can rotate fully. All three moving links oscillate between their extreme positions. Used in slow-motion couplings.

> A neat schematic showing all four configurations side-by-side is mandatory in the answer sheet for full marks.

---

**Q. (b)** A planar mechanism has **6 links** and **7 lower pairs**. Determine its **degree of freedom** using Kutzbach's equation. Is the mechanism a structure, a mechanism, or indeterminate? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= 6 \\
j_1 &= 7 \\
j_2 &= 0 \text{ (assumed no higher pairs given)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Kutzbach's equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
M &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0 \\
M &= 15 - 14 \\
M &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

Since **M = 1**, the system has **determinate motion** and qualifies as a **proper mechanism**. A single input crank/link will completely define all other link configurations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to include the ground link in the count *n*. Always count *n = (moving links) + 1*.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **j₁** (1-DOF joints: R, P) with **j₂** (2-DOF joints: cam-follower, gear mesh). A revolute joint is j₁, NOT j₂.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In Grashof problems, students often mix up "shortest" and "longest" identification. Always sort the four lengths in ascending order first.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "M = 3n − 2j₁ − j₂" (missing the **−1** inside the parentheses) — this costs 1 mark. The correct form is **3(n − 1)**.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to **label** which link is the ground in Grashof classification — examiners often award 1 mark specifically for correctly identifying the ground link.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Mobility (M)** is the minimum number of inputs to fully define a mechanism's configuration.
- **Kutzbach's equation** for planar mechanisms: **M = 3(n − 1) − 2j₁ − j₂**.
- **Gruebler's equation** for spatial mechanisms: **M = 6(n − 1) − 5j₁ − 4j₂ − 3j₃ − 2j₄ − j₅**.
- **M = 0** → structure; **M = 1** → proper mechanism; **M > 1** → indeterminate.
- **Always include the ground link** when counting *n*.
- **Revolute (R), Prismatic (P), Screw, Cylindrical** = lower pairs (1 DOF joints).
- **Cam-follower, Gear mesh, Ball-on-flat** = higher pairs (2 DOF joints).
- **Grashof's Law:** s + l ≤ p + q.
- **Class I (Grashof):** s + l < p + q — at least one link rotates fully.
- **Class II (Change-Point):** s + l = p + q — boundary case with toggle behavior.
- **Class III (Non-Grashof):** s + l > p + q — no link rotates fully.
- **Shortest link on side** → Crank-Rocker.
- **Shortest link on ground** → Double-Crank (Drag-Link).
- **Shortest link as coupler** → Double-Rocker (Grashof).
- **Sort link lengths first** before applying Grashof's check.
- **Kutzbach tells *whether* motion is determinate; Grashof tells *what type* of motion occurs.**
- **Real-world applications:** IC engine valves (crank-rocker), sewing machines (crank-rocker), windshield wipers (double-rocker), couplings (double-crank), toggle clamps (change-point), walking mechanisms (triple-rocker).
- **In KTU answer sheets, always:** (a) state the formula, (b) substitute values, (c) show computation, (d) state the physical interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT403 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Inversions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kinematic Inversion** is the process of obtaining a new mechanism (or motion) by fixing a different link of a given kinematic chain to the ground (frame), while keeping the relative motion between the links unchanged. In the study of mechanisms, we begin with a basic **kinematic chain** — a system of rigid links connected by lower pairs. The same chain can produce vastly different machines simply by changing which link is anchored to the frame. Each new configuration obtained by fixing a different link is called an **inversion** of the original chain. For a kinematic chain with **n** links, the number of possible inversions is: $$ \text{Number of Inversions} = n - 1 $$ This is because one link is always treated as the **fixed frame**, leaving **(n−1)** links that can be fixed in turn to obtain distinct mechanisms. > [!NOTE] > **Key Concept:** A chain and its inversions have the **same number of links and the same type of joints**. The only thing that changes is *which* link is treated as the stationary frame. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Think of a **chain of paper clips** linked together on your desk. The entire chain has a certain shape and structure. Now imagine: - If you **pin the first paper clip to the table**, the rest of the chain swings around it like a pendulum. - If you **pin the middle paper clip down**, the two outer ends can move in completely different patterns. - If you **pin the last paper clip**, yet another motion pattern emerges. The chain of paper clips hasn't changed — it's still the same number of clips connected the same way.
