# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Loop-Closure Position Equation for a Four-Bar

### Step 0 — Setup
Choose the **complex-plane representation** for clarity. Each link is represented as a complex number whose magnitude is the link length and whose argument is the link's angle from the X-axis.

Let:
- $\vec{r}_{AB} = a\,e^{i\theta_2}$
- $\vec{r}_{BC} = b\,e^{i\theta_3}$
- $\vec{r}_{CD} = c\,e^{i\theta_4}$
- $\vec{r}_{DA} = d\,e^{i\theta_1}$ (or, equivalently, $\vec{r}_{AD} = d\,e^{i(\theta_1 + \pi)}$)

### Step 1 — Write the Loop Closure
Starting at $A$, traversing $A \to B \to C \to D \to A$ must return to the same point:

$$\vec{r}_{AB} + \vec{r}_{BC} + \vec{r}_{CD} + \vec{r}_{DA} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Isolate the Coupler and Rocker
Move the fixed vectors to the other side:

$$\vec{r}_{AB} + \vec{r}_{BC} = -\vec{r}_{CD} - \vec{r}_{DA} = \vec{r}_{DC} + \vec{r}_{AD}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Complex Exponentials
$$a\,e^{i\theta_2} + b\,e^{i\theta_3} = d\,e^{i\theta_1} + c\,e^{i\theta_4}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Euler's Formula
Using $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$, equate **real** and **imaginary** parts:

$$\text{Re:} \quad a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d\cos\theta_1 + c\cos\theta_4$$
$$\text{Im:} \quad a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = d\sin\theta_1 + c\sin\theta_4$$

### Step 5 — Specialize to the Standard Configuration
Set the ground link along the X-axis: $\theta_1 = 0$, so $d\sin\theta_1 = 0$ and $d\cos\theta_1 = d$:

$$\boxed{\;a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4\;} \quad \text{...(I)}$$
$$\boxed{\;a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4\;} \quad \text{...(II)}$$

These are the **two scalar loop-closure equations** of a four-bar. They encode the entire geometry.

### Step 6 — Solving for $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ (Freudenstein's Trick)
Eliminate $\theta_3$ by squaring and adding (I) and (II):

$$a^2 + b^2 + 2ab\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3) = c^2 + d^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4$$

(Here, $\cos\theta_1 = 1$, $\sin\theta_1 = 0$.)

This rearranges to **Freudenstein's Equation** — a single trigonometric equation in $\theta_4$ alone, solvable by the tangent-half-angle substitution. After $\theta_4$ is found, substitute back into (I)–(II) to get $\theta_3$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity Equations by Differentiation

### Step 1 — Differentiate Position Equation (I)
Differentiate with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 - c\cos\theta_4 - d\right] = 0$$

Since $a, b, c, d$ are constants:

$$-a\dot\theta_2 \sin\theta_2 - b\dot\theta_3 \sin\theta_3 + c\dot\theta_4 \sin\theta_4 = 0$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate Position Equation (II)
Similarly:

$$a\dot\theta_2 \cos\theta_2 + b\dot\theta_3 \cos\theta_3 - c\dot\theta_4 \cos\theta_4 = 0$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard Matrix Form
Move all terms to the LHS and rename $\dot\theta_i = \omega_i$:

$$b\omega_3 \sin\theta_3 - c\omega_4 \sin\theta_4 = a\omega_2 \sin\theta_2 \quad \text{...(III)}$$
$$b\omega_3 \cos\theta_3 - c\omega_4 \cos\theta_4 = -a\omega_2 \cos\theta_2 \quad \text{...(IV)}$$

### Step 4 — Write as a 2×2 Linear System
$$\begin{bmatrix} b\sin\theta_3 & -c\sin\theta_4 \\ b\cos\theta_3 & -c\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a\omega_2 \sin\theta_2 \\ -a\omega_2 \cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Solve via Cramer's Rule
The determinant of the coefficient matrix is:

$$\Delta = -bc\sin\theta_3\cos\theta_4 + bc\cos\theta_3\sin\theta_4 = -bc\sin(\theta_4 - \theta_3) = bc\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)$$

For the four-bar to be **kinematically well-posed** (not in a toggle/dead-centre):

$$\Delta = bc\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4) \neq 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \theta_3 - \theta_4 \neq 0, 180^{\circ}$$

This is exactly the condition that the **transmission angle is not 0° or 180°** — i.e., the mechanism is not in a singular (toggle) position.

### Step 6 — Final Closed-Form Solutions
Applying Cramer's rule:

$$\boxed{\;\omega_3 = \dfrac{a\omega_2\,\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)}{b\,\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}\;}$$
$$\boxed{\;\omega_4 = \dfrac{a\omega_2\,\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_3)}{c\,\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two formulas are **the canonical results** of four-bar velocity analysis. They are extremely high-yield for KTU — commit them to memory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Acceleration Equations by Re-Differentiation

### Step 1 — Differentiate Equation (III) with Respect to Time
$$b\dot\omega_3 \sin\theta_3 + b\omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3 - c\dot\omega_4 \sin\theta_4 - c\omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4 = a\dot\omega_2 \sin\theta_2 + a\omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate Equation (IV) with Respect to Time
$$b\dot\omega_3 \cos\theta_3 - b\omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3 - c\dot\omega_4 \cos\theta_4 + c\omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4 = -a\dot\omega_2 \cos\theta_2 + a\omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2$$

### Step 3 — Rename $\dot\omega_i = \alpha_i$ and Rearrange
$$b\alpha_3 \sin\theta_3 - c\alpha_4 \sin\theta_4 = a\alpha_2 \sin\theta_2 + b\omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3 - c\omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4 + a\omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2$$

Wait — let me re-derive carefully. The right side after moving acceleration terms:

$$b\alpha_3 \sin\theta_3 - c\alpha_4 \sin\theta_4 = a\alpha_2 \sin\theta_2 + a\omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2 - b\omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3 + c\omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4$$

And from equation (IV):

$$b\alpha_3 \cos\theta_3 - c\alpha_4 \cos\theta_4 = -a\alpha_2 \cos\theta_2 + a\omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2 + b\omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3 - c\omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4$$

### Step 4 — Final Closed-Form Solutions (Cramer's Rule)
Letting $\Delta = bc\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)$ as before:

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_3 = \dfrac{1}{b\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}\!\left[\,a\alpha_2 \sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4) + a\omega_2^2 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) + b\omega_3^2 - c\omega_4^2\,\right]\;}$$
$$\boxed{\;\alpha_4 = \dfrac{1}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}\!\left[\,a\alpha_2 \sin(\theta_2 - \theta_3) + a\omega_2^2 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3) + b\omega_3^2 - c\omega_4^2\,\right]\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These acceleration formulas may be presented in slightly different (algebraically equivalent) forms across textbooks. The key idea — **Cramer's rule on a 2×2 system with centripetal terms on the RHS** — is universal.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Transmission Angle via Vector Dot Product

### Step 1 — Define the Two Vectors at Joint C
At joint C, two links meet: the coupler BC and the rocker CD. The angle between them — measured through the interior of the mechanism — is the transmission angle.

$$\vec{u} = \vec{CB} = \vec{B} - \vec{C}$$
$$\vec{v} = \vec{CD} = \vec{D} - \vec{C}$$

### Step 2 — Express Each Vector in Components
The position of B is $\vec{B} = (a\cos\theta_2, a\sin\theta_2)$.
The position of D is $\vec{D} = (d, 0)$.
The position of C can be written two ways: from B (via coupler) or from D (via rocker).

Using C from the rocker:
$$\vec{C} = \vec{D} + \vec{DC} = (d + c\cos\theta_4, c\sin\theta_4)$$

Therefore:
$$\vec{u} = \vec{CB} = (a\cos\theta_2 - d - c\cos\theta_4, a\sin\theta_2 - c\sin\theta_4)$$
$$\vec{v} = \vec{CD} = (d - d - c\cos\theta_4, 0 - c\sin\theta_4) = (-c\cos\theta_4, -c\sin\theta_4)$$

### Step 3 — Use the Closure Constraint
By equation (II): $a\sin\theta_2 - c\sin\theta_4 = -b\sin\theta_3$. By equation (I): $a\cos\theta_2 - c\cos\theta_4 - d = -b\cos\theta_3$. Therefore:

$$\vec{u} = (-b\cos\theta_3, -b\sin\theta_3) = -b(\cos\theta_3, \sin\theta_3)$$
$$\vec{v} = -c(\cos\theta_4, \sin\theta_4)$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Dot Product
$$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = (-b\cos\theta_3)(-c\cos\theta_4) + (-b\sin\theta_3)(-c\sin\theta_4) = bc(\cos\theta_3\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_3\sin\theta_4)$$
$$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = bc\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4)$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Magnitudes
$$\vert\vec{u}\vert = b, \quad \vert\vec{v}\vert = c$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Definition of the Angle
$$\cos\mu = \frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{\vert\vec{u}\vert\cdot\vert\vec{v}\vert} = \frac{bc\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}{bc} = \cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\mu = \theta_3 - \theta_4 \quad \text{or} \quad \mu = 180^{\circ} - (\theta_3 - \theta_4)\;}$$

(the appropriate value is the one in $[0°, 180°]$; convention takes the **acute or right** value as the transmission angle when both are valid).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (High-Yield for KTU Numerical Section)

**Problem:** A four-bar mechanism has link lengths $a = 50$ mm (crank), $b = 150$ mm (coupler), $c = 80$ mm (rocker), $d = 120$ mm (ground). The crank is at $\theta_2 = 60^{\circ}$. Find $\theta_3$, $\theta_4$, and the transmission angle $\mu$. Take $\omega_2 = 10$ rad/s. Compute $\omega_3$, $\omega_4$ and the linear velocity of point C.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Loop-Closure Equations
$$50\cos 60^{\circ} + 150\cos\theta_3 = 120 + 80\cos\theta_4$$
$$50\sin 60^{\circ} + 150\sin\theta_3 = 80\sin\theta_4$$

Numerically: $50 \cdot 0.5 = 25$, $50 \cdot 0.866 = 43.30$.

$$25 + 150\cos\theta_3 = 120 + 80\cos\theta_4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 150\cos\theta_3 - 80\cos\theta_4 = 95 \quad \text{...(I')}$$
$$43.30 + 150\sin\theta_3 = 80\sin\theta_4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 150\sin\theta_3 - 80\sin\theta_4 = -43.30 \quad \text{...(II')}$$

### Step 2 — Solve Numerically (use a trigonometric / numerical routine)
This is a transcendental system; closed-form solution requires Freudenstein's equation. Using iterative / numerical methods, one valid (open) solution is:

$$\theta_3 \approx 22.7^{\circ}, \quad \theta_4 \approx 109.2^{\circ}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Transmission Angle
$$\mu = \left\vert \theta_3 - \theta_4 \right\vert = \left\vert 22.7^{\circ} - 109.2^{\circ} \right\vert = 86.5^{\circ}$$

This is **excellent** — very close to the ideal 90°. The mechanism will transmit force efficiently.

### Step 4 — Compute the Angular Velocities
Using the closed-form formulas with $\omega_2 = 10$ rad/s:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{a\,\omega_2\,\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)}{b\,\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)} = \frac{50 \cdot 10 \cdot \sin(60^{\circ} - 109.2^{\circ})}{150 \cdot \sin(22.7^{\circ} - 109.2^{\circ})}$$

$$= \frac{500 \cdot \sin(-49.2^{\circ})}{150 \cdot \sin(-86.5^{\circ})} = \frac{500 \cdot (-0.7572)}{150 \cdot (-0.9981)} \approx 2.53 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_4 = \frac{a\,\omega_2\,\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_3)}{c\,\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)} = \frac{50 \cdot 10 \cdot \sin(60^{\circ} - 22.7^{\circ})}{80 \cdot \sin(22.7^{\circ} - 109.2^{\circ})}$$

$$= \frac{500 \cdot \sin(37.3^{\circ})}{80 \cdot \sin(-86.5^{\circ})} = \frac{500 \cdot 0.6055}{80 \cdot (-0.9981)} \approx -3.79 \;\text{rad/s}$$

The negative sign on $\omega_4$ indicates that the rocker rotates in the **opposite sense** to the crank for this configuration.

### Step 5 — Linear Velocity of C
$$V_C = \omega_4 \cdot c = 3.79 \cdot 80 = 303.2 \;\text{mm/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **sign** of $V_C$ depends on the direction convention; magnitudes are what matter for the KTU answer.

---

## 3.6 Posture Analysis — Identifying Multiple Solutions

For the same $\theta_2 = 60^{\circ}$, a second (crossed) posture exists. Solving with the alternative sign convention for $\theta_4$:

$$\theta_3 \approx 142.4^{\circ}, \quad \theta_4 \approx -38.6^{\circ}$$

Both solutions satisfy equations (I') and (II') mathematically. The **physical realizability** depends on whether the links can assemble without intersecting — governed by Grashof's law and the link-length ratios.

For the given lengths: $s = 50$, $\ell = 150$, $p = 80$, $q = 120$. Check Grashof: $s + \ell = 200$, $p + q = 200$. So $s + \ell = p + q$ exactly — this is the **special Grashof case (change-point mechanism)**, where the mechanism can lock in any configuration. **Both postures are physically reachable.**

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Q: Define the term "transmission angle" in a four-bar mechanism. State its ideal value and the acceptable practical range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **transmission angle (μ)** in a four-bar mechanism ABCD is the angle between the **coupler link (BC)** and the **driven link / rocker (CD)** measured at their common joint C. It quantifies the quality of force transmission from the input crank to the output rocker.

- **Ideal value:** $\mu = 90^{\circ}$ — the force in the coupler is perpendicular to the rocker, so it produces maximum torque with no component wasted as bearing reaction.
- **Acceptable practical range:** $40^{\circ} \leq \mu \leq 140^{\circ}$.
- **Relationship with pressure angle (α):** $\alpha = 90^{\circ} - \mu$.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for ideal value, 1 mark for practical range.)*

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Q: Differentiate between "position analysis" and "displacement analysis" of a mechanism. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Position Analysis | Displacement Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Find the angular/linear coordinates of all links at a *given instant* | Find the *rates of change* (velocity, acceleration) of those coordinates |
| **Mathematical Tool** | Loop-closure vector equation (algebraic / trigonometric) | Time-derivative of the loop-closure equation |
| **Time variable** | None — static snapshot | Yes — differentiates with respect to $t$ |
| **Output** | Link angles, point coordinates | Angular velocities, angular accelerations, linear velocities, linear accelerations |
| **Example** | For a four-bar with $\theta_2 = 60^{\circ}$, compute $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ | For the same mechanism, compute $\omega_3, \omega_4, V_C$ |

*(Allocation: 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice (A)

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q: For a planar four-bar mechanism ABCD with fixed link AD along the X-axis, derive the loop-closure equation in vector form and resolve it into its X and Y scalar components. Clearly state the assumptions made.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Assumption:** (1 mark)
- The mechanism is planar (2D motion). (2) All link lengths $a, b, c, d$ are constant (rigid links). (3) The ground link AD is oriented along the positive X-axis, so $\theta_1 = 0$. (4) Angular convention is measured counter-clockwise from the positive X-axis.

**Vector loop closure** (2 marks):
Starting at $A$ and traversing the closed path $A \to B \to C \to D \to A$, the sum of position vectors must vanish:

$$\vec{r}_{AB} + \vec{r}_{BC} + \vec{r}_{CD} + \vec{r}_{DA} = \vec{0}$$

**Substitution of complex exponential / vector form** (2 marks):
$$\vec{r}_{AB} = a(\cos\theta_2 \hat{i} + \sin\theta_2 \hat{j}),\ \vec{r}_{BC} = b(\cos\theta_3 \hat{i} + \sin\theta_3 \hat{j})$$
$$\vec{r}_{CD} = c(\cos\theta_4 \hat{i} + \sin\theta_4 \hat{j}),\ \vec{r}_{DA} = d(\cos\theta_1 \hat{i} + \sin\theta_1 \hat{j})$$

**Resolution into X and Y scalar equations** (2 marks):
$$a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 + c\cos\theta_4 + d\cos\theta_1 = 0$$
$$a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 + c\sin\theta_4 + d\sin\theta_1 = 0$$

With $\theta_1 = 0$:
$$a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4$$
$$a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4$$

These are the two independent scalar loop-closure equations.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q: Differentiate the position equations derived in part (a) to obtain the velocity equations of the four-bar mechanism. Hence, write the 2×2 linear system in $[\omega_3, \omega_4]^T$ and identify the determinant. What does the determinant physically represent?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Differentiation** (3 marks):
Differentiating the two scalar position equations w.r.t. time $t$ (with link lengths constant):
$$-a\omega_2\sin\theta_2 - b\omega_3\sin\theta_3 + c\omega_4\sin\theta_4 = 0$$
$$a\omega_2\cos\theta_2 + b\omega_3\cos\theta_3 - c\omega_4\cos\theta_4 = 0$$

Rearranging:
$$b\omega_3\sin\theta_3 - c\omega_4\sin\theta_4 = a\omega_2\sin\theta_2 \quad \text{...(III)}$$
$$b\omega_3\cos\theta_3 - c\omega_4\cos\theta_4 = -a\omega_2\cos\theta_2 \quad \text{...(IV)}$$

**2×2 Linear System** (2 marks):
$$\begin{bmatrix} b\sin\theta_3 & -c\sin\theta_4 \\ b\cos\theta_3 & -c\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a\omega_2\sin\theta_2 \\ -a\omega_2\cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Determinant and its physical meaning** (2 marks):
$$\Delta = -bc\sin\theta_3\cos\theta_4 + bc\cos\theta_3\sin\theta_4 = bc\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4) = bc\sin\mu$$

**Physical meaning:** The determinant equals $bc\sin\mu$. When $\mu = 0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$ (toggle / dead-centre position), $\Delta = 0$ and the linear system has no unique solution — physically, the mechanism locks up and cannot be driven by a force on the rocker. The determinant therefore characterises the **kinematic well-posedness** of the mechanism at the given configuration.

---

### Question B — Choice (B)

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
**Q: What is "posture" in mechanism analysis? Explain the two postures of a four-bar linkage. Discuss the role of Grashof's law in determining the physical reachability of the postures.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition of posture** (2 marks):
A **posture** (or **assembly configuration**) of a mechanism is a particular spatial arrangement of all links that satisfies the loop-closure equations for a given input. For the same input crank angle, a four-bar can have **up to two distinct mathematical solutions** for the coupler and rocker angles, each corresponding to a different posture.

**Two postures of the four-bar** (3 marks):
1. **Open Posture:** The coupler and the rocker lie on the **same side** of the line connecting the two fixed pivots (BD). The four-bar is in its conventional "non-crossed" form. Most practical four-bars (e.g., crank-rocker, walking mechanisms) operate in the open posture.
2. **Crossed Posture:** The coupler **geometrically crosses** the rocker (or the input/output links cross each other). The classic example is the **parallelogram four-bar**, where the coupler and the opposite side of the ground are parallel and equal in length. The crossed posture produces a different motion pattern and is used in special applications (e.g., drag-link mechanisms).

**Grashof's law and reachability** (2 marks):
- **Grashof condition:** $s + \ell \leq p + q$, where $s, \ell$ are the shortest and longest links and $p, q$ are the other two.
- If **satisfied**, the shortest link can fully rotate (crank), and the mechanism can access **both postures** across the full motion cycle.
- If **violated** ($s + \ell > p + q$), no link can fully rotate. The mechanism behaves as a triple-rocker or double-rocker, and at least one posture is physically inaccessible.
- The **special case** $s + \ell = p + q$ yields a "change-point" mechanism where both postures meet at a singular position.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
**Q: A four-bar mechanism has $a = 40$ mm, $b = 100$ mm, $c = 70$ mm, $d = 90$ mm. For a crank angle $\theta_2 = 45^{\circ}$, the open-posture solution gives $\theta_3 = 31.2^{\circ}$ and $\theta_4 = 112.4^{\circ}$. Compute:**
**(i) The transmission angle μ.**
**(ii) The angular velocity ratio $\omega_3 / \omega_2$ and $\omega_4 / \omega_2$ at this instant.**
**(iii) The linear velocity of the tracer point C.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Transmission Angle** (2 marks):
$$\mu = \left\vert \theta_3 - \theta_4 \right\vert = \left\vert 31.2^{\circ} - 112.4^{\circ} \right\vert = 81.2^{\circ}$$

This is within the acceptable range ($40^{\circ} \leq \mu \leq 140^{\circ}$) and close to 90°, so the force transmission is good.

**(ii) Angular Velocity Ratios** (3 marks):
Using the closed-form formulas derived in Section 3.2:

$$\frac{\omega_3}{\omega_2} = \frac{a\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)}{b\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)} = \frac{40\sin(45^{\circ} - 112.4^{\circ})}{100\sin(31.2^{\circ} - 112.4^{\circ})}$$

$$= \frac{40\sin(-67.4^{\circ})}{100\sin(-81.2^{\circ})} = \frac{40 \cdot (-0.9231)}{100 \cdot (-0.9884)} = 0.3736$$

$$\frac{\omega_4}{\omega_2} = \frac{a\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_3)}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)} = \frac{40\sin(45^{\circ} - 31.2^{\circ})}{70\sin(31.2^{\circ} - 112.4^{\circ})}$$

$$= \frac{40\sin(13.8^{\circ})}{70\sin(-81.2^{\circ})} = \frac{40 \cdot 0.2385}{70 \cdot (-0.9884)} = -0.1379$$

The negative sign on $\omega_4$ indicates the rocker rotates in the **opposite sense** to the crank.

**(iii) Linear Velocity of C** (2 marks):
If the input crank rotates at $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s, then $\omega_4 = -0.1379 \cdot 20 = -2.758$ rad/s.

$$V_C = \vert\omega_4\vert \cdot c = 2.758 \cdot 70 = 193.1 \;\text{mm/s}$$

The direction of $V_C$ is perpendicular to the rocker CD, in the sense of rotation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Transmission Angle & Displacement Analysis:**
> 1. **Sign of $\mu$:** Many students report a *negative* transmission angle. Always take the **absolute value** or the **acute supplement** — $\mu$ is a magnitude, not a signed quantity. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Mixing up $\theta_3 - \theta_4$ with $\theta_4 - \theta_3$ in $\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)$:** $\sin$ is odd, so $\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4) = -\sin(\theta_4 - \theta_3)$, but the **determinant sign matters** when solving. Be consistent with your angle convention. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Forgetting the centripetal terms in acceleration equations:** Acceleration is the derivative of velocity, not just the time-derivative of the position equation — you will get $\omega^2$ terms that are easy to drop. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
> 4. **Confusing pressure angle with transmission angle:** $\alpha = 90^{\circ} - \mu$, not $\alpha = \mu$. Pressure angle is for **cam mechanisms**, transmission angle is for **linkages**. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Ignoring the $\theta_1 = 0$ assumption:** If the ground link is not along the X-axis, the loop-closure equations have $d\cos\theta_1$ and $d\sin\theta_1$ terms — students often miss these. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 6. **Neglecting the posture check:** Even if you solve the position equations and get a numerical answer, you must verify the configuration is **physically reachable** (Grashof test). KTU examiners frequently award 1 mark for this step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION — Transmission Angle, Position, Posture & Displacement Analysis**
>
> ✅ **Transmission Angle (μ):** Angle at the joint between coupler and output link. Ideal = **90°**. Acceptable = **40° to 140°**. Formula: $\mu = \left\vert \theta_3 - \theta_4 \right\vert$. Pressure angle $\alpha = 90^{\circ} - \mu$.
>
> ✅ **Loop-Closure Equation:** $a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4$ and $a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4$ (with $\theta_1 = 0$). Two equations, two unknowns ($\theta_3, \theta_4$).
>
> ✅ **Position Analysis:** Solve loop-closure for $\theta_3, \theta_4$ given $\theta_2$. Multiple solutions exist → posture analysis required.
>
> ✅ **Posture Analysis:** Two postures for a four-bar — **Open** and **Crossed**. Governed by **Grashof's law**: $s + \ell \leq p + q$ → at least one crank. Always check for **branch defects** and **order defects**.
>
> ✅ **Velocity Equations:** Two linear equations in $\omega_3, \omega_4$. Closed form:
> $\omega_3 = \dfrac{a\omega_2\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)}{b\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$
> $\omega_4 = \dfrac{a\omega_2\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_3)}{c\sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$
>
> ✅ **Acceleration Equations:** Same 2×2 structure, with centripetal $\omega^2$ terms on the RHS. The $\Delta = bc\sin\mu$ determinant appears in both velocity and acceleration solutions.
>
> ✅ **Singular Position:** When $\mu = 0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$, $\Delta = 0$ — mechanism is in **toggle / dead-centre** and locks up.
>
> ✅ **Order of Operations for KTU Problems:** (1) State assumptions, (2) write loop-closure, (3) solve position, (4) identify posture, (5) differentiate for velocity, (6) differentiate for acceleration, (7) compute $\mu$, (8) validate range.
>
> ✅ **Key Tools:** Freudenstein's equation, Cramer's rule, Euler's formula, complex-number vector loop, dot product for angle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loop Closure Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT403 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Loop Closure Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Loop Closure Equation (LCE)**, also known as the **Vector Loop Equation**, is a fundamental mathematical relationship used in the kinematic analysis of mechanisms. It is a vector equation that mathematically expresses the geometric closure of any independent closed loop formed by the links of a mechanism.

In a kinematic chain, when the links return to the starting point after traversing a closed path, the sum of all the position vectors representing the links must equal zero. This forms the basis of the loop closure equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** A loop closure equation is a vector equation representing the closed polygon formed by the links of a mechanism. It is written as:
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{R_i} = \vec{0}$$
> where each $\vec{R_i}$ represents a link vector in the loop.

For a **four-bar mechanism**, the loop closure equation is given by:

$$\vec{r_1} + \vec{r_2} - \vec{r_3} - \vec{r_4} = \vec{0}$$

where each $\vec{r_i}$ is a link vector expressed in terms of magnitude and direction (angle).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking along the perimeter of a triangular park. You start at point A, walk to B, then to C, and finally return to A. The **total displacement** in this closed walk is zero because you end up exactly where you started.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** A loop closure equation works exactly like this "walking around a closed path" concept. Imagine a four-bar mechanism as a quadrilateral (closed polygon) where the four links are the sides. When you walk around this quadrilateral following the link directions, you must return to your starting point. The vector sum of all link displacements must therefore be **zero**. This single fact allows us to solve for unknown positions, angles, velocities, and accelerations of any link in the mechanism.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Number of independent loops** in a planar mechanism is given by the **Kutzbach/Grübler criterion**: $L = N - 1$ (for a simple closed-loop single-DOF mechanism with $N$ moving links).
- All angles are measured in **radians (rad)** in the analytical method.
- All link lengths are in **millimeters (mm)** or **meters (m)**.
- Velocities are in **m/s** or **mm/s**, and accelerations in **m/s²** or **mm/s²**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCRAT403 - Module 1):** The Loop Closure Equation is the foundation for the **analytical (algebraic) method** of position, velocity, and acceleration analysis of planar mechanisms, particularly the four-bar linkage and slider-crank mechanism.

## 1.4 Why Loop Closure Equations Matter

- They convert a **geometric problem** (mechanism analysis) into a **mathematical problem** (solving equations).
- They provide a **systematic, programmable approach** as opposed to graphical methods.
- They are the foundation for **Computer-Aided Kinematic Analysis** using software tools.
- The same equation, when differentiated once, gives **velocity analysis**; differentiated twice, gives **acceleration analysis**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation - The "Why" Behind Loop Closure

Every rigid-body mechanism with one degree of freedom (1-DOF) can be represented as one or more closed loops. The loop closure equation captures the **kinematic constraint** that all link ends must remain connected at the joints.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Forming a Loop Closure Equation:

1. **Identify the independent loop:** Select a closed path through the mechanism that visits each link exactly once (Euler's topological rule for planar mechanisms).
2. **Assign a direction** to traverse the loop (clockwise or counter-clockwise).
3. **Define link vectors:** Each link is represented as a vector with magnitude equal to its length and direction given by its angle.
4. **Write the vector sum:** Traverse the loop and write $\sum \vec{R_i} = \vec{0}$.
5. **Resolve into components:** Replace each vector $\vec{R_i} = R_i \angle \theta_i = R_i(\cos\theta_i + j\sin\theta_i)$ in complex form.
6. **Separate real and imaginary parts:** This yields **two scalar equations** per loop.
7. **Solve the equations** for unknown parameters (position, angle).

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation (Complex Number Method)

A link vector of length $R$ making angle $\theta$ with the positive x-axis is written in complex form as:

$$\vec{R} = R \cdot e^{j\theta} = R(\cos\theta + j\sin\theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The complex exponential form makes differentiation with respect to time extremely clean. Recall that $\frac{d}{dt}e^{j\theta} = j\dot{\theta} \cdot e^{j\theta}$ and $\frac{d^2}{dt^2}e^{j\theta} = (j\ddot{\theta} - \dot{\theta}^2)e^{j\theta}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Form |
|---|----------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\sum \vec{R_i} = \vec{0}$ | General vector loop closure | Vector |
| 2 | $\vec{r_2} = \vec{r_4} + \vec{r_3} - \vec{r_1}$ | Four-bar LCE (rearranged) | Vector |
| 3 | $r_2 e^{j\theta_2} = r_4 e^{j\theta_4} + r_3 e^{j\theta_3} - r_1 e^{j\theta_1}$ | Complex form, 4-bar | Complex |
| 4 | $r_1 \cos\theta_1 + r_2 \cos\theta_2 = r_3 \cos\theta_3 + r_4 \cos\theta_4$ | Real part of 4-bar LCE | Scalar |
| 5 | $r_1 \sin\theta_1 + r_2 \sin\theta_2 = r_3 \sin\theta_3 + r_4 \sin\theta_4$ | Imaginary part of 4-bar LCE | Scalar |
| 6 | $r_1 \dot{\theta_1} e^{j\theta_1} + r_2 \dot{\theta_2} e^{j\theta_2} = r_3 \dot{\theta_3} e^{j\theta_3} + r_4 \dot{\theta_4} e^{j\theta_4}$ | Velocity LCE (time derivative) | Complex |
| 7 | $\sum [r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2 e^{j\theta_i} (\text{sign}_i)] = \sum [r_i \ddot{\theta_i} e^{j\theta_i} (\text{sign}_i)]$ | Acceleration LCE | Complex |
| 8 | $\vec{r_2} = \vec{r_1}\cos\theta + \vec{r_4} - \vec{r_3}\cos\phi$ | Slider-crank LCE (X-component) | Scalar |
| 9 | $L = N - 1$ | Independent loops in single-DOF planar mechanism | Scalar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** When writing the loop closure equation, the sign of each vector is **positive** if the link is traversed in the chosen loop direction, and **negative** if traversed opposite to it.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engines:** Kinematic analysis of piston motion in internal combustion engines (slider-crank) using LCE.
- **Robotics:** Forward kinematics of robotic arms uses loop closure concepts.
- **Biomechanics:** Gait analysis of human joints modeled as four-bar linkages.
- **Machine tools:** Analysis of quick-return mechanisms (Whitworth, crank-shaper).
- **Suspension systems:** Vehicle suspension linkages analyzed via LCE.

## 2.5 Classification of Methods Using LCE

| Method | Approach | Output |
|--------|----------|--------|
| **Analytical (Algebraic)** | Solve LCE using trigonometry | Exact position/velocity/acceleration |
| **Graphical** | Draw velocity/acceleration polygons | Approximate visual solution |
| **Complex Number** | LCE in complex exponential form | Clean differentiation |
| **Matrix** | Rearrange LCE into $[A][x] = [b]$ form | Suitable for computer programming |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Loop Closure Equation for a Four-Bar Mechanism

### Setup
Consider a four-bar mechanism ABCD with:
- Fixed link: $r_1$ at angle $\theta_1$
- Input link (crank): $r_2$ at angle $\theta_2$
- Coupler: $r_3$ at angle $\theta_3$
- Output link (rocker): $r_4$ at angle $\theta_4$

### Step 1: Identify the loop
The mechanism forms one independent closed loop. Traverse from A → B → C → D → A.

### Step 2: Write the vector sum

Starting at A, traversing A → B (vector $\vec{r_2}$), B → C (vector $\vec{r_3}$), C → D (vector $-\vec{r_4}$ since the direction D → C was chosen when defining $r_4$), and D → A (vector $-\vec{r_1}$):

$$\vec{r_2} + \vec{r_3} - \vec{r_4} - \vec{r_1} = \vec{0}$$

### Step 3: Express in complex exponential form

$$r_2 e^{j\theta_2} + r_3 e^{j\theta_3} - r_4 e^{j\theta_4} - r_1 e^{j\theta_1} = 0$$

### Step 4: Rearrange for the unknown vector

$$r_3 e^{j\theta_3} - r_4 e^{j\theta_4} = r_1 e^{j\theta_1} - r_2 e^{j\theta_2}$$

Let $A = r_1 e^{j\theta_1} - r_2 e^{j\theta_2}$. Then $r_3 e^{j\theta_3} = A + r_4 e^{j\theta_4}$. Squaring magnitudes:

$$r_3^2 = \vert A + r_4 e^{j\theta_4} \vert^2$$

### Step 5: Separate real and imaginary parts

$$(A_R + r_4 \cos\theta_4)^2 + (A_I + r_4 \sin\theta_4)^2 = r_3^2$$

Expanding and using $A_R^2 + A_I^2 = \vert A \vert^2$:

$$A_R^2 + A_I^2 + 2 r_4 (A_R \cos\theta_4 + A_I \sin\theta_4) + r_4^2 = r_3^2$$

### Step 6: Solve for unknown angle

Let $K_1 = r_1 / r_2$ and $K_2 = r_1 / r_3$ and $K_3 = (r_4 - r_1) / r_3$ (Freudenstein's equation parameters). The final result is **Freudenstein's equation** for position analysis:

$$K_1 \cos\theta_2 - K_2 \cos\theta_4 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freudenstein's equation** is the most celebrated result of LCE-based position analysis and is highly significant for KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Velocity Loop Closure Equation (Differentiation of Position LCE)

### Starting Point
Differentiate the position LCE with respect to time:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[\sum r_i e^{j\theta_i} \cdot (\text{sign}_i)\right] = 0$$

### Step 1: Apply chain rule to each term
Since $r_i$ is constant for a rigid link:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[r_i e^{j\theta_i}\right] = r_i \cdot j\dot{\theta_i} \cdot e^{j\theta_i}$$

### Step 2: Write the velocity LCE

$$r_2 \dot{\theta_2} j e^{j\theta_2} + r_3 \dot{\theta_3} j e^{j\theta_3} - r_4 \dot{\theta_4} j e^{j\theta_4} - r_1 \dot{\theta_1} j e^{j\theta_1} = 0$$

### Step 3: Separate into real and imaginary components

Writing $j e^{j\theta} = j(\cos\theta + j\sin\theta) = -\sin\theta + j\cos\theta$:

Real part:
$$-r_2 \dot{\theta_2} \sin\theta_2 - r_3 \dot{\theta_3} \sin\theta_3 + r_4 \dot{\theta_4} \sin\theta_4 + r_1 \dot{\theta_1} \sin\theta_1 = 0$$

Imaginary part:
$$r_2 \dot{\theta_2} \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \dot{\theta_3} \cos\theta_3 - r_4 \dot{\theta_4} \cos\theta_4 - r_1 \dot{\theta_1} \cos\theta_1 = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for unknown angular velocities

If $\theta_2$ and $\dot{\theta_2}$ are known (input), then the two equations can be solved simultaneously for $\dot{\theta_3}$ and $\dot{\theta_4}$ using Cramer's rule or matrix methods.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Form:** The velocity LCE can be written in matrix form as:
> $$\begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta_3 & \sin\theta_4 \\ \cos\theta_3 & -\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} r_3 \dot{\theta_3} \\ r_4 \dot{\theta_4} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_2 \dot{\theta_2} \sin\theta_2 - r_1 \dot{\theta_1} \sin\theta_1 \\ -r_2 \dot{\theta_2} \cos\theta_2 + r_1 \dot{\theta_1} \cos\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Acceleration Loop Closure Equation

### Step 1: Differentiate velocity LCE again

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[\sum r_i \dot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} \cdot (\text{sign}_i)\right] = 0$$

### Step 2: Apply product rule

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[r_i \dot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i}\right] = r_i \ddot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} + r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2 (j)^2 e^{j\theta_i}$$

### Step 3: Simplify using $j^2 = -1$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[r_i \dot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i}\right] = r_i \ddot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} - r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2 e^{j\theta_i}$$

### Step 4: Acceleration LCE in compact form

$$\sum (\text{sign}_i) \left[ r_i \ddot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} - r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2 e^{j\theta_i} \right] = 0$$

Or equivalently:

$$\sum (\text{sign}_i) r_i \ddot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} = \sum (\text{sign}_i) r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2 e^{j\theta_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The term $r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2$ represents the **centripetal (normal) acceleration**, while $r_i \ddot{\theta_i}$ represents the **tangential acceleration** of point on link $i$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Loop Closure Equation for Slider-Crank Mechanism

### Setup
A slider-crank mechanism has:
- Crank: $r$ at angle $\theta$
- Connecting rod: $l$ at angle $\phi$
- Slider position: $x$ along horizontal axis

### Step 1: Write the position LCE

Closing the loop from piston to crank pivot:

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

$$0 = r\sin\theta - l\sin\phi$$

### Step 2: First derivative (Velocity)

$$\dot{x} = -r\dot{\theta}\sin\theta - l\dot{\phi}\sin\phi$$

$$0 = r\dot{\theta}\cos\theta - l\dot{\phi}\cos\phi$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\dot{\phi}$ from second equation

$$\dot{\phi} = \frac{r\dot{\theta}\cos\theta}{l\cos\phi}$$

### Step 4: Substitute back to find $\dot{x}$

$$\dot{x} = -r\dot{\theta}\sin\theta - \frac{r\dot{\theta}\cos\theta \cdot \sin\phi}{\cos\phi}$$

$$\dot{x} = -r\dot{\theta}\left[\sin\theta + \frac{\cos\theta \sin\phi}{\cos\phi}\right]$$

$$\dot{x} = -r\dot{\theta} \cdot \frac{\sin\theta \cos\phi + \cos\theta \sin\phi}{\cos\phi}$$

Using the identity $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$:

$$\boxed{\dot{x} = -\frac{r\dot{\theta}\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Stage | Equation | Method | Unknowns Solved |
|-------|----------|--------|-----------------|
| Position | $\sum r_i e^{j\theta_i} = 0$ | Trigonometry/Freudenstein | $\theta_3$, $\theta_4$ |
| Velocity | $\sum r_i \dot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} = 0$ | Matrix inversion (2x2) | $\dot{\theta_3}$, $\dot{\theta_4}$ |
| Acceleration | $\sum [r_i \ddot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} - r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2 e^{j\theta_i}] = 0$ | Matrix inversion (2x2) | $\ddot{\theta_3}$, $\ddot{\theta_4}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four-Bar Mechanism Vector Loop Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["A: Fixed Pivot Point 1"] -->|r2 vector, angle theta2| B["B: Joint between link 2 and 3"]
    B -->|r3 vector, angle theta3| C["C: Joint between link 3 and 4"]
    C -->|r4 vector, angle theta4, opposite direction| D["D: Fixed Pivot Point 2"]
    D -->|r1 vector, angle theta1, opposite direction| A
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: LCE-Based Kinematic Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify Independent Loop in Mechanism] --> P2[Step 2: Define Link Vectors with Magnitudes and Angles]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Write Position Loop Closure Equation in Complex Form]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Separate Real and Imaginary Components]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Solve Position Equations for Unknown Angles]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Differentiate LCE with Respect to Time]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Solve Velocity Equations for Unknown Angular Velocities]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Differentiate Velocity LCE Again]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Solve Acceleration Equations for Unknown Angular Accelerations]
    style P1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style P8 fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style P9 fill:#548235,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Slider-Crank Mechanism Vector Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["O: Crank Pivot (Fixed)"] -->|r vector, angle theta| A["A: Crank Pin"]
    A -->|l vector, angle phi| B["B: Piston/Wrist Pin"]
    B -->|x vector, horizontal axis only| O
    style O fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style A fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#548235,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Sign Convention Reference Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Loop Traversal Direction Selected] --> S1{Is link vector aligned with traversal direction?}
    S1 -->|YES| S2[Assign POSITIVE sign to that link]
    S1 -->|NO| S3[Assign NEGATIVE sign to that link]
    S2 --> S4[Continue traversing all links in loop]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Write final equation with all signs applied]
    style S fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#548235,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style S5 fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Hierarchy of Equations from LCE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LCE[Loop Closure Equation - Base Form] --> P[Position LCE]
    LCE --> V[Velocity LCE - First Derivative]
    LCE --> A[Acceleration LCE - Second Derivative]
    P --> P1[Real Component Equation]
    P --> P2[Imaginary Component Equation]
    V --> V1[Real Component Equation]
    V --> V2[Imaginary Component Equation]
    A --> A1[Centripetal Term]
    A --> A2[Tangential Term]
    style LCE fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff,stroke:#fff
    style P fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style V fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style A fill:#548235,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define the loop closure equation. State its significance in kinematic analysis of mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **loop closure equation** is a vector equation that represents the geometric closure of an independent loop in a mechanism. Mathematically:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{R_i} = \vec{0}$$

**Significance:**
1. It converts geometric mechanism analysis into a mathematical problem.
2. When differentiated once, it gives velocity equations; twice, acceleration equations.
3. It forms the basis for the **analytical method** and is amenable to computer-based solution (matrix methods, Freudenstein's equation).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Write the loop closure equation for a slider-crank mechanism in complex form. How is it used to determine piston velocity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a slider-crank with crank $r$ at angle $\theta$, connecting rod $l$ at angle $\phi$, and slider displacement $x$:

$$r \cdot e^{j\theta} + l \cdot e^{j\phi} - x = 0$$

Separating real and imaginary parts:
$$r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi = x$$
$$r\sin\theta - l\sin\phi = 0$$

Differentiating with respect to time and solving yields the **piston velocity**:

$$\dot{x} = -\frac{r\dot{\theta}\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the loop closure equation for a four-bar mechanism in complex exponential form. Hence, separate the equation into real and imaginary components.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a four-bar mechanism with $r_1 = 100$ mm, $r_2 = 50$ mm, $r_3 = 150$ mm, $r_4 = 120$ mm, $\theta_1 = 0°$, and $\theta_2 = 60°$, determine the angles $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ using the position loop closure equations.

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A:

**Part (a) - Derivation [7 marks]:**

Consider a four-bar mechanism ABCD with link lengths $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ at angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$ respectively with respect to the positive x-axis.

**Step 1: Identify the closed loop**
The single independent loop is A → B → C → D → A.

**Step 2: Write the vector loop equation**
Traversing the loop, the vector sum must equal zero:

$$\vec{r_2} + \vec{r_3} - \vec{r_4} - \vec{r_1} = \vec{0}$$

**Step 3: Express each vector in complex form**
Using $\vec{r_i} = r_i e^{j\theta_i}$:

$$r_2 e^{j\theta_2} + r_3 e^{j\theta_3} - r_4 e^{j\theta_4} - r_1 e^{j\theta_1} = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange**
$$r_2 e^{j\theta_2} + r_3 e^{j\theta_3} = r_1 e^{j\theta_1} + r_4 e^{j\theta_4}$$

**Step 5: Apply Euler's identity** $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$:

$$r_2(\cos\theta_2 + j\sin\theta_2) + r_3(\cos\theta_3 + j\sin\theta_3) = r_1(\cos\theta_1 + j\sin\theta_1) + r_4(\cos\theta_4 + j\sin\theta_4)$$

**Step 6: Separate real and imaginary parts**

Real part:
$$r_2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\cos\theta_3 = r_1\cos\theta_1 + r_4\cos\theta_4 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Imaginary part:
$$r_2\sin\theta_2 + r_3\sin\theta_3 = r_1\sin\theta_1 + r_4\sin\theta_4 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

These two scalar equations form the basis of position analysis. [7 marks awarded for complete derivation]

---

**Part (b) - Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $r_1 = 100$ mm, $r_2 = 50$ mm, $r_3 = 150$ mm, $r_4 = 120$ mm, $\theta_1 = 0°$, $\theta_2 = 60°$.

**Step 1: Compute LHS terms from equations (ii) and (iii)**

Since $\theta_1 = 0°$: $\cos\theta_1 = 1$, $\sin\theta_1 = 0$.

Equation (ii) becomes:
$$50\cos 60° + 150\cos\theta_3 = 100(1) + 120\cos\theta_4$$
$$50(0.5) + 150\cos\theta_3 = 100 + 120\cos\theta_4$$
$$25 + 150\cos\theta_3 = 100 + 120\cos\theta_4$$
$$150\cos\theta_3 - 120\cos\theta_4 = 75 \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Equation (iii) becomes:
$$50\sin 60° + 150\sin\theta_3 = 0 + 120\sin\theta_4$$
$$50(0.866) + 150\sin\theta_3 = 120\sin\theta_4$$
$$43.30 + 150\sin\theta_3 = 120\sin\theta_4$$
$$150\sin\theta_3 - 120\sin\theta_4 = -43.30 \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to form a known vector equation**

From (iv): $150\cos\theta_3 = 75 + 120\cos\theta_4$
From (v): $150\sin\theta_3 = -43.30 + 120\sin\theta_4$

These represent the x and y components of a vector equation. The vector $150 \angle \theta_3$ ends at a point that depends on the value of $\theta_4$.

**Step 3: Square and add to eliminate $\theta_3$**

$(150\cos\theta_3)^2 + (150\sin\theta_3)^2 = (75 + 120\cos\theta_4)^2 + (-43.30 + 120\sin\theta_4)^2$

Left side: $150^2 = 22500$

Right side expansion:
$$= 75^2 + 2(75)(120)\cos\theta_4 + 120^2\cos^2\theta_4 + 43.30^2 - 2(43.30)(120)\sin\theta_4 + 120^2\sin^2\theta_4$$

$$= 5625 + 18000\cos\theta_4 + 14400\cos^2\theta_4 + 1874.89 - 10392\sin\theta_4 + 14400\sin^2\theta_4$$

$$= 5625 + 1874.89 + 14400(\cos^2\theta_4 + \sin^2\theta_4) + 18000\cos\theta_4 - 10392\sin\theta_4$$

$$= 7499.89 + 14400 + 18000\cos\theta_4 - 10392\sin\theta_4$$

$$= 21899.89 + 18000\cos\theta_4 - 10392\sin\theta_4$$

**Step 4: Equate and solve**

$$22500 = 21899.89 + 18000\cos\theta_4 - 10392\sin\theta_4$$

$$600.11 = 18000\cos\theta_4 - 10392\sin\theta_4$$

$$0.03334 = \cos\theta_4 - 0.5773\sin\theta_4$$

**Step 5: Write in form** $A\cos\theta_4 + B\sin\theta_4 = C$

$$A = 1, B = -0.5773, C = 0.03334$$

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.3333} = \sqrt{1.3333} = 1.1547$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{B}{A}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.5773) = -30°$$

So: $1.1547\cos(\theta_4 - 30°) = 0.03334$

$$\cos(\theta_4 - 30°) = 0.02887$$

$$\theta_4 - 30° = \pm 88.345°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_4 = 118.345° \text{ (open config)} \text{ or } \theta_4 = -58.345° \text{ (crossed config)}}$$

**Step 6: Find $\theta_3$ from equation (iv)**

For $\theta_4 = 118.345°$: $\cos 118.345° = -0.4782$

$$150\cos\theta_3 = 75 + 120(-0.4782) = 75 - 57.38 = 17.62$$
$$\cos\theta_3 = 0.1175 \Rightarrow \theta_3 = 83.25°$$

For $\theta_4 = -58.345°$: $\cos(-58.345°) = 0.5252$

$$150\cos\theta_3 = 75 + 120(0.5252) = 75 + 63.02 = 138.02$$
$$\cos\theta_3 = 0.9201 \Rightarrow \theta_3 = 23.05°$$

[7 marks awarded for complete numerical solution]

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme - Alternative]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Starting from the loop closure equation of a four-bar mechanism, derive the velocity and acceleration loop closure equations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a slider-crank mechanism with crank length $r = 100$ mm, connecting rod length $l = 350$ mm, and crank angle $\theta = 30°$, find the velocity of the slider if the crank rotates at $\omega = 20$ rad/s (constant).

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B:

**Part (a) - Derivation of Velocity and Acceleration LCE [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Position LCE**
$$r_2 e^{j\theta_2} + r_3 e^{j\theta_3} - r_4 e^{j\theta_4} - r_1 e^{j\theta_1} = 0$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to time to get velocity LCE**

For a rigid link, $r_i$ is constant, so:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[r_i e^{j\theta_i}\right] = r_i \cdot j\dot{\theta_i} \cdot e^{j\theta_i}$$

Therefore, the velocity LCE is:
$$r_2 \dot{\theta_2} j e^{j\theta_2} + r_3 \dot{\theta_3} j e^{j\theta_3} - r_4 \dot{\theta_4} j e^{j\theta_4} - r_1 \dot{\theta_1} j e^{j\theta_1} = 0$$

**Step 3: Differentiate again to get acceleration LCE**

Applying product rule to $r_i \dot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i}$:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[r_i \dot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i}\right] = r_i \ddot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} + r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2 (j)^2 e^{j\theta_i}$$

Since $j^2 = -1$:
$$= r_i \ddot{\theta_i} j e^{j\theta_i} - r_i \dot{\theta_i}^2 e^{j\theta_i}$$

The acceleration LCE is:
$$r_2 \ddot{\theta_2} j e^{j\theta_2} - r_2 \dot{\theta_2}^2 e^{j\theta_2} + r_3 \ddot{\theta_3} j e^{j\theta_3} - r_3 \dot{\theta_3}^2 e^{j\theta_3} - r_4 \ddot{\theta_4} j e^{j\theta_4} + r_4 \dot{\theta_4}^2 e^{j\theta_4} - r_1 \ddot{\theta_1} j e^{j\theta_1} + r_1 \dot{\theta_1}^2 e^{j\theta_1} = 0$$

This is the **general acceleration LCE** in complex form. [7 marks]

---

**Part (b) - Slider Velocity Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $r = 100$ mm, $l = 350$ mm, $\theta = 30°$, $\omega = 20$ rad/s.

**Step 1: Find the connecting rod angle $\phi$**

From position equation $r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r\sin\theta}{l} = \frac{100 \times \sin 30°}{350} = \frac{100 \times 0.5}{350} = \frac{50}{350} = 0.1429$$

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.1429) = 8.213°$$

**Step 2: Apply velocity LCE in scalar form**

$$\dot{x} = -\frac{r\dot{\theta}\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$\theta + \phi = 30° + 8.213° = 38.213°$

$\sin(38.213°) = 0.6186$

$\cos(8.213°) = 0.9898$

$$\dot{x} = -\frac{100 \times 20 \times 0.6186}{0.9898} = -\frac{1237.2}{0.9898} = -1249.85 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{x} \approx -1250 \text{ mm/s} = -1.25 \text{ m/s}}$$

The negative sign indicates the slider is moving in the negative x-direction at this instant. [7 marks]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (Most Common):** Students often forget to apply the correct sign (+/-) when traversing the loop. *Always draw the loop arrow first, then assign signs.*
> 2. **Confusion between $j$ and $i$:** In engineering, the imaginary unit is $j$ (not $i$ as in mathematics) to avoid confusion with current.
> 3. **Forgetting to differentiate $e^{j\theta}$ correctly:** The derivative is $j\dot{\theta}e^{j\theta}$, not $\dot{\theta}e^{j\theta}$. The factor $j$ is critical.
> 4. **Mixing degrees and radians:** Always use radians in $\dot{\theta}$ and $\ddot{\theta}$; convert from degrees by multiplying $\pi/180$.
> 5. **Neglecting the centripetal term $r\dot{\theta}^2$:** In acceleration analysis, the $r\dot{\theta}^2$ term arises from the second derivative of $e^{j\theta}$ and must be retained.
> 6. **Choosing the wrong configuration:** A four-bar mechanism has two assembly configurations (open and crossed) for each input position. Verify which is physically valid.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Loop Closure Equation (LCE):** Vector sum of link vectors in a closed loop equals zero: $\sum \vec{R_i} = \vec{0}$.
- ✅ **Four-Bar LCE:** $r_2 e^{j\theta_2} + r_3 e^{j\theta_3} = r_1 e^{j\theta_1} + r_4 e^{j\theta_4}$.
- ✅ **Real and Imaginary parts** yield two scalar equations that can be solved for unknown angles.
- ✅ **Velocity LCE:** Multiply each term by $j\dot{\theta_i}$ — arises from differentiation of $e^{j\theta_i}$.
- ✅ **Acceleration LCE:** Two terms appear per link — tangential ($r\ddot{\theta}$) and centripetal ($r\dot{\theta}^2$).
- ✅ **Slider-Crank LCE:** $r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$ gives the rod angle; piston velocity $\dot{x} = -\frac{r\omega\sin(\theta+\phi)}{\cos\phi}$.
- ✅ **Freudenstein's Equation:** Closed-form result of LCE for four-bar position analysis: $K_1\cos\theta_2 - K_2\cos\theta_4 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$.
- ✅ **Number of Independent Loops:** $L = N - 1$ for a 1-DOF planar mechanism.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Follow the loop traversal arrow; $+ve$ for forward links, $-ve$ for reverse.
- ✅ **LCE enables systematic analysis** without drawing velocity/acceleration polygons, suitable for programming and computer solution.
- ✅ **KTU Favorite:** Expect either a derivation question (7-10 marks) or a numerical problem (7-10 marks) on LCE every exam cycle.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT403 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity and Acceleration Analysis - Graphical and Analytical Methods, Complex Number Methods, Computer Oriented Methods, Instant Centre, Kennedy's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Velocity Analysis — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Analysis** is the branch of kinematics of machinery that determines the linear and angular velocities of all the moving links of a mechanism at a given instant, given the input motion of one link. The methods prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: **Graphical (velocity polygon)**, **Analytical (relative velocity equations)**, **Complex Number (vector loop)**, and **Computer-Oriented (matrix/iterative solver)** methods.

## 1.2 Acceleration Analysis — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceleration Analysis** is the determination of linear and angular accelerations of every moving link of a mechanism at a given configuration, when the input link motion (velocity + acceleration) is known. For mechanisms with sliding pairs and Coriolis effects, the Coriolis component $a_{Cor} = 2\,\omega \times v_{rel}$ must be included.

## 1.3 Instant Centre (Instantaneous Centre of Rotation) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Instant Centre** of a rigid link in plane motion is the unique point (in the body, in the fixed frame, or in an extension) about which the link may be considered to be in pure rotation at that instant. The number of instant centres for a mechanism with $N$ links is given by the **Euler–Kluyver relation**:
>
> $$n_{IC} = \frac{N\,(N - 1)}{2}$$

## 1.4 Kennedy's Theorem (Aronhold–Kennedy Theorem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kennedy's Theorem:** When three rigid links of a mechanism are in plane relative motion, their three instant centres are collinear (lie on a single straight line). This theorem is the key to locating the secondary (derived) instant centres in a multi-link mechanism.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are sitting on a giant **Ferris wheel** with a friend on a different seat. Even though the wheel rotates at one steady angular velocity, the **linear velocity** of your friend is *not* the same as yours — it depends on the distance of his seat from the rotation centre. A point on the wheel that happens to lie *exactly* on the rotation axis at that instant has **zero linear velocity** — that is, the **Instant Centre** of the wheel at that instant.

Similarly, consider a **scissor lift** being raised. Every cross-member rotates and translates; the tip of one arm appears to rotate about some hidden pivot — that pivot is the instant centre of that arm at that instant. **Kennedy's Theorem** is the rule that says: the instant centres of three connected members always lie on a straight line, just like three men standing in a line can only see each other along that line.

For a rotating arm carrying a sliding block (e.g., a **Whitworth quick-return mechanism**), the block experiences an extra apparent acceleration called the **Coriolis acceleration** — this is the same sideways "push" you feel when sliding across a rotating merry-go-round.

## 1.6 Standard Units (KTU Convention)

- Linear velocity: $\mathbf{m/s}$
- Angular velocity: $\mathbf{rad/s}$
- Linear acceleration: $\mathbf{m/s^2}$
- Angular acceleration: $\mathbf{rad/s^2}$
- Lengths: $\mathbf{mm}$ (in mechanism diagrams) or $\mathbf{m}$ (in calculations)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Velocity Analysis — Logic Flow

1. **Establish the configuration** of the mechanism at the given instant (link lengths, position angles $\theta_2,\theta_3,\theta_4$, etc.).
2. **Identify the velocity of the input point** (e.g., $V_B = \omega_2 \cdot L_{AB}$ with direction perpendicular to $AB$).
3. **Apply the relative-velocity equation** between two points on the same rigid link:
   $$\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{CB}, \quad \vec{V}_{CB} \perp BC$$
4. **Apply joint constraints** (e.g., a slider has velocity along the slider axis only).
5. **Solve the system** for unknown angular velocity $\omega_3$ and the unknown point velocity.
6. **Repeat for all links**.

## 2.2 Acceleration Analysis — Logic Flow

1. From velocity analysis, obtain $\omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4, V_{\text{points}}$.
2. For a fixed-length link, the relative acceleration has **two components**:
   $$a_{CB} = a_{CB}^{t} + a_{CB}^{n}$$
   - **Tangential:** $a_{CB}^{t} = \alpha_3 \cdot L_{BC}$, perpendicular to $BC$.
   - **Normal/Centripetal:** $a_{CB}^{n} = \omega_3^{2} \cdot L_{BC}$, directed from $C$ to $B$.
3. **Apply the relative-acceleration equation:**
   $$\vec{a}_C = \vec{a}_B + \vec{a}_{CB}^{t} + \vec{a}_{CB}^{n}$$
4. **If a slider exists on a rotating link**, include the **Coriolis component**:
   $$\vec{a}_{Cor} = 2\,\vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{v}_{rel} \quad (\text{magnitude } 2\omega_3\, v_{rel})$$
5. **Apply joint constraints** to find $\alpha_3$ and the slider linear acceleration.

## 2.3 Graphical Method — Velocity Polygon

- Choose a **pole $o$** and draw $\overrightarrow{o\,b} = \vec{V}_B$ (perpendicular to $AB$, magnitude $\omega_2 L_{AB}$).
- From $b$, draw a line **perpendicular to $BC$**; from $o$, draw a line representing the constraint of the next joint; the intersection gives point $c$.
- $\overrightarrow{o\,c} = \vec{V}_C$. The **velocity image** is the figure $obc\ldots$ with pole at $o$.

## 2.4 Analytical (Relative Velocity) Method

For a four-bar $ABCD$ with $B$ on crank $AB$ and $C$ on coupler $BC$ and $CD$:
- Write $V_C = V_B + V_{CB}$ in $x$ and $y$ components.
- Use perpendicularity ($V_{CB} \perp BC$) to express $V_{CB}$ in terms of $\omega_3$.
- Solve two linear equations for $\omega_3$ and $V_C$.

## 2.5 Complex Number (Vector Loop) Method

- Loop-closure equation: $\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 = \vec{r}_4 + \vec{r}_1$.
- Using $\vec{r}_k = L_k e^{i\theta_k}$:
  $$L_2 e^{i\theta_2} + L_3 e^{i\theta_3} = L_4 e^{i\theta_4} + L_1 e^{i\theta_1}$$
- Differentiate once: $i L_2 \omega_2 e^{i\theta_2} + i L_3 \omega_3 e^{i\theta_3} = i L_4 \omega_4 e^{i\theta_4}$ (when $\omega_1 = \alpha_1 = 0$).
- Separate into **real and imaginary parts** to obtain two scalar equations in $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$.
- Differentiate again: $-L_2 \omega_2^{2} e^{i\theta_2} + i L_2 \alpha_2 e^{i\theta_2} - L_3 \omega_3^{2} e^{i\theta_3} + i L_3 \alpha_3 e^{i\theta_3} = -L_4 \omega_4^{2} e^{i\theta_4} + i L_4 \alpha_4 e^{i\theta_4}$.
- Separate real and imaginary parts for $\alpha_3, \alpha_4$.

## 2.6 Computer-Oriented Method

- Express the loop-closure as a **non-linear algebraic vector equation**.
- Use the **Newton–Raphson iterative solver** for position; then evaluate velocity and acceleration equations by closed-form differentiation.
- Industrial CAE tools (e.g., ADAMS, RecurDyn) implement the **matrix loop-closure approach**:
  $$\begin{bmatrix} -L_3 \sin\theta_3 & L_4 \sin\theta_4 \\ \phantom{-}L_3 \cos\theta_3 & -L_4 \cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} -L_2 \omega_2 \sin\theta_2 \\ \phantom{-}L_2 \omega_2 \cos\theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

## 2.7 Instant Centre Analysis — Step-by-Step

1. Draw the kinematic sketch of the mechanism.
2. Identify all **primary instant centres** (those at the actual pin joints and coincident with slider pivots).
3. Apply **Kennedy's theorem** between triplets of links to locate the **secondary (derived) instant centres** by drawing straight lines through known centres and using the geometry of the mechanism.
4. Verify the count: $n_{IC} = N(N-1)/2$.
5. Use the instant-centre method to compute the velocity of any point $P$ on link $i$:
   $$V_P = \omega_i \cdot \overline{P\,I_{1i}}$$
   where $I_{1i}$ is the instant centre of link $i$ with respect to the fixed link 1.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engine design:** Velocity & acceleration analysis of the slider-crank is essential to compute piston speed, inertial forces, and the secondary (inertia) couple.
- **Robotic manipulators:** Joint velocities and accelerations are derived using the **Jacobian** which is essentially a velocity coefficient matrix from the same logic.
- **Quick-return mechanisms:** The instant-centre method gives an elegant graphical solution to the velocity ratio in shaper and Whitworth drives.
- **Wind-turbine blade design:** Coriolis-corrected blade accelerations determine fatigue loading.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Direction / Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Velocity of a point on a rotating link | $V = \omega \cdot r$ | $\perp$ to radius $r$, in sense of $\omega$ |
| 2 | Relative velocity (rigid link) | $\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{CB}$ | $V_{CB} \perp BC$ |
| 3 | Instant-centre count | $n_{IC} = N(N-1)/2$ | pure number |
| 4 | Velocity by IC method | $V_P = \omega_i \cdot \vert P\,I_{1i} \vert$ | m/s |
| 5 | Kennedy's theorem | $I_{12}, I_{23}, I_{13}$ are collinear | geometry |
| 6 | Centripetal (normal) acceleration | $a^{n} = \omega^{2} \cdot r$ | directed from point to centre of rotation |
| 7 | Tangential acceleration | $a^{t} = \alpha \cdot r$ | $\perp$ to radius, in sense of $\alpha$ |
| 8 | Coriolis acceleration | $a_{Cor} = 2\,\omega\,v_{rel}$ | rotate $v_{rel}$ by $90°$ in sense of $\omega$ |
| 9 | Complex-loop velocity | $iL_2\omega_2 e^{i\theta_2} + iL_3\omega_3 e^{i\theta_3} = iL_4\omega_4 e^{i\theta_4}$ | separate Re / Im |
| 10 | Complex-loop acceleration | $-L_2\omega_2^{2}e^{i\theta_2}+iL_2\alpha_2 e^{i\theta_2} -L_3\omega_3^{2}e^{i\theta_3}+iL_3\alpha_3 e^{i\theta_3} = -L_4\omega_4^{2}e^{i\theta_4}+iL_4\alpha_4 e^{i\theta_4}$ | separate Re / Im |
| 11 | Velocity ratio by IC | $\dfrac{V_C}{V_B} = \dfrac{\vert C\,I_{13}\vert}{\vert B\,I_{13}\vert}$ | where $I_{13}$ is IC of coupler 3 wrt ground |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relative Velocity Equation (Vector Form)

For a rigid link $BC$ in plane motion, the position of $C$ relative to $B$ is the constant vector $\vec{r}_{C/B}$ (its length $L_{BC}$ is fixed). Differentiating the position vector $\vec{r}_C = \vec{r}_B + \vec{r}_{C/B}$ with respect to time:

$$\vec{V}_C = \frac{d\vec{r}_C}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt} + \frac{d\vec{r}_{C/B}}{dt}$$

Since $\vec{r}_{C/B}$ has constant magnitude but its direction rotates with the angular velocity $\vec{\omega}_3$ of link $BC$:

$$\frac{d\vec{r}_{C/B}}{dt} = \vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{C/B} = \vec{V}_{CB}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{CB}, \qquad \vec{V}_{CB} \perp BC\;}$$

For a 2-D mechanism, $\vec{V}_{CB}$ is drawn **perpendicular to $BC$** with magnitude $L_{BC}\,\omega_3$ and sense determined by the assumed sense of $\omega_3$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relative Acceleration Equation (with Centripetal + Tangential Split)

Differentiating the velocity equation:

$$\vec{a}_C = \vec{a}_B + \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\vec{V}_{CB}\bigr) = \vec{a}_B + \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{C/B}\bigr)$$

Using the product rule and noting that $\vec{r}_{C/B}$ is constant in length but its orientation changes:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{C/B}\bigr) = \frac{d\vec{\omega}_3}{dt} \times \vec{r}_{C/B} + \vec{\omega}_3 \times \frac{d\vec{r}_{C/B}}{dt}$$

$$= \vec{\alpha}_3 \times \vec{r}_{C/B} + \vec{\omega}_3 \times \bigl(\vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{C/B}\bigr)$$

The first term is the **tangential** part; the second is the **centripetal** part, which simplifies to $-\omega_3^{2}\,\vec{r}_{C/B}$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\;\vec{a}_C = \vec{a}_B + \vec{a}_{CB}^{\,t} + \vec{a}_{CB}^{\,n}\;}$$

with magnitudes $\vert a_{CB}^{\,t}\vert = \alpha_3 L_{BC}$ and $\vert a_{CB}^{\,n}\vert = \omega_3^{2} L_{BC}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Coriolis Component for a Slider on a Rotating Link

For a slider $C$ sliding on a link $3$ that is itself rotating with angular velocity $\omega_3$:

$$\vec{a}_C = \vec{a}_{\text{transport}} + \vec{a}_{\text{rel}} + \vec{a}_{Cor}$$

where:
- $\vec{a}_{\text{transport}} = \vec{\alpha}_3 \times \vec{r}_{C/O_3} + \vec{\omega}_3 \times (\vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{C/O_3})$
- $\vec{a}_{\text{rel}} = \ddot{s}\,\hat{e}_t$ (along the slot direction)
- $\vec{a}_{Cor} = 2\,\vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{v}_{rel}$, with $\vert a_{Cor}\vert = 2\,\omega_3\,v_{rel}$.

**Rule of direction:** rotate $\vec{v}_{rel}$ by $90°$ in the sense of $\omega_3$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Complex Number (Vector-Loop) Method

Consider a four-bar mechanism with links $L_1$ (ground, fixed), $L_2$ (crank), $L_3$ (coupler), $L_4$ (rocker).

**Step 1 — Loop closure:**

$$L_2 e^{i\theta_2} + L_3 e^{i\theta_3} = L_4 e^{i\theta_4} + L_1 e^{i\theta_1}$$

If link 1 is horizontal (so $\theta_1 = 0$, $L_1 e^{i\theta_1} = L_1$):

$$L_2 e^{i\theta_2} + L_3 e^{i\theta_3} - L_4 e^{i\theta_4} = L_1$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate (assume $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ constant; $\omega_1 = 0$):**

Differentiate using $\frac{d}{dt}e^{i\theta} = i\omega\,e^{i\theta}$:

$$iL_2\omega_2 e^{i\theta_2} + iL_3\omega_3 e^{i\theta_3} = iL_4\omega_4 e^{i\theta_4}$$

**Step 3 — Separate real and imaginary parts:**

Let $R_j = L_j \omega_j$ and define $\beta_j = \theta_j + 90°$ (so $i e^{i\theta_j} = e^{i\beta_j}$):

$$R_2\cos\beta_2 + R_3\cos\beta_3 = R_4\cos\beta_4$$
$$R_2\sin\beta_2 + R_3\sin\beta_3 = R_4\sin\beta_4$$

**Step 4 — Solve the $2 \times 2$ linear system for $R_3, R_4$** (i.e., $\omega_3, \omega_4$).

**Step 5 — Differentiate again for acceleration:**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(iL_2\omega_2 e^{i\theta_2}\bigr) = -L_2\omega_2^{2}e^{i\theta_2} + iL_2\alpha_2 e^{i\theta_2}$$

so:

$$-L_2\omega_2^{2}e^{i\theta_2} + iL_2\alpha_2 e^{i\theta_2} - L_3\omega_3^{2}e^{i\theta_3} + iL_3\alpha_3 e^{i\theta_3} = -L_4\omega_4^{2}e^{i\theta_4} + iL_4\alpha_4 e^{i\theta_4}$$

**Step 6 — Separate Re / Im** and solve for $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$ (the angular accelerations of the coupler and rocker).

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example — Slider-Crank Acceleration

**Data:** Crank $AB = 50$ mm, $\omega_2 = 50$ rad/s CCW (constant), connecting rod $BC = 150$ mm, $\theta_2 = 60°$ from horizontal, slider $C$ moves horizontally. Find $V_C$, $\omega_3$, $a_C$, $\alpha_3$.

**Step 1 — Position of $B$:**
$$B = (L_{AB}\cos 60°,\;L_{AB}\sin 60°) = (25.0,\;43.30)\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Position of $C$ on x-axis (front configuration):**

Using $C = B + L_{BC}(\cos\theta_3,\sin\theta_3)$ with $C_y = 0$:

$$\sin\theta_3 = -B_y/L_{BC} = -43.30/150 = -0.2887 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_3 = -16.78°$$

$$C_x = 25.0 + 150\cos(-16.78°) = 25.0 + 143.62 = 168.62\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity of $B$:**

$$V_B = \omega_2 L_{AB} = 50 \times 0.05 = 2.5\;\text{m/s}, \quad \text{perpendicular to } AB \text{ (at }150°\text{)}$$

$$V_{Bx} = 2.5\cos 150° = -2.165\;\text{m/s}, \quad V_{By} = 2.5\sin 150° = +1.25\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity equation $V_C = V_B + V_{CB}$ with $V_C$ horizontal:**

$V_{CB}$ is $\perp BC$ with $\theta_3 = -16.78°$, so $V_{CB}$ is at $73.22°$:

$$V_{CB,x} = \omega_3 L_{BC}\cos 73.22° = 0.0433\,\omega_3, \quad V_{CB,y} = 0.1436\,\omega_3$$

Since $V_{C,y} = 0$:

$$0 = 1.25 + 0.1436\,\omega_3 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_3 = -8.704\;\text{rad/s (CW)}$$

$$V_{C,x} = -2.165 + 0.0433(-8.704) = -2.542\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Acceleration of $B$ (only centripetal, $\omega_2$ constant):**

$$a_B^{n} = \omega_2^{2} L_{AB} = 2500 \times 0.05 = 125\;\text{m/s}^2, \text{ from }B\to A \text{ (at }240°\text{)}$$

$$a_{Bx} = 125\cos 240° = -62.5\;\text{m/s}^2, \quad a_{By} = 125\sin 240° = -108.25\;\text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 6 — Acceleration of $C$ (slider, so $a_C$ horizontal):**

$$a_{CB}^{n} = \omega_3^{2} L_{BC} = 75.76 \times 0.150 = 11.36\;\text{m/s}^2, \text{ from }C\to B$$

Unit vector $C\to B = (-0.957,\;0.289)$:

$$a_{CB,x}^{n} = -10.88\;\text{m/s}^2, \quad a_{CB,y}^{n} = +3.28\;\text{m/s}^2$$

$$a_{CB}^{t} = \alpha_3 L_{BC} \text{ perpendicular to }BC, \text{ at }73.22°: \quad (0.0433\,\alpha_3,\;0.1436\,\alpha_3)$$

$$a_{C,y} = 0:\;\; 0 = -108.25 + 3.28 + 0.1436\,\alpha_3 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha_3 = +731.0\;\text{rad/s}^2 \text{ (CCW)}$$

$$a_{C,x} = -62.5 - 10.88 + 0.0433(731.0) = -73.38 + 31.65 = -41.7\;\text{m/s}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $V_C = -2.54$ m/s, $\omega_3 = -8.70$ rad/s; $a_C = -41.7$ m/s², $\alpha_3 = +731$ rad/s².

## 3.6 Derivation: Number of Instant Centres

For a mechanism with $N$ links, the number of independent pairs is the number of ways to choose 2 from $N$:

$$n_{IC} = \binom{N}{2} = \frac{N\,(N-1)}{2}$$

Examples:
- Four-bar ($N=4$): $n_{IC} = 4\cdot3/2 = 6$.
- Six-bar (e.g., Stephenson III, Watt): $N=6$: $n_{IC} = 6\cdot5/2 = 15$.

## 3.7 Derivation: Kennedy's Theorem (Geometric Proof)

Consider three rigid links $A, B, C$ in plane relative motion. Let $I_{AB}$, $I_{BC}$, $I_{AC}$ be their three instant centres. Choose $I_{AB}$ as the reference: a point on link $A$ that is the instant centre of $A$ w.r.t. $B$ has zero velocity w.r.t. $B$, and a point on link $B$ coincident with it has zero velocity w.r.t. $A$. In link $B$, this point has zero velocity w.r.t. link $A$.

Now consider a particle of link $B$ located at $I_{AC}$. The velocity of $I_{AC}$ as a point of $B$ is the same as the velocity of $I_{AC}$ as a point of $C$ (since they are coincident in space at the instant). The locus of points of $B$ with zero velocity w.r.t. $A$ is a straight line through $I_{AB}$ (perpendicular to $V_B$ of any point in $B$). Similarly, the locus of points of $C$ with zero velocity w.r.t. $A$ is a line through $I_{AC}$. The point $I_{BC}$ must lie at the intersection of these two loci, and therefore:

$$\boxed{\;I_{AB},\;I_{BC},\;I_{AC}\text{ are collinear.}\;}$$

## 3.8 Velocity Ratio Using Instant Centre

For a four-bar with coupler link $3$ having instant centre $I_{13}$ (w.r.t. ground), the points $B$ (on link 2) and $C$ (on link 4) of the coupler link both rotate about $I_{13}$ at the same instant. Thus:

$$\frac{V_B}{\overline{B\,I_{13}}} = \omega_3 = \frac{V_C}{\overline{C\,I_{13}}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{V_C}{V_B} = \frac{\overline{C\,I_{13}}}{\overline{B\,I_{13}}}$$

This ratio is **read directly as a length ratio** from the mechanism diagram, once the secondary instant centre has been located.

```mermaid
    C --> D1[Graphical - Velocity and Acceleration Polygons]

    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{Sliding pair present?}

    F -->|No| H[Compute accelerations using aC equals aB plus aCBt plus aCBn]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Separate normal and tangential components]
    I --> J[Apply joint constraints to solve]
```

```mermaid
    J14 -->|on link 4| L4[Link 4 Rocker CD]
    L4 -->|pin at D| J14
    L4 -->|pin at C| J34[I34 at C]
    J23 -->|line 1| KT[Kennedy line for links 1 2 3]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Four-Bar Mechanism Block Architecture (Functional Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fixed link 1 - ground A and D] --> B[Link 2 - Crank AB rotating with omega2]
    B -->|pin at B| C[Link 3 - Coupler BC with unknown omega3]

    D -->|pin at D| A
    B -.->|V at B = omega2 times AB perpendicular to AB| V1[Velocity vector V B]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2-3 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the Coriolis component** in acceleration analysis of mechanisms with a slider on a rotating arm (e.g., quick-return, Scotch-yoke variants).
> 2. **Drawing $V_{CB}$ parallel to $BC$** instead of perpendicular — a very common error worth -2 marks.
> 3. **Sign error in centripetal direction** — must point from point towards the centre of rotation, never away.
> 4. **Wrong sense of $\omega_3$** — a positive sign convention must be chosen first (CCW positive) and maintained.
> 5. **Instant-centre count mismatch** — every derived (secondary) IC must satisfy Kennedy's collinearity, and the final count must equal $N(N-1)/2$.
> 6. **Treating $I_{13}$ as the geometric centre** of link 3 — it is a *virtual* point, often lying outside the link body.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Conceptual)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

> Define an **Instant Centre of a rigid link** in plane motion. State the formula for the number of instant centres in a mechanism having $N$ links.

**Model Answer:**
An **Instant Centre (IC)** is a point, either on the link itself or on its extension, about which the link may be considered to be in pure rotation at that instant. The velocity of this point is zero relative to the link's instant of motion.
The number of instant centres in a mechanism with $N$ links is:
$$n_{IC} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent]**

> State and explain **Kennedy's theorem** of three centres in line. Mention its importance in locating secondary instant centres.

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** When three rigid links of a mechanism move relative to each other in plane motion, their three instant centres are collinear (lie on a single straight line).
**Importance:** It provides a graphical means to locate the *derived* (secondary) instant centres when the primary ones (at the actual pin joints) are insufficient. The intersection of two Kennedy lines (each formed by the line through two known ICs) gives the third, unknown IC.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> Two alternative questions A and B are provided. Each contains sub-parts of 7 marks each. Choose **one** full question.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Velocity Polygon + Coriolis Acceleration

**[KTU University Exam — Model Equivalent, ESE Pattern]**

> Consider a **four-bar mechanism** $ABCD$ with the following data:
> - Crank $AB = 50$ mm rotating CCW at $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s (constant).
> - Coupler $BC = 150$ mm, Rocker $CD = 100$ mm, Ground $AD = 175$ mm.
> - At the instant considered, $\theta_2 = 60°$ (crank with respect to ground link $AD$).
> - The mechanism is in the *open* configuration.
>
> **(a)** Construct the **velocity polygon** and determine the velocity of point $C$ and the angular velocities of links $BC$ and $CD$. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Determine the **linear acceleration of $C$** and the **angular accelerations of $BC$ and $CD$** using the relative-acceleration method. (7 Marks)

#### (a) Model Solution (7 Marks) — Velocity Polygon

**Step 1 — Position of $B$:**
$$B = (50\cos 60°,\;50\sin 60°) = (25.0,\;43.30)\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Find $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ from geometry (using closure):**
Loop closure: $L_2 e^{i\theta_2} + L_3 e^{i\theta_3} = L_4 e^{i\theta_4} + L_1$.

Substituting the position equations:
$(25 + 150\cos\theta_3 - 100\cos\theta_4) = 175$
$(43.30 + 150\sin\theta_3 - 100\sin\theta_4) = 0$

Solving (iteration / trigonometric method):
$$\theta_3 \approx 21.6° \quad \text{and} \quad \theta_4 \approx 99.6°$$

(Students are expected to show the trigonometric method: separate Re and Im of the loop-closure, obtain two equations, solve for $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$.)

**Step 3 — Velocity of $B$:**
$$\vec{V}_B = \omega_2 \times L_{AB} = 20 \times 0.05 = 1.0\;\text{m/s, perpendicular to } AB \text{ at }150°$$
$$\vec{V}_B = (1.0\cos 150°,\;1.0\sin 150°) = (-0.866,\;0.5)\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity of $C$ (from joint constraints):**
$\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{CB}$, where $V_{CB} \perp BC$ ($\theta_3 = 21.6°$, so $V_{CB}$ direction is $111.6°$).
$\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_D + \vec{V}_{CD}$, where $V_{CD} \perp CD$ ($\theta_4 = 99.6°$, so $V_{CD}$ direction is $189.6°$).

**Step 5 — Velocity Polygon Construction:**
- Choose pole $o$ (any point, usually at the upper-left).
- Draw $\overrightarrow{ob} = \vec{V}_B$ of length proportional to 1.0 m/s in the direction 150°.
- From $b$, draw a line **perpendicular to $BC$**.
- From $o$, draw a line **perpendicular to $CD$**.
- The intersection is the point $c$.
- Measure $\overrightarrow{oc} \approx 1.5$ m/s, $\overrightarrow{bc} \approx 0.6$ m/s.

**Step 6 — Compute angular velocities:**
$$\omega_3 = \frac{\vert \vec{V}_{CB}\vert}{L_{BC}} = \frac{0.6}{0.150} = 4.0\;\text{rad/s (CCW)}$$
$$\omega_4 = \frac{\vert \vec{V}_{CD}\vert}{L_{CD}} = \frac{0.7}{0.100} = 7.0\;\text{rad/s (CW, since $V_{CD}$ direction opposes assumed $\omega_4$ sense)}$$

#### (b) Model Solution (7 Marks) — Acceleration Analysis

**Step 1 — Acceleration of $B$ (only centripetal as $\alpha_2 = 0$):**
$$a_B = \omega_2^{2} L_{AB} = 400 \times 0.05 = 20\;\text{m/s}^2,\text{ directed from } B \to A \text{ at }240°$$
$$\vec{a}_B = (20\cos 240°,\;20\sin 240°) = (-10.0,\;-17.32)\;\text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Centripetal component of $a_{CB}$:**
$$a_{CB}^{n} = \omega_3^{2} L_{BC} = 16.0 \times 0.150 = 2.4\;\text{m/s}^2,\text{ from }C\to B$$
Unit vector $C\to B = (\cos(21.6°+180°),\;\sin(21.6°+180°)) = (-0.930,\;-0.368)$
$$\vec{a}_{CB}^{n} = 2.4 \times (-0.930,\;-0.368) = (-2.232,\;-0.883)\;\text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Tangential component of $a_{CB}$:** unknown magnitude $\alpha_3 L_{BC} = 0.150\,\alpha_3$ in direction $111.6°$:
$$\vec{a}_{CB}^{t} = 0.150\alpha_3\,(\cos 111.6°,\;\sin 111.6°) = (-0.0559\alpha_3,\;0.1395\alpha_3)$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration of $D$ (pin on ground, so zero):**
$\vec{a}_D = \vec{0}$.

**Step 5 — Centripetal and tangential of $a_{CD}$:**
$$a_{CD}^{n} = \omega_4^{2} L_{CD} = 49.0 \times 0.100 = 4.9\;\text{m/s}^2,\text{ from }C\to D \text{ (direction }279.6°\text{)}$$
Unit vector $C\to D$: $(\cos 279.6°,\;\sin 279.6°) = (0.162,\;-0.987)$
$$\vec{a}_{CD}^{n} = 4.9 \times (0.162,\;-0.987) = (0.794,\;-4.836)\;\text{m/s}^2$$

Tangential $a_{CD}^{t} = 0.100\alpha_4$ in direction $189.6°$:
$$\vec{a}_{CD}^{t} = 0.100\alpha_4\,(-0.987,\;-0.162) = (-0.0987\alpha_4,\;-0.0162\alpha_4)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the equation $\vec{a}_C = \vec{a}_B + \vec{a}_{CB}^{t} + \vec{a}_{CB}^{n} = \vec{a}_D + \vec{a}_{CD}^{t} + \vec{a}_{CD}^{n}$:**

$$x: -10.0 - 0.0559\alpha_3 - 2.232 = 0 - 0.0987\alpha_4 + 0.794$$
$$y: -17.32 + 0.1395\alpha_3 - 0.883 = 0 - 0.0162\alpha_4 - 4.836$$

Simplifying:
$$-12.232 - 0.0559\alpha_3 = 0.794 - 0.0987\alpha_4$$
$$-18.203 + 0.1395\alpha_3 = -4.836 - 0.0162\alpha_4$$

From the second equation: $0.1395\alpha_3 + 0.0162\alpha_4 = 13.367$

From the first: $-0.0559\alpha_3 + 0.0987\alpha_4 = 13.026$

Solving this $2\times 2$ system:
$$\alpha_3 \approx +89.0\;\text{rad/s}^2 \text{ (CCW)}, \qquad \alpha_4 \approx +183.0\;\text{rad/s}^2 \text{ (CCW)}$$

**Step 7 — Acceleration of $C$:** Substitute back into the $x$ or $y$ component:
