# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Function Generator. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **Function Generator** is a mechanism that produces a prescribed mathematical relationship between the input and output motions. In a 4-bar function generator, the angular displacement $\phi$ of the output rocker is a desired function $f$ of the input crank angle $\theta$:

$$\phi = f(\theta)$$

The function $f$ can be linear ($\phi = k\theta$), sinusoidal, parabolic, or any arbitrary function. Common examples include machine tool feeds, cam mechanisms, and shoe-lasting machine linkages.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Tip:** Always state (1) the definition, (2) the mathematical form $\phi = f(\theta)$, and (3) one real-world example. This structure fetches full marks consistently.

---

### Question 2: State the Principle of Inversion as applied to mechanism synthesis. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

The **Principle of Inversion** states that the relative motion between the links of a mechanism remains unchanged if the entire mechanism is inverted about any one of its links, holding that link fixed in space.

In dimensional synthesis, this principle is used to convert an unknown moving pivot into a known fixed pivot. By inverting the mechanism about the output link, the problem of finding an unknown fixed pivot $O_4$ reduces to the much simpler problem of finding a point equidistant from two given positions of a moving pivot — i.e., the perpendicular bisector construction.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Tip:** Always state the principle clearly, then state *why* it is useful in synthesis (converts unknown moving pivot to known fixed pivot via perpendicular bisector).

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Part B questions are 14 marks each with internal choice. Each typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve both sub-parts thoroughly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): 3-Position Synthesis Using Freudenstein's Equation

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** State Freudenstein's equation and define each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a four-bar function generator to move a rocker through the following three positions, where the corresponding input crank positions are given:
- Position 1: $\theta_1 = 30°, \phi_1 = 45°$
- Position 2: $\theta_2 = 60°, \phi_2 = 75°$
- Position 3: $\theta_3 = 90°, \phi_3 = 100°$

Take input crank length $a = 40$ mm. Verify Grashof's condition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Freudenstein's Equation:**

$$K_1 \cos\phi - K_2 \cos\theta + K_3 = \cos(\theta - \phi)$$

where:
- $K_1 = d/a$, $K_2 = d/c$, $K_3 = \dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$ — Freudenstein constants (dimensionless, depend on link length ratios only).
- $\theta$ = input crank angle, $\phi$ = output rocker angle.
- $a, b, c, d$ = input crank, coupler, output rocker, and ground link lengths respectively.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute trig values for each position.**

| Position | $\cos\theta$ | $\cos\phi$ | $\cos(\theta - \phi)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\cos 30° = 0.8660$ | $\cos 45° = 0.7071$ | $\cos(-15°) = 0.9659$ |
| 2 | $\cos 60° = 0.5$ | $\cos 75° = 0.2588$ | $\cos(-15°) = 0.9659$ |
| 3 | $\cos 90° = 0$ | $\cos 100° = -0.1736$ | $\cos(-10°) = 0.9848$ |

**Step 2: Write Freudenstein's equation at each position:**

Position 1: $0.7071 K_1 - 0.8660 K_2 + K_3 = 0.9659$ → (Eq. 1)

Position 2: $0.2588 K_1 - 0.5 K_2 + K_3 = 0.9659$ → (Eq. 2)

Position 3: $-0.1736 K_1 - 0 K_2 + K_3 = 0.9848$ → (Eq. 3)

**Step 3: Solve the linear system.**

Subtract Eq. 2 from Eq. 1:
$$(0.7071 - 0.2588)K_1 - (0.8660 - 0.5)K_2 = 0$$
$$0.4483 K_1 - 0.3660 K_2 = 0$$
$$K_2 = 1.2249 K_1 \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Subtract Eq. 3 from Eq. 2:
$$(0.2588 + 0.1736)K_1 - 0.5 K_2 = 0.9659 - 0.9848$$
$$0.4324 K_1 - 0.5 K_2 = -0.0189 \quad \text{...(5)}$$

Substituting (4) into (5):
$$0.4324 K_1 - 0.5 (1.2249 K_1) = -0.0189$$
$$(0.4324 - 0.6124) K_1 = -0.0189$$
$$-0.1800 K_1 = -0.0189$$
$$K_1 = 0.1050$$

Then:
$$K_2 = 1.2249 \times 0.1050 = 0.1286$$
$$K_3 = 0.9848 + 0 = 0.9848 \text{ (from Eq. 3)}$$

**Step 4: Compute link lengths with $a = 40$ mm:**

$$d = K_1 \cdot a = 0.1050 \times 40 = 4.20 \text{ mm}$$
$$c = \frac{d}{K_2} = \frac{4.20}{0.1286} = 32.66 \text{ mm}$$

For $b$:
$$K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$$
$$0.9848 = \frac{1600 - b^2 + 1066.68 + 17.64}{2(40)(32.66)}$$
$$0.9848 = \frac{2684.32 - b^2}{2612.8}$$
$$2572.31 = 2684.32 - b^2$$
$$b^2 = 112.01$$
$$b = 10.58 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Grashof's Check:**

- $S = 4.20$ mm (shortest = $d$)
- $L = 40$ mm (longest = $a$)
- $S + L = 44.20$ mm
- $P + Q = 10.58 + 32.66 = 43.24$ mm
- $S + L = 44.20 > P + Q = 43.24$ → **Non-Grashof (Triple-Rocker)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to check Grashof's condition. This is worth 1 mark and is a *guaranteed* loss if missed. **Always verify Grashof!**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Sign errors in $\cos(\theta - \phi)$ when angles span quadrants. Verify with a calculator — and for $\cos(\theta - \phi)$ when $\theta < \phi$, the result is still positive (it's the cosine of a small negative angle).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): 2-Position Synthesis by Geometric Inversion

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Question, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the graphical procedure for 2-position synthesis of a four-bar function generator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-bar linkage is to be designed such that its output rocker $B_1B_2$ moves from a starting position to a second position. The two positions of point $B$ are: $B_1$ at $(0, 0)$ and $B_2$ at $(60, 30)$ mm, with the rocker link length being $80$ mm in both positions. Determine the location of the fixed pivot $O_4$. Also state the Grashof condition satisfied. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Graphical Procedure for 2-Position Synthesis:**

1. Draw the rigid output link (rocker) in its two prescribed positions $B_1C_1$ and $B_2C_2$.
2. Identify the moving pivot (typically $B$) whose path we need to determine.
3. Apply the principle of inversion by fixing the output link in its first position.
4. Mark point $B_1$ (fixed pivot candidate location in the inverted frame) and the inverted position $B_2$ (where the second position maps to in the inverted mechanism).
5. The fixed pivot $O_4$ must be equidistant from $B_1$ and $B_2$ (since rocker length $B_1O_4 = B_2O_4$).
6. Therefore, $O_4$ must lie on the **perpendicular bisector** of segment $B_1B_2$.
7. Choose a suitable $O_4$ on this bisector based on:
   - Required range of motion of the rocker
   - Ground clearance
   - Grashof's condition
8. Compute the link length $c$ (rocker) as the distance $B_1 O_4$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify coordinates.**
- $B_1 = (0, 0)$ mm
- $B_2 = (60, 30)$ mm
- Rocker length $c = 80$ mm (both positions)

**Step 2: Midpoint of $B_1B_2$:**

$$M = \left( \frac{0 + 60}{2}, \frac{0 + 30}{2} \right) = (30, 15) \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Direction of $B_1B_2$:**

$$\vec{B_1 B_2} = (60, 30), \quad \vert B_1 B_2 \vert = \sqrt{60^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{4500} = 67.08 \text{ mm}$$

Unit vector along $B_1B_2$: $\hat{u} = \left( \frac{60}{67.08}, \frac{30}{67.08} \right) = (0.8944, 0.4472)$

**Step 4: Perpendicular direction:**

$$\hat{n} = (-0.4472, 0.8944) \text{ or } (0.4472, -0.8944)$$

**Step 5: Distance from midpoint to $O_4$:**

Since $O_4$ lies on the perpendicular bisector at distance $h$ from $M$, and $O_4 B_1 = c = 80$ mm:

$$h^2 + \left( \frac{\vert B_1 B_2 \vert}{2} \right)^2 = c^2$$

$$h^2 + (33.54)^2 = 80^2$$

$$h^2 = 6400 - 1124.97 = 5275.03$$

$$h = 72.63 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Locate $O_4$:**

$$O_4 = M \pm h \cdot \hat{n}$$

Option 1: $O_4 = (30 - 72.63 \times 0.4472, \; 15 + 72.63 \times 0.8944) = (30 - 32.48, \; 15 + 64.96) = (-2.48, 79.96)$ mm

Option 2: $O_4 = (30 + 32.48, \; 15 - 64.96) = (62.48, -49.96)$ mm

**Step 7: Grashof's Check** (Assuming the input crank and coupler dimensions from a corresponding input pivot $O_2$ chosen appropriately):

Without the input crank specified, the Grashof check on the rocker-ground-coupler subsystem is performed when $a, b$ are also determined. The key principle: for continuous rotation, $S + L \leq P + Q$ must hold. With $c = 80$, $d$ would be the distance $O_2O_4$ once $O_2$ is chosen.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** In 2-position graphical problems, students often place $O_4$ on the segment $B_1B_2$ itself (the midpoint) instead of on the perpendicular bisector. This is wrong because the rocker length would then equal *half* the distance, not the prescribed 80 mm. **Always solve for $h$ first using the rocker length and the half-chord length.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** When two locations of $O_4$ exist, students often give only one. **Always state both candidates** and the geometric reason for selecting one (e.g., to ensure proper clearance or Grashof satisfaction).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam!**

### 🔑 Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- ✅ **Function generation** is a sub-problem of dimensional synthesis where $\phi = f(\theta)$.
- ✅ A 4-bar linkage has **4 design parameters** → can exactly satisfy **up to 4 precision points**.
- ✅ **2-position synthesis** is solved by the **perpendicular bisector method** using the **principle of inversion**.
- ✅ **3-position synthesis** can be solved by either:
  - Geometric: two successive 2-position inversions, intersecting circles.
  - Analytical: **Freudenstein's equation** with 3 equations and 3 unknowns ($K_1, K_2, K_3$).
- ✅ **Freudenstein's equation**: $K_1 \cos\phi - K_2 \cos\theta + K_3 = \cos(\theta - \phi)$.
- ✅ Always check **Grashof's condition** after computing link lengths: $S + L \leq P + Q$.

### 🔑 Common Traps and Pitfalls

- ⚠️ In Freudenstein's equation, the constants $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are **dimensionless ratios** — they are *not* link lengths. You must multiply by a chosen scale factor $a$ to get absolute lengths.
- ⚠️ $\cos(\theta - \phi)$ is **not** equal to $\cos\theta \cos\phi + \sin\theta \sin\phi$ subtracted; it equals $\cos\theta \cos\phi + \sin\theta \sin\phi$. Sign errors here lose 2-3 marks.
- ⚠️ The **perpendicular bisector** of $B_1B_2$ is a *line*, not a point. The location of $O_4$ on this line is determined by the **rocker length**.
- ⚠️ In 3-position graphical synthesis, you get **two circles** intersecting at **up to 2 points** — both are valid candidates; choose based on design constraints.
- ⚠️ For 3-position analytical synthesis, after finding $K_1, K_2, K_3$, **always verify that the resulting mechanism can physically reach all three positions** (no lock-up at intermediate angles).

### 🔑 High-Yield Mnemonics

- **"2-Pos = Bisector; 3-Pos = 3 Eqs; 4-Pos = Unique; 5+ = Optimize"**
- **"Invert, Bisect, Choose"** — the 2-position synthesis mantra.
- **"Freudenstein's Three K's: $K_1$, $K_2$, $K_3$ — solve 3 equations to find 3 K's, then scale by $a$."**

### 🔑 Examiner's Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme)

| Sub-part | Typical Marks | What Examiners Look For |
|---|---|---|
| Definition / Statement | 2-3 | Clear, syllabus-aligned wording |
| Diagram / Sketch | 2-3 | Neatness, proper labeling, conventions |
| Step-by-step working | 4-5 | Each step shown explicitly, no skipping |
| Final answer with units | 1-2 | Correct numerical values, correct units (mm) |
| Grashof verification | 1-2 | Always checked in any synthesis problem |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU-Specific Tip:** In the 2024 scheme, OBE (Outcome-Based Education) emphasizes Course Outcomes (COs). Map your answers to the relevant CO. For Module 2 Function Generation, the primary CO is typically: *"CO2: Apply kinematic synthesis principles to design mechanisms for prescribed motion."* Always frame your final summary in terms of this CO.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overlay Method, Freudenstein's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT403 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overlay Method, Freudenstein's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Overlay Method (Klein's Construction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Overlay Method**, also called **Klein's Construction**, is a graphical-analytical synthesis technique for designing a four-bar linkage that guides a rigid body (the *coupler*) through a finite number of prescribed positions in a plane. The procedure is rooted in **Burmester's Theory** and uses the **Circle Point Circle (CPC)** and the **Center Point Circle (CPCp)** of the moving and fixed planes, which are superimposed ("overlaid") on a common sheet to locate the fixed pivots **O₂** and **O₄**.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English
Imagine a magician's wand that must sweep through **three specific points** in the air (these are your "precision positions"). The wand is the coupler link of a four-bar mechanism. You need to find two fixed points in the room — let's call them the *ceiling hook* (fixed pivot O₂) and the *wall hook* (fixed pivot O₄) — such that a chain of two rigid arms attached to these hooks can swing the wand tip exactly through all three prescribed points. The **overlay method** is the geometric trick by which the engineer locates those two hooks by superimposing the moving wand's traced loci (drawn on a transparent sheet) over the fixed room geometry (drawn on a base sheet).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Overlay Method is the standard prescribed approach in PCRAT403 for **three-position synthesis** of four-bar mechanisms for both **function generation** and **path generation** (rigid-body guidance).

---

## 1.2 Freudenstein's Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Freudenstein's Equation** (developed by Ferdinand Freudenstein, 1954) is the exact closed-form transcendental equation that relates the **input angle (θ₂)** and the **output angle (θ₄)** of a four-bar linkage purely in terms of the four fixed link lengths **a, b, c, d**. It is the most fundamental analytical tool in the **dimensional synthesis** of four-bar mechanisms for prescribed function generation.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English
Think of a four-bar linkage as a pair of scissors with one fixed handle. If you know the lengths of all four links, the scissors open and close in a **completely determined** way. Freudenstein's equation is the *mathematical fingerprint* of that opening-and-closing motion — it tells you, for any given input angle, exactly what the output angle must be. It is to the four-bar linkage what a *transfer function* is to an electronic circuit.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive suspensions** — relating wheel motion to chassis displacement.
- **Pump & compressor drives** — converting rotary motion into non-linear reciprocation.
- **Robot joints** — inverse kinematics of planar manipulators.
- **Function-generating mechanisms** in analog computing and cam-replacement applications.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Grashof condition** (s + l ≤ p + q) does **not** appear in Freudenstein's equation directly, but the link lengths **a, b, c, d** must satisfy it for the linkage to have continuous rotation (crank-rocker, double-crank, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Overlay Method — Underlying Theory

### 2.1.1 Burmester's Theorem (Foundational Pillar)
Burmester's theorem states that for a rigid body to occupy **five** prescribed positions, the locus of all possible moving pivots (B) of the input crank is the **Circle-Point Curve**, and the locus of the corresponding fixed pivot (A) is the **Center-Point Curve**. For the restricted case of **three prescribed positions**, both curves reduce to **a single circle each**, dramatically simplifying synthesis.

### 2.1.2 The Three Geometric Loci
For three positions (1, 2, 3) of the moving coupler:

| Locus | Symbol | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Circle Point Circle (Moving)** | $CPC_m$ | Circle passing through $B_1, B_2, B_3$ — locus of moving pivots B. |
| **Center Point Circle (Fixed)** | $CPC_f$ | Circle on which the fixed pivot $O_2$ lies such that the radius $\overline{O_2 B_i} = $ input crank length. |
| **Circle Point Circle (Other Side)** | $CPC'_m$ | A second solution branch — locus of moving pivots for the OTHER four-bar configuration. |
| **Center Point Circle (Other Side)** | $CPC'_f$ | Corresponding fixed-pivot circle. |

### 2.1.3 Klein's Construction (Step-by-Step Logic)
- **Why does it work?** Because the moving pivot B is rigidly attached to the coupler, it must be a *single physical point*. Therefore the CPC drawn through $B_1, B_2, B_3$ in the **moving plane** and the CPCp drawn through the corresponding centers in the **fixed plane** must be **coincident** when the two planes are overlaid.
- **How to find the pivot:**
  1. Plot positions 1, 2, 3 of the moving body in the **moving plane** (transparent sheet).
  2. Compute the **circumcircle** of $B_1, B_2, B_3$ — this is the candidate $CPC_m$.
  3. The center of this circle is the **moving pivot B**; the **fixed pivot O₂** lies on the perpendicular bisectors of chords $B_1B_2$ and $B_2B_3$.
  4. Repeat for the output side using $C_1, C_2, C_3$ to obtain $O_4$ and C.
  5. Overlay the transparent sheet on the base sheet — adjust until the two loci align — read off pivot coordinates.

### 2.1.4 Branch-Point (Folding Position) Logic
Three precision points yield **two distinct four-bar solutions**:
- **Open configuration** (one side of the moving plane) — pivots $O_2$ and $O_4$ on one side.
- **Crossed configuration** (other side) — pivots on the opposite side, giving a different mechanism geometry.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test: *"Why are there two solutions to a three-position synthesis problem?"* — Answer: Because the locus of compatible (B, O₂) pairs degenerates to **two circle-point circles** in the three-position case, one for each folding branch of the four-bar mechanism.

---

## 2.2 Freudenstein's Equation — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Vector Loop Closure (Geometric Foundation)
A four-bar mechanism ABCD satisfies the **loop-closure equation**:
$$\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{d} + \vec{c}$$

Resolving into Cartesian components (with d along the x-axis):
$$a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4$$
$$a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4$$

where **θ₃ is eliminated** to yield a direct relationship between θ₂ and θ₄.

### 2.2.2 Coefficient Definitions
The three **Freudenstein coefficients** $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are **dimensionless groups** of link lengths:

| Coefficient | Expression | Role in Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $K_1$ | $\dfrac{d}{a}$ | Coupling of output angle $\theta_4$ |
| $K_2$ | $\dfrac{d}{c}$ | Coupling of input angle $\theta_2$ |
| $K_3$ | $\dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$ | Geometric / constant term |

> [!NOTE]
> The denominator **2ac** emerges naturally from eliminating the coupler angle by squaring and adding the loop equations. Note: the coupler length **b** appears in the constant term but **not** as a denominator.

### 2.2.3 Standard Functional Form
$$K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$

### 2.2.4 Alternate (Solved-for-θ₄) Form
Rearranging for the output angle:
$$\frac{\cos\theta_4 - \cos\theta_2}{\cos\theta_2 - 1} = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2b(c + d - a\cos\theta_2)}$$

This alternate form is used when **b is the unknown** to be solved iteratively.

### 2.2.5 Three-Precision-Point Synthesis Logic
- For function generation, three (θ₂, θ₄) pairs are prescribed.
- Each pair is substituted into Freudenstein's equation.
- Resulting in **3 linear equations in 3 unknowns** (K₁, K₂, K₃) — once normalized by **ground link d**, this becomes a system in (a, c, b).
- One link (usually d) is chosen as a *scale reference*; the other three are solved for.

### 2.2.6 Why It Matters in Real Engineering
| Application | Use of Freudenstein's Equation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive suspension** | Predict wheel camber vs. steering input. |
| **Function generator (computing mechanisms)** | Replace cams/camshafts for high-speed, no-sliding-contact output. |
| **Walking robot legs** | Couple hip-knee trajectories without sensors. |
| **Pumping mechanisms** | Drive a non-uniform piston stroke from uniform crank rotation. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $K_1 = \dfrac{d}{a}$ | First Freudenstein coefficient | dimensionless |
| 2 | $K_2 = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | Second Freudenstein coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K_3 = \dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$ | Third Freudenstein coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$ | Standard Freudenstein equation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\cos\theta_4 - \cos\theta_2}{\cos\theta_2 - 1} = \dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2b(c + d - a\cos\theta_2)}$ | Alternate form (solved for $b$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | Grashof: $s + \ell \le p + q$ | Necessary condition for crank rotation | lengths |
| 7 | Burmester CPC radius: $R_{CPC} = \dfrac{\overline{B_1 B_3}}{2\sin(2\alpha)}$ | Radius of circle through $B_1, B_2, B_3$ | length |
| 8 | Branch-point: $B_p = $ intersection of $CPC$ and $CPC'$ | Gives the **folding position** | length |
| 9 | $\tan(\theta_2/2) = \dfrac{\sin\theta_2}{1 + \cos\theta_2}$ | Half-angle identity (used in Weiszfeld substitutions) | dimensionless |
| 10 | Vector loop: $\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{d} + \vec{c}$ | Geometric closure of four-bar | length |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Freudenstein's Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumed Notation:** Four-bar linkage $O_2ABO_4$ with link lengths $a = \overline{O_2A}$, $b = \overline{AB}$, $c = \overline{BO_4}$, $d = \overline{O_4O_2}$. Input angle $\theta_2$ (link $a$), output angle $\theta_4$ (link $c$). Coupler angle $\theta_3$ (link $b$).

### Step 1 — Vector Loop Closure
Tracing the loop starting from $O_2$ and returning to $O_2$:
$$\vec{O_2 A} + \vec{AB} = \vec{O_2 O_4} + \vec{O_4 B}$$

Substituting link vectors:
$$\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{d} + \vec{c}$$

### Step 2 — Resolve into Cartesian Components
With the ground link $d$ taken along the x-axis, and each link making the appropriate angle with x-axis:
$$a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 \quad \cdots (i)$$
$$a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = 0 + c\sin\theta_4 \quad \cdots (ii)$$

### Step 3 — Isolate the Coupler Terms
Rearranging (i) and (ii) to gather $b\cos\theta_3$ and $b\sin\theta_3$:
$$b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2 \quad \cdots (iii)$$
$$b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2 \quad \cdots (iv)$$

### Step 4 — Square and Add to Eliminate θ₃
$$b^2\cos^2\theta_3 + b^2\sin^2\theta_3 = (d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^2 + (c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^2$$

Since $\cos^2\theta_3 + \sin^2\theta_3 = 1$, LHS reduces to $b^2$:
$$b^2 = (d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^2 + (c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^2$$

### Step 5 — Expand the Right-Hand Side
Expanding the first squared term:
$$(d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^2 = d^2 + c^2\cos^2\theta_4 + a^2\cos^2\theta_2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4$$

Expanding the second squared term:
$$(c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^2 = c^2\sin^2\theta_4 + a^2\sin^2\theta_2 - 2ac\sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$$

Adding both:
$$b^2 = d^2 + c^2(\cos^2\theta_4 + \sin^2\theta_4) + a^2(\cos^2\theta_2 + \sin^2\theta_2) + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac(\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4)$$

### Step 6 — Apply Pythagorean and Cosine-Difference Identities
Using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$:
$$b^2 = d^2 + c^2 + a^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$

Using the cosine difference identity $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$.

### Step 7 — Isolate the Cosine-Difference Term
Moving the cosine-difference term to one side:
$$2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - b^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2$$

### Step 8 — Divide Through by 2ac
$$\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} + \frac{d}{a}\cos\theta_4 - \frac{d}{c}\cos\theta_2$$

### Step 9 — Identify the Freudenstein Coefficients
Recognising the dimensionless groups:
$$K_1 \equiv \frac{d}{a}, \qquad K_2 \equiv \frac{d}{c}, \qquad K_3 \equiv \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$$

### Step 10 — Write the Final Standard Form

$$\boxed{\,K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)\,}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the unique closed-form relationship between input $\theta_2$ and output $\theta_4$ for a four-bar of given dimensions. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Three-Precision-Point Synthesis Procedure (Analytical Matrix)

### Procedure to Design a Four-Bar for Three Pairs of Angles $(\theta_{2,i}, \theta_{4,i})$, $i = 1, 2, 3$:

| Step | Action | Mathematical Operation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Fix the ground link | Choose a value for $d$ (scale reference). |
| 2 | Apply Freudenstein's equation at each precision point | Substitute $(\theta_{2,1}, \theta_{4,1}), (\theta_{2,2}, \theta_{4,2}), (\theta_{2,3}, \theta_{4,3})$ into the equation. |
| 3 | Obtain 3 linear equations in $K_1, K_2, K_3$ | Treat $K_1, K_2, K_3$ as the three unknowns. |
| 4 | Solve the linear system | Use Cramer's rule / matrix inversion. |
| 5 | Recover physical link lengths | $a = d/K_1, \;\; c = d/K_2, \;\; b = \sqrt{a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2ac K_3}$ |
| 6 | Verify Grashof condition | $s + \ell \le p + q$ for continuous motion. |
| 7 | Check for assembly defects | Branch defect, order defect, etc. |

### Worked Example — Setup for KTU-style Numerical

**Problem:** Design a four-bar mechanism for three precision points where:

| Precision Point $i$ | $\theta_{2,i}$ (deg) | $\theta_{4,i}$ (deg) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 30° | 60° |
| 2 | 60° | 100° |
| 3 | 90° | 130° |

**Given:** $d = 50$ mm (ground link, chosen as scale).

**Solution (concise):**
At each precision point, write Freudenstein's equation:
$$K_1\cos\theta_{4,i} - K_2\cos\theta_{2,i} + K_3 = \cos(\theta_{2,i} - \theta_{4,i})$$

Substituting and forming a matrix:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \cos 60° & -\cos 30° & 1 \\ \cos 100° & -\cos 60° & 1 \\ \cos 130° & -\cos 90° & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} K_1 \\ K_2 \\ K_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(30° - 60°) \\ \cos(60° - 100°) \\ \cos(90° - 130°) \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving the linear system yields $(K_1, K_2, K_3)$, and the link lengths follow as $a = d/K_1$, $c = d/K_2$, $b = \sqrt{a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2ac K_3}$.

> [!NOTE]
> For an exhaustive step-by-step numerical solution, the student should perform the matrix inversion using a calculator and substitute back. The above template is **exactly** the KTU 14-mark question format.

---

## 3.3 Alternate Form Derivation (Weiszfeld-style)

Starting from the standard form:
$$K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$

Substitute $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$:
$$K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$$

Group terms in $\cos\theta_4$ and rearrange:
$$(K_1 - \cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_4 = K_2\cos\theta_2 - K_3 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4$$

Using the identity $\sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4 = \cos\theta_2 - \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) - \cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4$ ... (a routine manipulation) and solving for the cosine terms yields the alternate form. This form is used when the **coupler length $b$ is the unknown** to be determined iteratively.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Four-Bar Linkage — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef fixed fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ffd700,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef moving fill:#ffd700,stroke:#1f2a44,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2a44
    classDef coupler fill:#3a7ca5,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef label fill:#ffffff,stroke:#888888,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2a44

    O2["FIXED PIVOT O2 GROUND d"]:::fixed
    O4["FIXED PIVOT O4 GROUND d"]:::fixed
    A["MOVING PIVOT A INPUT CRANK a LENGTH"]:::moving
    B["MOVING PIVOT B COUPLER END b LENGTH"]:::moving
    TH2["ANGLE THETA 2 INPUT ROTATION"]:::label
    TH4["ANGLE THETA 4 OUTPUT ROTATION"]:::label
    TH3["COUPLER ANGLE THETA 3 ELIMINATED"]:::label

    O2 --- A
    A --- B
    B --- O4
    O4 --- O2
    O2 --- TH2
    O4 --- TH4
    A --- TH3
```

---

## 4.2 Overlay Method (Klein Construction) — Procedural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f2a44,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1f2a44
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#856404
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#155724

    S1["STEP 1 PLOT 3 POSITIONS OF MOVING BODY IN MOVING PLANE TRANSPARENT SHEET"]:::step
    S2["STEP 2 MARK MOVING PIVOT CANDIDATES B1 B2 B3 ON COUPLER"]:::step
    S3["STEP 3 CONSTRUCT CIRCLE POINT CIRCLE CPC THROUGH B1 B2 B3"]:::step
    S4["STEP 4 CONSTRUCT PERPENDICULAR BISECTORS OF B1B2 AND B2B3"]:::step
    S5["STEP 5 INTERSECTION GIVES FIXED PIVOT O2"]:::output
    S6["STEP 6 REPEAT FOR OUTPUT SIDE TO GET C1 C2 C3 AND O4"]:::step
    S7["STEP 7 OVERLAY MOVING SHEET ON FIXED BASE PLANE"]:::step
    DEC["DECISION BRANCH OPEN CONFIG OR CROSSED CONFIG"]:::decision
    S8["STEP 8 READ OFF PIVOT COORDINATES O2 AND O4 ON BASE SHEET"]:::output
    S9["STEP 9 VERIFY GRASHOF CONDITION AND DRAW MECHANISM"]:::output

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7 --> DEC
    DEC -->|OPEN| S8
    DEC -->|CROSSED| S8
    S8 --> S9
```

---

## 4.3 Freudenstein's Equation — Derivation & Synthesis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0a58ca
    classDef process fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#856404
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#155724
    classDef warning fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,stroke-width:2px,color:#842029

    IN1["INPUT FOUR LINK LENGTHS a b c d"]:::input
    IN2["INPUT THREE PAIRS OF ANGLES TH2 AND TH4"]:::input
    P1["PROCESS VECTOR LOOP CLOSURE a plus b equals d plus c"]:::process
    P2["PROCESS RESOLVE INTO X AND Y COMPONENTS"]:::process
    P3["PROCESS ELIMINATE COUPLER ANGLE TH3 SQUARE AND ADD"]:::process
    P4["PROCESS APPLY COSINE DIFFERENCE IDENTITY"]:::process
    OUT["OUTPUT FREUDENSTEIN EQUATION K1 cos TH4 minus K2 cos TH2 plus K3 equals cos TH2 minus TH4"]:::output
    SYN1["SYNTHESIS STAGE SUBSTITUTE 3 PRECISION PAIRS"]:::process
    SYN2["SYNTHESIS STAGE SOLVE 3 EQUATIONS FOR K1 K2 K3"]:::process
    SYN3["SYNTHESIS STAGE RECOVER a b c FROM COEFFICIENTS"]:::process
    W1["WARNING CHECK GRASHOF CONDITION S plus L le p plus q"]:::warning

    IN1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> OUT
    IN2 --> SYN1 --> SYN2 --> SYN3
    SYN3 --> W1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (SHORT ANSWER)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Cycle 1]**

**State Freudenstein's Equation for a four-bar mechanism. What are the three Freudenstein coefficients? How are they related to the link lengths?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
Freudenstein's Equation expresses the exact relationship between the input angle $\theta_2$ and the output angle $\theta_4$ of a four-bar linkage in terms of its link lengths:

$$K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$

The three Freudenstein coefficients are:
1. $K_1 = \dfrac{d}{a}$ — dimensionless ratio of ground to input link.
2. $K_2 = \dfrac{d}{c}$ — dimensionless ratio of ground to output link.
3. $K_3 = \dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$ — geometric constant involving all four link lengths.

The coefficients are **dimensionless groups** because they relate ratios of like quantities (lengths), and the equation is a pure transcendental identity between trigonometric functions of the angles. **(3 Marks — 1 for equation, 1 for coefficients, 1 for physical interpretation)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Explain the principle of the Overlay Method (Klein's Construction) for the synthesis of a four-bar mechanism for three prescribed positions of a rigid body.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
The Overlay Method is a graphical synthesis technique based on **Burmester's Theorem**, applied for the special case of **three precision positions** of a moving rigid body. The principle rests on the following logic:

1. The **moving pivot B** (joint between input crank and coupler) must be a single physical point attached to the moving body, so the locus of B over all three positions lies on a single circle — the **Circle Point Circle (CPC)** in the moving plane.
2. The **fixed pivot O₂** of the input crank must lie on a corresponding **Center Point Circle (CPCp)** in the fixed plane.
3. Since both circles share the same geometric relationship (one is the locus of the centre, the other of the circumference), they must be **coincident** when the moving and fixed planes are overlaid (superimposed on a common sheet).
4. Adjusting the overlay reveals the candidate positions for the pivots, which are then read off directly from the intersection geometry.

The method gives **two solution branches** (open and crossed four-bar configurations) corresponding to the two valid ways of folding the coupler. **(3 Marks — 1 each for principle, loci, and overlay logic)**

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (FULL ESE PATTERN WITH INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Cycle 2]**

#### (a) Derive Freudenstein's Equation for a four-bar linkage ABCD, with the ground link taken along the x-axis. Clearly state the assumed notation. **(7 Marks)**

#### (b) For the three precision points given in the table below, determine the link lengths of a four-bar mechanism using Freudenstein's equation. Take the ground link length $d = 50$ mm. Verify the Grashof condition. **(7 Marks)**

| Precision Point $i$ | $\theta_{2,i}$ (deg) | $\theta_{4,i}$ (deg) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 30 | 80 |
| 2 | 60 | 120 |
| 3 | 90 | 160 |

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Notation:** Link $a$ = input crank (length $\overline{O_2 A}$), Link $b$ = coupler, Link $c$ = output (length $\overline{O_4 B}$), Link $d$ = ground (length $\overline{O_2 O_4}$). Angles $\theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$ measured from the x-axis.

**Step 1 — Vector loop:** $\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{d} + \vec{c}$.

**Step 2 — Components:**
$$a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4$$
$$a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4$$

**Step 3 — Isolate coupler terms:** $b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2$, and $b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2$.

**Step 4 — Square and add:** $b^2 = (d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^2 + (c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^2$.

**Step 5 — Expand:** $b^2 = a^2 + c^2 + d^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$.

**Step 6 — Solve for the cosine term:**
$$2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - b^2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2$$

**Step 7 — Final form** (with $K_1 = d/a$, $K_2 = d/c$, $K_3 = (a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2)/(2ac)$):
$$\boxed{K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)}$$

**(Full credit: 7 marks — 1 for notation, 2 for vector loop + components, 2 for algebraic manipulation, 1 for coefficient definition, 1 for final equation.)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Substitute each precision point into Freudenstein's equation:**

For Point 1 ($\theta_2 = 30°, \theta_4 = 80°$):
$$K_1\cos 80° - K_2\cos 30° + K_3 = \cos(30° - 80°)$$
$$0.1736 K_1 - 0.8660 K_2 + K_3 = \cos(-50°) = 0.6428$$

For Point 2 ($\theta_2 = 60°, \theta_4 = 120°$):
$$K_1\cos 120° - K_2\cos 60° + K_3 = \cos(60° - 120°)$$
$$-0.5000 K_1 - 0.5000 K_2 + K_3 = \cos(-60°) = 0.5000$$

For Point 3 ($\theta_2 = 90°, \theta_4 = 160°$):
$$K_1\cos 160° - K_2\cos 90° + K_3 = \cos(90° - 160°)$$
$$-0.9397 K_1 - 0.0000 K_2 + K_3 = \cos(-70°) = 0.3420$$

**Step 2 — Write the linear system in matrix form:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0.1736 & -0.8660 & 1 \\ -0.5000 & -0.5000 & 1 \\ -0.9397 & 0.0000 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} K_1 \\ K_2 \\ K_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6428 \\ 0.5000 \\ 0.3420 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Solve by elimination.** Subtract Equation 2 from Equation 1:
$$0.6736 K_1 - 0.3660 K_2 = 0.1428 \quad \cdots (iv)$$

Subtract Equation 3 from Equation 1:
$$1.1133 K_1 - 0.8660 K_2 = 0.3008 \quad \cdots (v)$$

Solve (iv) and (v) simultaneously: multiply (iv) by (0.8660/0.3660) = 2.3661:
$$1.5942 K_1 - 0.8660 K_2 = 0.3379 \quad \cdots (iv')$$

Subtract (v) from (iv'):
$$0.4809 K_1 = 0.0371 \;\Rightarrow\; K_1 \approx 0.0771$$

Back-substitute into (iv): $0.6736(0.0771) - 0.3660 K_2 = 0.1428$:
$$0.0519 - 0.3660 K_2 = 0.1428 \;\Rightarrow\; K_2 \approx -0.2483$$

Back-substitute into Equation 1: $0.1736(0.0771) - (-0.2483)(0.8660) + K_3 = 0.6428$:
$$0.0134 + 0.2150 + K_3 = 0.6428 \;\Rightarrow\; K_3 \approx 0.4144$$

**Step 4 — Recover link lengths:**
$$a = d/K_1 = 50/0.0771 \approx 648.5 \text{ mm}$$
$$c = d/K_2 = 50/(-0.2483) \approx -201.4 \text{ mm (sign indicates orientation, take } c \approx 201.4 \text{ mm)}$$

$$b = \sqrt{a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2ac K_3} = \sqrt{648.5^2 + 201.4^2 + 50^2 - 2(648.5)(201.4)(0.4144)}$$
$$b = \sqrt{420553 + 40562 + 2500 - 108257} = \sqrt{355358} \approx 596.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Grashof check:** Shortest link $= d = 50$ mm, Longest link $= a = 648.5$ mm. Sum of others: $b + c = 596.0 + 201.4 = 797.4$ mm. Since $50 + 596.0 = 646.0 \le 648.5 + 201.4 = 849.9$ ✓ — Grashof condition **satisfied**. This is a **crank-rocker** (since shortest link is the ground).

**(Full credit: 7 marks — 1 each for substitutions, 1 for matrix setup, 2 for solution, 1 for link recovery, 1 for Grashof check.)**

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — KTU Model Paper]**

#### (a) Explain in detail the Overlay Method (Klein's Construction) for the synthesis of a four-bar mechanism to guide a rigid body through three prescribed positions. Use neat sketches / flow diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

#### (b) A four-bar mechanism has link lengths $a = 100$ mm, $b = 200$ mm, $c = 150$ mm, $d = 180$ mm. Using Freudenstein's equation, determine the output angle $\theta_4$ when the input angle $\theta_2 = 45°$. Take $\theta_2 = 60°$ as the reference where $\theta_4 = 90°$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Statement of the Problem:** A four-bar mechanism has its coupler (rigid body) prescribed to occupy three specific positions in the plane. We need to determine the locations of the two fixed pivots $O_2$ and $O_4$ and the two moving pivots $A$ and $B$ such that the four-bar can guide the coupler through all three positions.

**Burmester's Foundation:** Burmester's theorem states that for five positions, the locus of all possible moving pivots is a curve (the *circle-point curve*) and the locus of fixed pivots is another curve (the *center-point curve*). For **three positions**, these curves degenerate to **single circles**, making graphical synthesis feasible.

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

1. **Draw the three positions** of the moving body on a transparent sheet ("moving plane"). Label key features in each position (1, 2, 3).

2. **Identify a candidate point B** on the coupler (this is the moving pivot shared with the input crank). The same point appears at $B_1, B_2, B_3$ in the three positions.

3. **Construct the Circle Point Circle (CPC):** Draw a circle passing through $B_1, B_2, B_3$. Its centre is found as the intersection of perpendicular bisectors of chords $B_1 B_2$ and $B_2 B_3$. This CPC is the locus of the moving pivot B.

4. **Construct the Center Point Circle (CPCp):** The fixed pivot $O_2$ lies on the **perpendicular bisectors of $B_1 B_2$** and **$B_2 B_3$**. The CPCp is the locus of $O_2$ such that the radius equals the chosen input crank length $a$.

5. **Repeat for the output side:** Pick another point C on the coupler. Construct the corresponding CPC (through $C_1, C_2, C_3$) and CPCp (locus of $O_4$) for the output link $c$.

6. **Overlay operation:** Superimpose the transparent "moving sheet" on the base "fixed sheet" such that the CPC of the input side (moving) aligns with the corresponding CPCp of the input side (fixed). The point of coincidence identifies B and $O_2$ directly.

7. **Two branches:** A second alignment is possible (the **crossed configuration**), giving an alternative four-bar geometry.

8. **Final mechanism:** Connect $O_2 A B O_4$ with the link lengths determined, and verify the Grashof condition $s + \ell \le p + q$ to confirm the type of mechanism.

**Sketch Required:** Student must draw three superimposed positions, the CPC, the CPCp, the perpendicular bisectors, and the resulting four-bar linkage. **(7 Marks — 1 each for problem statement, Burmester basis, CPC construction, CPCp construction, overlay logic, two branches, final verification.)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $a = 100$ mm, $b = 200$ mm, $c = 150$ mm, $d = 180$ mm. Reference: at $\theta_2 = 60°$, $\theta_4 = 90°$. Find $\theta_4$ when $\theta_2 = 45°$.

**Step 1 — Compute Freudenstein coefficients:**
$$K_1 = d/a = 180/100 = 1.8$$
$$K_2 = d/c = 180/150 = 1.2$$
$$K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac} = \frac{10000 - 40000 + 22500 + 32400}{2(100)(150)} = \frac{24900}{30000} = 0.8300$$

**Step 2 — Use the reference position to verify the equation:**

At $\theta_2 = 60°, \theta_4 = 90°$:
LHS $= 1.8 \cos 90° - 1.2 \cos 60° + 0.8300 = 0 - 0.6 + 0.8300 = 0.2300$
RHS $= \cos(60° - 90°) = \cos(-30°) = 0.8660$

The equation is **not satisfied**, which indicates that the given reference angle does not match the specified link geometry. This is a *consistency check failure* — in a real KTU exam, students should report this discrepancy.

**Step 3 — Procedural substitution** (assuming the question expects direct substitution, ignoring the inconsistency):

Plug $\theta_2 = 45°$ into Freudenstein's equation:
$$1.8 \cos\theta_4 - 1.2 \cos 45° + 0.8300 = \cos(45° - \theta_4)$$
$$1.8 \cos\theta_4 - 0.8485 + 0.8300 = \cos(45° - \theta_4)$$
$$1.8 \cos\theta_4 - 0.0185 = \cos(45° - \theta_4)$$

**Step 4 — Expand RHS using cosine difference identity:**
$$\cos(45° - \theta_4) = \cos 45° \cos\theta_4 + \sin 45° \sin\theta_4 = 0.7071 \cos\theta_4 + 0.7071 \sin\theta_4$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into a single trig form:**
$$1.8 \cos\theta_4 - 0.7071 \cos\theta_4 - 0.7071 \sin\theta_4 = 0.0185$$
$$1.0929 \cos\theta_4 - 0.7071 \sin\theta_4 = 0.0185$$

Let $R = \sqrt{1.0929^2 + 0.7071^2} = \sqrt{1.1944 + 0.5000} = \sqrt{1.6944} = 1.3017$

Let $\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.7071/1.0929) = \tan^{-1}(0.6470) = 32.89°$

So the equation becomes:
$$R \cos(\theta_4 + \phi) = 0.0185$$
$$\cos(\theta_4 + 32.89°) = 0.0185 / 1.3017 = 0.01422$$

$$\theta_4 + 32.89° = \cos^{-1}(0.01422) = 89.18° \text{ or } 270.82°$$

$$\theta_4 = 56.29° \text{ or } 237.93°$$

**Step 6 — Physical branch selection:** Take the principal solution: $\theta_4 \approx \mathbf{56.3°}$.

**(Full credit: 7 marks — 1 for K-coefficients, 1 for verification step, 2 for algebraic substitution, 2 for trigonometric solution, 1 for branch selection.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors in K-coefficients:** Students often write $K_1 = a/d$ instead of $d/a$. Always check by substituting a known symmetric case (e.g., rhombus linkage where all lengths are equal).
> 2. **Forgetting the cosine-difference sign:** The right-hand side of Freudenstein's equation is $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$, NOT $\cos(\theta_4 - \theta_2)$ — both are numerically equal due to cosine being even, but in non-symmetric extensions this distinction matters.
> 3. **Confusing Burmester's curves with circles:** For FIVE positions, the loci are curves; only for THREE positions do they reduce to circles. Many students wrongly draw curves when circles are required.
> 4. **Missing the two-branch solution:** The overlay method ALWAYS gives two configurations (open and crossed). A complete answer must mention both.
> 5. **Grashof verification omitted:** A synthesized mechanism that violates Grashof's law cannot move continuously — a -1 mark penalty is common if this check is skipped.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** All link lengths must be in the same unit (typically mm). A 0.5-mark penalty is standard for mixing units in the K-coefficient computation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and revise 10 minutes before the exam:**

- [ ] **Freudenstein's Equation** is the **only** closed-form transcendental relation between $\theta_2$ and $\theta_4$ of a four-bar linkage — no approximations.
- [ ] The **three coefficients** are: $K_1 = d/a$, $K_2 = d/c$, $K_3 = (a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2)/(2ac)$ — all **dimensionless**.
- [ ] The **denominator of $K_3$** is $2ac$, NOT $2bc$ (the coupler length $b$ appears only in the numerator).
- [ ] The **vector loop** must be drawn *first* — never write Freudenstein's equation without deriving it from the loop.
- [ ] The **Overlay Method** is a special case of Burmester's theory valid only for **three precision points** (five points give curves).
- [ ] Each three-position synthesis gives **two solutions** (open and crossed four-bar).
- [ ] **Grashof's condition** $s + \ell \le p + q$ MUST be checked after every synthesis — it determines mechanism type.
- [ ] The **moving pivot B** lies on the Circle Point Circle (CPC) in the moving plane; the **fixed pivot O₂** lies on the Center Point Circle (CPCp) in the fixed plane.
- [ ] Perpendicular bisectors of $B_1 B_2$ and $B_2 B_3$ intersect at the center of the CPC.
- [ ] For the **K-coefficients**: $a$ is the **input crank**, $c$ is the **output link**, $d$ is the **ground** — getting this wrong by swapping $a$ and $c$ invalidates the entire problem.
- [ ] The **alternate form** of Freudenstein's equation is used when the coupler length $b$ is the unknown to be solved.
- [ ] For KTU's typical 14-mark question, students should **show all matrix steps** — partial credit is awarded for setup even if the final numerical answer has a small error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT403 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Gears

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):** A **gear** is a rotating machine element having cut teeth (or cogs) of identical profile, designed to mesh with another toothed element to transmit motion and power between rotating shafts without slip, maintaining a definite velocity ratio.

The teeth are equally spaced around the periphery of the gear body (the **rim**), and the geometry of meshing teeth is governed by the fundamental **law of gearing**, which states:

> **Law of Gearing (Fundamental Law of Toothed Gearing):** The common normal at the point of contact between a pair of mating teeth must always pass through the **pitch point**, which lies on the line joining the centers of the two gears. This ensures a *constant angular velocity ratio* during engagement.

Mathematically, the law of gearing is expressed as:

$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{r_2}{r_1} = \text{constant}$$

where $N$ is the number of teeth and $r$ is the pitch circle radius.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Think of a Bicycle's Gear System:** Imagine two coins with saw-tooth edges pushed together on a table so their teeth interlock. As you roll one coin, the other must roll in the opposite direction. A gear is precisely this concept — a precision-engineered version of the same idea. The teeth act like tiny levers that push each other, ensuring the rotation of one wheel **forces** a corresponding rotation in the other wheel *without any slipping*. This is why a small gear driving a large gear produces a *slower but stronger* output — much like the lowest gear on a bicycle makes pedalling easier up a hill, but you cover less ground per pedal revolution.

### 1.3 Why Gears? — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are gears indispensable in mechanical engineering?**
> - They provide a **positive drive** (no slip) unlike belt or rope drives.
> - They offer a **constant velocity ratio**.
> - They can transmit **very high torque** within a compact envelope.
> - They enable **direction reversal**, **speed reduction/amplification**, and **motion between non-parallel shafts** (using bevel, worm, etc.).

The three fundamental engineering quantities related to a gear are:
- **Pitch Circle Diameter ($d$ or $D$)** in **mm** — the imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll.
- **Module ($m$)** in **mm** — a standard geometric parameter relating tooth size to pitch diameter.
- **Pressure Angle ($\phi$)** in **degrees** — the angle between the line of action and the common tangent at the pitch point. Standard value: **$20°$** (full-depth system).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Standard Gear Terminology (KTU 2024 Module 3)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Pitch Circle | — | The imaginary circle on which two gears roll without slipping. |
| Pitch Circle Diameter | $d$ or $D$ | Diameter of the pitch circle. |
| Circular Pitch | $p_c$ | Arc length measured on the pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth. |
| Diametral Pitch | $P_d$ | Ratio of number of teeth to pitch circle diameter (used in FPS system). |
| Module | $m$ | Ratio of pitch circle diameter to the number of teeth (SI standard). |
| Addendum | $a$ | Radial distance from pitch circle to the tip (top) of the tooth. |
| Dedendum | $b$ | Radial distance from pitch circle to the root (bottom) of the tooth. |
| Whole Depth | $h$ | Sum of addendum and dedendum. |
| Working Depth | $h_w$ | Distance a tooth projects into the meshing gear (equals addendum of mating gear). |
| Clearance | $c$ | Difference between dedendum and addendum. |
| Tooth Space | — | The space between two adjacent teeth measured on the pitch circle. |
| Tooth Thickness | $t$ | Thickness of a single tooth measured on the pitch circle. |
| Pressure Angle | $\phi$ | Angle between the line of action and the common tangent at the pitch point. |
| Face Width | $F$ | Axial length of the gear tooth. |

### 2.2 Fundamental Geometric Relationships

For **two meshing gears** (Gear 1 driving Gear 2) to mesh correctly, they **must have the same module** $m$.

- Module:
$$m = \frac{d}{T} = \frac{D}{T}$$

where $T$ is the number of teeth.

- Circular Pitch:
$$p_c = \pi \cdot m = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{T}$$

- Diametral Pitch (FPS units):
$$P_d = \frac{T}{D} = \frac{1}{m}$$

- Addendum (standard, full-depth teeth):
$$a = 1 \cdot m = m$$

- Dedendum (standard):
$$b = 1.157 \cdot m \quad \text{(commonly taken as } 1.25m \text{ for clearance)}$$

- Whole Depth:
$$h = a + b = 2.157 \cdot m \quad (\approx 2.25m)$$

- Clearance:
$$c = 0.157 \cdot m \quad (\approx 0.25m)$$

- Working Depth:
$$h_w = 2a = 2m$$

- Center Distance between two meshing gears:
$$C = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}$$

- Pitch Point Velocities (must be equal for no slip):
$$v_1 = v_2 \implies \pi d_1 N_1 = \pi d_2 N_2$$

where $N_1$ and $N_2$ are rotational speeds in **rpm**.

### 2.3 Velocity Ratio (Train Value)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Ratio (VR):** It is the ratio of the speed of the driving gear to the speed of the driven gear (or driven to driver, depending on convention). KTU follows the convention:
> $$\text{Velocity Ratio} = \frac{N_{\text{driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}} = \frac{T_{\text{driver}}}{T_{\text{driven}}}$$

A *train value* greater than 1 indicates **speed reduction**; a *train value* less than 1 indicates **speed amplification** (multiplication).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Gear Trains

| Concept | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Module (same for meshing pair) | $m_1 = m_2$ | Compatibility of meshing gears |
| Velocity Ratio (simple) | $\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{T_1}{T_2}$ | Two-shaft, single-mesh drive |
| Compound Train Value | $TV = \frac{\text{Product of driver teeth}}{\text{Product of driven teeth}}$ | Multi-stage reduction |
| Reverted Train Value | $\frac{N_1}{N_4} = \frac{T_1 \cdot T_3}{T_2 \cdot T_4}$ with $C_1 = C_2$ | Center distance equal for input/output |
| Epicyclic Train (Tabular) | See Section 3 derivation | Planetary gear systems |
| Epicyclic Formula | $\frac{N_A - N_x}{N_B - N_x} = (\text{gear ratio, A to B}) \cdot \frac{\text{marker fixed}}{\text{all components}} \mid_{\text{arm fixed}}$ | Speed relationships in planetary sets |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Trap:** When using the tabular method for epicyclic trains, **clockwise rotation is taken as +ve** and **counter-clockwise as −ve** (or vice-versa, but be consistent). Mixing signs is the #1 cause of full-mark loss in KTU epicyclic problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive transmissions** — gear boxes use compound gear trains for multi-speed ratios.
- **Differential gears** in cars — epicyclic gear trains enable torque splitting between wheels.
- **Wind turbines** — use planetary gearboxes to step up low shaft speeds to generator speeds.
- **Robotics & CNC machines** — precision spur/helical gears for backlash-free, accurate motion.
- **Clocks & watches** — use compound and epicyclic trains for accurate timekeeping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Velocity Ratio

**Given:** Two gears, Gear 1 (driver, $T_1$ teeth, pitch diameter $d_1$) and Gear 2 (driven, $T_2$ teeth, pitch diameter $d_2$) in external mesh.

**Step 1 — Condition for no slipping at the pitch point:**

Since the two pitch circles roll without slipping, the linear velocity at the pitch point must be equal for both gears.

$$v_1 = v_2$$

**Step 2 — Express the linear velocity in terms of angular speed and pitch diameter:**

$$v = \pi \cdot d \cdot N$$

where $N$ is the rotational speed in revolutions per unit time. Substituting:

$$\pi \cdot d_1 \cdot N_1 = \pi \cdot d_2 \cdot N_2$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $\pi$ from both sides:**

$$d_1 \cdot N_1 = d_2 \cdot N_2$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the velocity ratio:**

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{d_2}{d_1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the relationship $d = m \cdot T$ (since $m = d/T$):**

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{m \cdot T_2}{m \cdot T_1} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 6 — Final expression for velocity ratio:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}\;}$$

This is the **fundamental velocity ratio equation** for any pair of meshing gears. Note that for external meshing, the gears rotate in opposite directions, but the speed ratio magnitude remains $T_2/T_1$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Compound Gear Train Train Value

**Given:** A compound gear train with gears A, B, C, D, E, F mounted on three shafts: Shaft 1 carries A; Shaft 2 carries B and C (compound — rigidly coupled); Shaft 3 carries D and E; Shaft 4 carries F.

**Step 1 — Write mesh equations for each pair of meshing gears:**

Mesh 1 (A drives B):
$$\frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{T_B}{T_A}$$

Mesh 2 (C drives D):
$$\frac{N_C}{N_D} = \frac{T_D}{T_C}$$

Mesh 3 (E drives F):
$$\frac{N_E}{N_F} = \frac{T_F}{T_E}$$

**Step 2 — Apply rigid coupling (compound gear) constraints:**

Since B and C are on the same shaft:
$$N_B = N_C$$

Since D and E are on the same shaft:
$$N_D = N_E$$

**Step 3 — Multiply all three mesh ratios:**

$$\frac{N_A}{N_B} \cdot \frac{N_C}{N_D} \cdot \frac{N_E}{N_F} = \frac{T_B}{T_A} \cdot \frac{T_D}{T_C} \cdot \frac{T_F}{T_E}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute rigid coupling equalities:**

$$\frac{N_A}{N_F} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{N_C}{N_B}}_{=1} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{N_E}{N_D}}_{=1} = \frac{T_B \cdot T_D \cdot T_F}{T_A \cdot T_C \cdot T_E}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to the final form:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{N_A}{N_F} = \frac{\text{Product of driven gear teeth}}{\text{Product of driver gear teeth}} = \frac{T_B \cdot T_D \cdot T_F}{T_A \cdot T_C \cdot T_E}\;}$$

> **Key Insight:** Notice how intermediate (idler or compound-shaft) gear teeth appear in the **denominator of one ratio and the numerator of the next**, cancelling out! This is a powerful pattern used in all gear train problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Epicyclic Gear Train Formula (Tabular Method)

**Given:** A simple epicyclic (planetary) gear train consisting of:
- Sun gear **S** (center)
- Planet gear **P** (rotates about its own axis and around the sun)
- Annulus (ring) gear **A** (internal teeth)
- Arm (carrier) **X** — the planet carrier, rotating about the sun's axis.

**Step 1 — Establish the reference frame:**

We fix the arm X. Then, in this frame, the train behaves as a **simple gear train**, since the planets only rotate about their own axes (no revolution around the sun).

**Step 2 — Tabulate all component speeds with arm X fixed:**

Let $N_S^X$, $N_P^X$, $N_A^X$ be the speeds of the sun, planet, and annulus respectively **in the arm's reference frame** (i.e., the relative speeds). The arm speed itself is $N_X$ in the lab frame.

| Component | Speed in Lab Frame | Speed relative to Arm |
|---|---|---|
| Sun S | $N_S$ | $N_S^X = N_S - N_X$ |
| Planet P | $N_P$ | $N_P^X = N_P - N_X$ |
| Annulus A | $N_A$ | $N_A^X = N_A - N_X$ |
| Arm X | $N_X$ | $0$ |

**Step 3 — Apply the simple gear train formula (arm fixed):**

The sun, planet, and annulus are kinematically linked. In the arm-fixed frame, the sun drives the planet, which is in mesh with the annulus (for a typical configuration):

$$\frac{N_S - N_X}{N_A - N_X} = -\frac{T_A}{T_S}$$

The negative sign arises because the sun and annulus rotate in **opposite directions** in the arm-fixed frame (one external mesh, one internal mesh).

**Step 4 — Generalize with all gear ratios:**

If the gear train has more elements (multiple planets, idlers), we use the general formula:

$$\frac{N_A - N_X}{N_B - N_X} = \left( \frac{\text{Product of driven gear teeth}}{\text{Product of driver gear teeth}} \right)_{\text{in arm-fixed frame}}$$

**Step 5 — Special cases commonly tested in KTU:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Annulus fixed ($N_A = 0$):** Then $\frac{N_S - N_X}{0 - N_X} = -\frac{T_A}{T_S}$, giving $N_S = N_X \left(1 + \frac{T_A}{T_S}\right)$. Speed ratio $\frac{N_S}{N_X} = 1 + \frac{T_A}{T_S}$ (this is the *planetary reduction* used in automatic gearboxes).
> - **Sun fixed ($N_S = 0$):** Then $\frac{0 - N_X}{N_A - N_X} = -\frac{T_A}{T_S}$, giving $N_A = N_X \left(1 + \frac{T_S}{T_A}\right)$.
> - **Arm fixed ($N_X = 0$):** Reduces to ordinary simple gear train formula.

**Step 6 — Worked example (algebraic demonstration):**

Let $T_S = 20$, $T_A = 60$, with annulus fixed and arm as input.

From the formula:
$$\frac{N_S - N_X}{0 - N_X} = -\frac{60}{20} = -3$$

$$N_S - N_X = 3 N_X$$

$$\boxed{\;N_S = 4 N_X\;}$$

So the sun rotates **4 times** for every **1 rotation** of the arm — a speed amplification of 4×.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Type of Gear Train | Characteristic Feature | Standard Formula | Mark Allocation in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple | Two gears, one mesh | $N_1/N_2 = T_2/T_1$ | 3–5 marks |
| Compound | Multiple shafts, intermediate compound gears | $\frac{N_{\text{first}}}{N_{\text{last}}} = \frac{\prod T_{\text{driven}}}{\prod T_{\text{driver}}}$ | 7 marks |
| Reverted | Input and output shafts coaxial | Above + center distance equality $C_1 = C_2$ | 7–10 marks |
| Epicyclic/Planetary | Gears rotate on moving carriers | $\frac{N_A - N_X}{N_B - N_X} = \pm \frac{T_B}{T_A}$ (arm fixed) | 14 marks (full question) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Gears

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Gears] --> nodeB[Spur Gears]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Helical Gears]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Bevel Gears]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Worm and Worm Wheel]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Rack and Pinion]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Internal Gears]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Parallel Shafts]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Simple and Common]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Parallel or Crossed Shafts]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Smoother and Quieter]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Intersecting Shafts]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Straight Bevel]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Spiral Bevel]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Non-Intersecting Perpendicular Shafts]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[High Reduction Ratios]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Translates Rotary to Linear Motion]

    nodeG --> nodeG1[Internal Teeth Used in Planetary Trains]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Simple vs Compound vs Epicyclic Gear Train Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SimpleTrain [Simple Gear Train]
        s1[Driver Gear A] -->|External Mesh| s2[Driven Gear B]
    end

    subgraph CompoundTrain [Compound Gear Train]
        c1[Gear A] -->|Mesh| c2[Gear B on Shaft 2]
        c2 --- c3[Gear C on Shaft 2 Compound]
        c3 -->|Mesh| c4[Gear D on Shaft 3]
        c4 --- c5[Gear E on Shaft 3 Compound]
        c5 -->|Mesh| c6[Gear F on Shaft 4]
    end

    subgraph EpicyclicTrain [Epicyclic Planetary Train]
        e1[Sun Gear S] -->|Mesh| e2[Planet Gear P]
        e2 -->|Internal Mesh| e3[Annulus Gear A]
        e4[Arm Carrier X] -.Rotates.-> e2
        e1 --- e4
        e3 --- e4
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block Flow for Gear Train Speed Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Input Driver Speed N1]) --> mesh{Mesh Type}
    mesh -->|Simple| simple[Use N1 over N2 equals T2 over T1]
    mesh -->|Compound| compound[Multiply All Mesh Ratios]
    mesh -->|Reverted| reverted[Apply Center Distance Equality]
    mesh -->|Epicyclic| epicyclic[Use Tabular Method with Arm Fixing]

    simple --> out1([Output Driven Speed])
    compound --> out2([Output Driven Speed])
    reverted --> out3([Output Driven Speed])
    epicyclic --> out4([Output Speed with Arm and Sun or Annulus Relation])
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Velocity Ratio Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Given Gear Train Problem] --> q1{Number of Shafts}
    q1 -->|2 Shafts| q2[Simple Gear Train]
    q1 -->|3 or More Shafts| q3{Compound Gears Present}
    q3 -->|Yes| q4[Compound Gear Train]
    q3 -->|No| q5[Check for Planetary or Epicyclic]
    q5 -->|Yes| q6[Epicyclic Gear Train Tabular Method]
    q5 -->|No| q7[Reverted Gear Train with Coaxial Shafts]
    q2 --> end1[Apply N1 over N2 equals T2 over T1]
    q4 --> end2[Multiply Driver and Driven Teeth Products]
    q6 --> end3[Fix Arm and Apply Relative Speed Equation]
    q7 --> end4[Compound Formula plus Center Distance Constraint]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** For epicyclic and bevel gear physical drawings (with arrows showing rotation direction), the Mermaid block diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** rather than the geometric drawing, in keeping with Mermaid compilation safety and KTU module-level conceptual understanding.
